- Provide five named tolerance classes: `Exact`, `Transfer`, `Pointwise`, `Neighborhood`, and `LegacyGpu`. Individual tests select a class; ad hoc numeric tolerances in tests are forbidden.
- Use CIEDE2000 on D50 Lab for perceptual comparison, plus maximum absolute error, maximum relative error, RMSE, PSNR, changed-pixel count, and outlier count.

## Required implementation

- Define versioned `DiffPolicy`, `DiffMetrics`, `DiffOutlier`, and `DiffReceipt` types with stable JSON output.
- Specify epsilon behavior near zero, alpha weighting, premultiplied conversion, negative/HDR samples, NaN/Inf mismatch, and clipped output.
- Report the worst 32 pixels with coordinates, source/reference values, per-channel error, Delta E, and surrounding tile identity without dumping complete images.
- Generate optional heatmap and blink-comparison artifacts for merge-only failures.
- Add property tests for identity, symmetry where applicable, monotonic error growth, one-pixel outliers, alpha-only differences, negative values, non-finite values, and tile-boundary errors.
- Add parser validation for tolerance classes in `architecture/darktable-operations.toml`; operation tests cannot override the canonical class without updating the manifest.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Exact inputs produce a zero-valued receipt with stable serialization.
- [ ] Each injected defect is detected by the intended metric and coordinate.
- [ ] CPU/WGPU and reference comparisons consume the same API and report schema.
- [ ] Comparison memory is bounded to two input images plus one optional artifact buffer.
- [ ] No test can silently pass because an image contains NaN or mismatched dimensions.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0006]; integrates with [0007] but can be tested independently.

## One-PR boundary

Only comparison types, algorithms, tolerance classes, reports, artifacts, and tests.
issues.title: Implement canonical differential image metrics and tolerance classes

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Populate the bounded corpus that all compatibility, image-I/O, color, pixelpipe, mask, operation, import, and migration issues will use. Every fixture must be registered through [0006] and mapped to explicit consumers.

## Fixed decisions

The initial corpus must include:

- Bayer: 12/14/16-bit, two-row and four-row CFA variants, compressed and uncompressed DNG, one Canon CR2/CR3, Nikon NEF, Sony ARW, and Olympus/Panasonic RAW representative;
- X-Trans: at least one uncompressed and one compressed RAF with the 6×6 CFA recorded;
- raster: JPEG 8/12-bit, PNG 8/16-bit with alpha, TIFF strips/tiles integer/float, JPEG XL, WebP, AVIF, HEIF, OpenEXR, RGBE, PFM, and JPEG 2000;
- color: matrix ICC, LUT ICC, display/output profiles, malformed profiles, HDR transfer examples;
- compatibility: supported darktable library/data schema pairs, XMP sidecars with simple and multi-instance histories, blend/masks/styles/presets, and unsupported-version samples;
- geometry/masks: portrait, landscape, extreme aspect ratio, one-pixel edge cases, crops, paths, brushes, gradients, raster masks, and source-heal coordinates.

## Required implementation

- Register every fixture with stable ID, checksum, size, privacy status, expected dimensions/metadata, and consuming issue ranges.
- Generate synthetic edge fixtures for Unicode paths, missing files, truncated containers, overlarge dimensions, decompression bombs, invalid offsets, duplicate tags, NaN/Inf parameters, and conflicting metadata.
- Add `corpus.toml` matrices for camera family, CFA, bit depth, compression, orientation, profile, metadata, history, masks, and expected decoder support.
- Add a completeness test that rejects any required matrix cell lacking a fixture or explicit unsupported status.
- Store expected outputs as compact hashes/metadata when exact bytes are sufficient; use image artifacts only where differential comparison requires them.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Corpus validation is deterministic and offline.
- [ ] Every required decoder/compatibility class has at least one positive and one malformed fixture.
- [ ] No individual PR test downloads assets.
- [ ] Large-asset absence produces a structured skip locally and a hard failure in the merge corpus job.
- [ ] Corpus additions do not contain undisclosed private metadata.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0006] and uses schemas from [0004]/[0005].

## One-PR boundary

Only corpus registration, synthetic generators, completeness matrix, and validation. No decoder or migration implementation.
issues.title: Build the representative RAW, raster, XMP, catalog, and color corpus

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Create the benchmark harness and stable receipt schema used throughout the rewrite. This issue establishes measurement, not optimization or hard release thresholds.

## Fixed decisions

- Use Criterion only for local microbench exploration; use a repository-owned harness for merge evidence because Criterion output is not the compatibility contract.
- Benchmark wall time, CPU time, peak resident memory, allocated bytes/count, cache hits/misses, decoded/processed megapixels per second, preview latency p50/p95/p99, GPU upload/download bytes, and GPU dispatch duration where available.
- Separate deterministic workload identity from host results. Baselines are keyed by scenario, fixture hashes, build profile, target triple, CPU model/features, GPU adapter/driver/backend, thread count, and source SHA.
- PR checks validate benchmark code and receipt parsing only. Full runs are merge-only and never compare unlike hosts.

## Required implementation

- Add `rusttable-testkit::bench` with typed scenario definitions, warmup/repetition policy, process isolation, cancellation, and JSON receipt generation.
- Create initial scenarios for catalog open/checkpoint, import registration, raster decode, RAW decode placeholder, thumbnail generation, minimal CPU pipeline, minimal WGPU pipeline placeholder, preview update, full export, and 10k-item library projection.
- Ensure each scenario declares fixture IDs, dimensions, operation set, thread count, memory cap, timeout, expected output hash, and counters.
- Add baseline comparison that detects regressions only against the same host fingerprint and reports percent/absolute deltas without auto-updating reviewed baselines.
- Add fake-clock/counter fixtures and parser tests for missing metrics, duplicated samples, interrupted runs, outliers, host mismatch, and schema migration.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Repeated no-op fixture runs produce stable workload identity and output hashes.
- [ ] Host mismatch prevents a pass/fail comparison rather than producing a false regression.
- [ ] Interrupted or timed-out runs emit no success receipt.
- [ ] The harness records peak memory and latency percentiles without network access.
- [ ] Merge artifacts are compact, machine-readable, and diffable.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0006], [0008], and [0009].

## One-PR boundary

Only benchmark framework, scenario schema, initial placeholders, receipts, and tests. No performance budget or optimization work.
issues.title: Implement deterministic benchmark scenarios and baseline receipts

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Create one typed Rust command surface for repository-owned generation, validation, fixtures, parity, benchmarking, and release preparation. Existing shell scripts remain only as thin process/bootstrap wrappers where GitHub Actions or hooks require them.

## Fixed decisions

- Add a root workspace package named `xtask` with `clap` subcommands and no dependency from product crates.
- Business logic lives in reusable Rust modules, not shell, TypeScript, or workflow YAML.
- Every command supports `--format human|json`; JSON output uses versioned tagged records and stdout is reserved for the selected output. Diagnostics go to stderr.
- Commands resolve the repository root from Cargo metadata, never from the caller's working directory.

## Required implementation

Implement the initial command tree:

- `xtask parity scan-darktable|verify`;
- `xtask fixtures verify|list|scrub-report`;
- `xtask bench run|compare|verify-receipt`;
- `xtask repo verify-dag|verify-files|verify-workflows` placeholders that call typed library APIs as their issues land;
- `xtask reference probe|render`;
- `xtask ci precommit|prepush|pr|main` orchestration placeholders preserving current commands and budgets.

Add a shared process runner with bounded stdout/stderr, timeout, cancellation, child-tree cleanup, exact environment construction, and stable command receipts. Replace duplicate repository-root, hashing, JSON emission, and temporary-directory utilities in existing RustTable scripts with xtask/library calls. Do not migrate active packaging scripts [0001]/[0002] until their behavior is stable.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `cargo xtask --help` exposes the complete initial tree with deterministic ordering.
- [ ] Commands behave identically from the root, a subdirectory, and a worktree.
- [ ] JSON mode is parseable and contains no human prefixes or ANSI escapes.
- [ ] Timeout/cancellation tests prove child cleanup.
- [ ] Product crate dependency graphs do not include `xtask` or `rusttable-testkit`.
- [ ] Existing validation time budgets are unchanged.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0003] and consumes library APIs from [0004]–[0010].

## One-PR boundary

Only xtask infrastructure, initial wrappers, shared process utilities, and migration of duplicated automation helpers. Do not add new product capabilities or broad CI policy.
issues.title: Consolidate repository automation behind the Rust xtask binary

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Prevent the repository from rebuilding or linking the inherited darktable C/C++ implementation while defining the only permitted native/unsafe boundaries and using the pinned Rust 1.98 beta plus isolated nightly verification tools to prove those boundaries under edition 2024.

## Fixed decisions

- RustTable-owned behavior is implemented in Rust. No source under the retained darktable tree may be compiled, linked, generated into bindings, loaded dynamically, or invoked by product code.
- Third-party native dependencies are permitted only in dedicated adapter crates named `rusttable-*-native`; initial approved purposes are SQLite compatibility reading, LittleCMS ICC transforms, libheif/codec integration where required, gPhoto2 camera control, and Lua 5.4 hosting. Each adapter exposes a safe Rust API and may not contain copied darktable code.
- Workspace `unsafe_code = "deny"`; only `rusttable-simd` and approved native adapter crates may locally allow narrowly scoped unsafe items. Every unsafe block, unsafe function, unsafe trait implementation, and unsafe extern call requires a local `// SAFETY:` invariant and focused tests.
- Edition-2024 unsafe requirements are enforced: `extern` blocks are explicitly unsafe, unsafe attributes use the required syntax, and unsafe operations inside unsafe functions still require explicit unsafe blocks. No crate may suppress these lints wholesale.
- Product/native-boundary source compiles with the authoritative `beta-2026-07-18` toolchain from [0018] and may use APIs available without feature gates on that compiler. Beta is not permission to use `#![feature]`, `-Z`, or any unstable ABI.
- Prefer safe standard-library APIs available on the primary compiler over custom FFI/platform code where they replace it. Naked functions, inline assembly, raw-dylib linkage, and custom allocators are prohibited unless a dedicated issue proves necessity and platform coverage.
- Pending raw-layout/non-null/alignment APIs (`size_of_val_raw`, `align_of_val_raw`, `Layout::for_value_raw`, Box/Vec NonNull parts, `ptr::try_cast_aligned`, and `Alignment`) remain tracked by [0018]. They may simplify internals after becoming available without feature gates on the authoritative compiler; nightly forms are not used in product adapters.
- Rust 1.99’s documented `NonNull` layout guarantees may inform future safety reviews only after those guarantees are available on the authoritative product compiler. Current invariants cite the pinned 1.98 beta documentation and explicit owned allocations.
- Pinned nightly from [0018A] is used for verification tooling only:
  - Miri for pure-Rust unsafe helpers and ownership/aliasing fixtures;
  - Address/Leak/Thread sanitizers on supported targets for native adapters and SIMD boundaries;
  - `-Zub-checks` as an additional best-effort unsafe test configuration;
  - rustdoc JSON for machine-checking public unsafe/raw surfaces with the exact format/compiler fingerprint in the receipt.
- Nightly verification artifacts cannot enter product/package dependency graphs. Sanitizers, Miri, rustdoc JSON, and UB checks do not define product behavior and are never product/package flags.
- First-party image operations, pixelpipe, masks, UI, WGPU shaders, catalog domain, and native redb persistence may not depend on adapter crates.

## Implementation

- Add `architecture/native-boundaries.toml` listing approved adapter crate, native library, purpose, owning API module, allowed dependents, build features, unsafe surface, callback/thread model, and removal/review condition.
- Add `xtask repo verify-native-boundaries` using Cargo metadata from the primary beta compiler, pinned-nightly rustdoc JSON where registered, and source scanning to reject workspace C/C++ files, `cc`/`cmake` build dependencies, unapproved `-sys` crates, unregistered `extern` blocks, link attributes, bindgen, linker directives, assembly/naked functions, unsafe attributes, and paths into the reference tree outside approved adapters.
- Verify adapter public APIs contain no raw pointers, `NonNull`, foreign integer handles, unchecked unions, or borrowed data whose lifetime originates in native code. Resource handles are opaque safe newtypes with deterministic close/drop behavior.
- Require native callback trampolines to catch panics before crossing FFI, bound native diagnostics, define thread affinity/reentrancy, and avoid unwinding or longjmp across Rust except inside a fully contained audited codec adapter.
- Register exact nightly verification commands in `quality/nightly-labs.toml` through [0018A], including targets, components, timeout/memory limits, expected exclusions, and artifact-retention rules.
- Add Miri tests for allocation ownership, slice/view creation, aliasing, lifetimes, partial initialization, drop, panic, and callback state in pure-Rust unsafe helpers.
- Add ASan/LSan/TSan jobs where supported for adapter allocation/free, callback races, buffer bounds, use-after-free, double close, cancellation, and error paths. Mixed C/Rust adapters instrument native code where feasible and record uninstrumented gaps.
- Add `-Zub-checks` executions for approved unsafe test packages, treating findings as hard failures while not treating a clean run as proof of soundness.
- Add primary-beta and rolling-beta compile/check coverage for every adapter so beta branch changes in unsafe parsing, lints, ABI/codegen, or FFI diagnostics are detected before a baseline refresh.
- Add fixtures for hidden build scripts, generated C, transitive `-sys` crates, feature-gated native code, copied reference paths, unsafe without rationale, public raw handles, panic across FFI, callback after drop, thread-affinity violation, compiler-channel leakage, and dependency edges from forbidden consumers.
- Run source/graph checks in pre-commit and PR validation; run primary-beta adapter tests and pinned Miri/sanitizers/UB checks in merge or adapter-specific jobs within declared budgets.

## Failure and edge behavior

- A primary-beta fingerprint mismatch is a hard failure under [0018]; adapter checks never fall back to stable or moving beta silently.
- A nightly tool/toolchain failure is classified separately from a detected safety defect and records the exact compiler/tool fingerprint.
- No adapter is accepted merely because Miri or sanitizers pass; manual invariants, safe public boundaries, and focused tests remain mandatory.
- Unsupported sanitizer/target combinations are explicit capability results, not silent skips. Every approved adapter has at least one applicable dynamic checker or a documented alternative strategy.
- A native panic, longjmp, callback, or thread violation cannot unwind into product code or outlive its owning service generation.
- A future beta/stable raw-pointer API is adopted only through a focused issue proving that it reduces the unsafe surface; no compatibility shim uses `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`.

## Test matrix

- Static fixtures cover every forbidden source/dependency/public-API pattern, edition-2024 unsafe rule, primary-beta fingerprint rule, and product/nightly graph boundary.
- Dynamic fixtures cover ownership transfer, zero/oversized allocations, odd alignment, partial initialization, callback races, cancellation during native work, native error stacks, panic containment, repeated close/drop, and process shutdown.
- Cross-platform jobs exercise Linux, macOS, and Windows adapter builds under the primary beta and every supported sanitizer combination; unavailable tools are receipt-visible.
- Rolling-beta and current-nightly stable-source checks detect upcoming parser/lint/ABI changes without enabling product feature gates.
- Package-graph tests prove no nightly component, sanitizer runtime, lab crate, unstable flag, or non-primary compiler artifact is present in accepted packages.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask repo verify-native-boundaries` passes.
- All approved adapter unit/integration tests pass under `beta-2026-07-18` and record the exact compiler fingerprint.
- Registered `cargo +nightly-2026-07-15 miri`, sanitizer, rustdoc-JSON, and `-Zub-checks` commands pass through [0018A] for supported packages/targets.
- The inherited darktable implementation cannot enter any RustTable binary.
- Edition-2024 unsafe rules are enforced without crate-wide suppressions.
- Approved adapters are explicit, minimal, panic-contained, dynamically checked where possible, and safe at their public boundary.

## Dependencies

Depends on #159, #167, #174, and #427.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the native-boundary policy manifest, beta/edition safety rules, checker, pinned-nightly verification registration, fixtures, and validation wiring. Do not add adapter implementations, broadly upgrade dependencies, adopt pending nightly raw-pointer APIs, or implement operation-specific SIMD.
issues.title: Enforce the Rust-owned implementation boundary and isolate native adapters

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Turn the ownership map from [0003] into a fail-closed architecture gate over the actual locked Cargo graph.

## Fixed decisions

- `architecture/workspace-dag.toml` is authoritative; the checker may not infer or silently add acceptable edges.
- Validate normal, build, dev, target-specific, optional, and feature-activated dependencies separately.
- Product integration tests live with the highest-level owning crate. Cross-domain test helpers live in `rusttable-testkit`; product crates cannot gain dev-dependencies merely to bypass ownership.
- A new workspace package or local edge must update the map in the same PR or validation fails.

## Required implementation

- Implement `xtask repo verify-dag` using `cargo metadata --locked --all-features --format-version 1` plus targeted no-default-feature metadata runs where feature topology differs.
- Compare the discovered package set and every local edge with the declared graph; reject missing/extra packages, undeclared edges, forbidden reverse edges, cycles, product-to-tooling edges, and integration dependencies owned by the wrong package.
- Verify that the composition-root exception applies only to `rusttable-app` and does not permit product crates to depend back on the app.
- Emit a stable JSON report containing package inventory, allowed/discovered edges, violations, feature context, and a topological order.
- Add fixtures for renamed packages, path aliases, target-specific dependencies, optional features, build scripts, dev cycles, tooling leaks, and a newly added crate.
- Replace the current partial crate-boundary test only after equivalent and broader coverage passes.
- Run in pre-commit and PR validation; execution must remain below five seconds on a warm workspace.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] The complete current workspace passes under default, all-feature, and selected minimal-feature graphs.
- [ ] Every invalid fixture fails with source package, destination package, dependency kind, feature context, and allowed alternatives.
- [ ] Adding an undeclared package or path dependency is detected immediately.
- [ ] The checker has no network dependency and consumes the committed lockfile.
- [ ] No duplicate architecture checker remains.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0003], [0011], and [0012].

## One-PR boundary

Only DAG verification, fixtures, replacement of the partial test, and validation wiring. No crate moves or product dependencies are added.
issues.title: Enforce the complete workspace dependency DAG against Cargo metadata

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Leave exactly the RustTable pull-request and merge workflows executable, then prevent future workflow drift into inherited builds, privileged events, floating actions, or unbounded jobs.

## Fixed decisions

- Delete inherited `.github/workflows/ci.yml` and `.github/workflows/nightly.yml`; do not archive them under another executable extension.
- The executable inventory after this PR is exactly `.github/workflows/rust-pr.yml` and `.github/workflows/rust-main.yml`.
- Allowed events are `pull_request` for the PR workflow and `push` to `main` for the merge workflow. No schedules, `workflow_run`, `pull_request_target`, repository dispatch, or manual dispatch.
- Repository permissions are `contents: read`; jobs may narrow further but may not request write permissions.
- Every third-party action is pinned by full commit SHA with a version comment. Shell steps use repository-owned scripts/xtask commands rather than multiline implementation logic.

## Required implementation

- Implement `xtask repo verify-workflows` to parse both `.yml` and `.yaml` files, validate the exact inventory, events, branches, permissions, action pins, job timeouts, runner labels, checkout persistence, and prohibited network/write commands.
- Require a job timeout of at most 5 minutes for PR jobs and 45 minutes for merge jobs; every shell command must inherit the repository process timeout wrapper.
- Reject Docker actions, mutable runner containers, secret references, artifact retention above 14 days, broad cache keys, and workflow expressions that can execute fork-controlled strings.
- Add fixtures for renamed workflows, extra events, floating tags, action SHA without comment, write permission, secret access, unbounded jobs, scheduled runs, and hidden `.yaml` files.
- Run the checker from pre-commit and both workflows without creating a third workflow/check.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] GitHub sees exactly two executable workflows.
- [ ] Both workflows retain their existing required check names and current validation behavior.
- [ ] Every prohibited fixture fails with file, YAML path, and required replacement.
- [ ] The workflow parser is deterministic and does not require GitHub API access.
- [ ] No inherited C/CMake job remains runnable.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0011] and must preserve active [0001]/[0002] packaging steps.

## One-PR boundary

Only workflow deletion, policy parser, fixtures, and existing validation wiring.
issues.title: Remove inherited darktable workflows and enforce the RustTable workflow inventory

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Make the repository’s one-issue/one-PR rule executable in the existing required PR validation check.

## Fixed decisions

- Every PR body contains exactly one line matching `Closes #<number>` and the referenced item must be an open issue in `cgasgarth/RustTable` with a `[NNNN]` title, a milestone, and parent #158 in its body.
- The PR title must begin with the exact issue sequence token. The head branch must be `issue-<number>-<slug>`; forks use the same branch basename.
- A PR may not close or resolve any second issue through body keywords, commit messages, or linked PR metadata.
- The check runs under `pull_request` with read-only permissions and uses the event payload plus GitHub API reads; it never uses `pull_request_target`.

## Required implementation

- Add `.github/pull_request_template.md` containing one closing line and `Why`, `Implementation`, `Validation`, and `Out of scope` sections.
- Implement `xtask github verify-pr-contract --event <path>` with a pure parser mode for fixtures and a live API mode.
- Validate issue state, repository, title token, milestone, parent reference, branch name, PR title, exact closing-keyword count, and required evidence sections.
- Inspect the PR commit messages and body for additional `close|closes|closed|fix|fixes|fixed|resolve|resolves|resolved` issue references.
- Treat API unavailability as an explicit infrastructure failure, not a pass.
- Add fixtures for missing issue, multiple issues, closed issue, PR item, wrong repository, unnumbered title, missing milestone, mismatched sequence/title/branch, malformed body, duplicate closing line, and fork PR.
- Wire it into the existing `rust-pr / validate` job without changing the required check name.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A conforming fixture and a real test PR pass.
- [ ] Every invalid fixture fails with one actionable reason and expected form.
- [ ] The command performs no repository writes and exposes no secrets.
- [ ] A PR cannot merge while referencing an issue outside the numbered queue.
- [ ] Parser tests run locally without GitHub access.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0011] and [0014].

## One-PR boundary

Only PR template, contract verifier, fixtures, API read path, and existing PR-check wiring.
issues.title: Enforce one open child issue per pull request

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Replace duplicated validation orchestration with one machine-readable contract describing every check, where it runs, its maximum cost, and its owning command.

## Fixed decisions

- Store the contract at `quality/validation-surfaces.toml`.
- Supported surfaces are `precommit`, `prepush`, `pull_request`, and `main`. There is no scheduled validation surface.
- Hard budgets remain: 30 seconds pre-commit, 60 seconds pre-push, 150 seconds PR. Main jobs are bounded individually and may run exhaustive checks.
- Each check has exactly one implementation command and may appear on multiple surfaces only through the contract. Scripts cannot duplicate command bodies.

## Required implementation

- Define fields for stable check ID, human label, command/arguments, owner crate/module, prerequisites, surfaces, expected parallel group, timeout, network policy, artifact outputs, platform matrix, and failure severity.
- Implement `xtask ci <surface>` to load the contract, select checks, validate prerequisites, execute independent groups in parallel, cancel siblings on failure where safe, enforce process-tree timeouts, and emit a stable receipt with per-check duration/status.
- Convert current precommit/prepush/PR/main scripts into thin wrappers around xtask.
- Reject unknown checks, duplicate IDs/labels, recursive surface invocation, missing commands, incompatible parallel groups, timeout sums above budget, PR/main drift, network-enabled fast checks, and merge-only checks placed on PR.
- Add deterministic fake-command fixtures for success, failure, timeout, descendant leak, cancellation, output limit, ordering, and parallel error propagation.
- Preserve current required check names and shell entry points.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Every repository-owned validation command appears exactly once in the contract.
- [ ] Fast-surface worst-case configured budgets do not exceed repository limits.
- [ ] A failed parallel check causes the surface to fail and cleans up all children.
- [ ] Local and hosted surfaces execute the same declared commands for the same surface.
- [ ] Receipts identify omitted checks and the explicit reason.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0011], [0014], and [0015].

## One-PR boundary

Only validation contract, orchestrator, wrappers, fixtures, and migration of existing checks. Do not add coverage, offline build, or new product tests.
issues.title: Declare and enforce validation-surface ownership and cost tiers

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Make RustTable-owned source, configuration, scripts, manifests, fixtures, and generated files byte-stable across Linux, macOS, Windows, and Git worktrees without rewriting the retained reference tree.

## Fixed decisions

- Govern only root RustTable files, `crates/rusttable-*`, `xtask`, `architecture`, `quality`, `scripts` owned by RustTable, `shaders`, and `fixtures`. The inherited darktable tree is excluded.
- Text files are UTF-8 without BOM, LF terminated, and end with exactly one newline. Binary fixtures use `-text`.
- Only declared executable entry points may have Git mode `100755`; Rust, TOML, JSON, YAML, Markdown, WGSL, and fixture manifests are never executable.
- Repository-relative logical paths use `/`; runtime native paths remain `Path`/`PathBuf` and are never normalized by string replacement.

## Required implementation

- Replace the inherited-only `.gitattributes` content with narrow RustTable rules for text/binary classes, EOL, generated markers, and export-subst where still required.
- Add `quality/repository-files.toml` listing governed roots, allowed extensions, binary classes, executable paths, maximum path length, reserved Windows names, and case-collision policy.
- Implement `xtask repo verify-files` to inspect tracked files and index modes, detect CRLF/BOM, invalid UTF-8, missing/extra final newline, NUL in text, executable drift, case-insensitive collisions, trailing space/dot components, Windows-reserved names, and path traversal in manifests.
- Add tests using synthetic Git index fixtures for mode changes and filesystem fixtures for case sensitivity, Unicode normalization, CRLF, BOM, binary false positives, and symlinks.
- Wire the check into pre-commit and PR validation; do not auto-rewrite files in CI.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A clean checkout passes on Linux, macOS, and Windows.
- [ ] Every invalid byte/path/mode fixture fails with exact file and rule.
- [ ] Binary assets are not decoded or modified.
- [ ] The retained reference tree produces no violations.
- [ ] The checker completes within the fast-validation budget.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0011] and [0016].

## One-PR boundary

Only attributes, file policy, checker, fixtures, and validation wiring. Do not broadly renormalize excluded legacy files.
issues.title: Enforce cross-platform text, path, and executable-bit normalization

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Establish the exact Rust compiler and dependency baseline for RustTable, using the current Rust 1.98 beta as the primary compiler immediately while preserving reproducible builds, compiler-regression detection, and a bounded transition to Rust 1.98.0 stable when it is released.

## Fixed decisions

- The primary development, CI, benchmark, package-build, and release-candidate compiler is the date-pinned Rust beta toolchain `beta-2026-07-17`, on the Rust 1.98 release line, with edition 2024.
- `rust-toolchain.toml` pins `beta-2026-07-17`; it does not use the moving `beta` alias. The accepted compiler fingerprint is the complete `rustc -vV`, Cargo version, LLVM version, rustup manifest date/hash, host, installed targets, and component hashes recorded by this PR.
- Every workspace package sets `rust-version = "1.98"`. Cargo requires a bare version and ignores compiler prerelease identifiers for the version check; no manifest uses `1.98.0-beta.*` as `rust-version`.
- All local validation, CI, rustfmt, Clippy, rustdoc, cargo-nextest, llvm-cov, benchmark, release-candidate, and package-builder surfaces use the pinned beta unless an issue explicitly owns a comparison/laboratory channel.
- Rust 1.97.1 is retained only as a compiler-regression reference and emergency comparison tool under [0018A]. It is not the primary product compiler and does not produce accepted main-branch or package artifacts.
- Before accepting the beta baseline, run the minimized Rust 1.97.1 LLVM-miscompilation reproducer plus RustTable’s numerical sentinel corpus. The selected beta archive must contain or otherwise pass the relevant fix. Failure blocks the baseline and requires advancing to a newer date-pinned 1.98 beta archive; it does not silently fall back to 1.97.1.
- Product source may use APIs that are stable on the 1.98 beta branch without `#![feature]`, but each newly used 1.98 API must be recorded in the ecosystem capability manifest with its call sites, reason, fallback/removal impact, tests, and platform implications.
- Beta does not authorize unstable features. Product crates, default workspace features, build scripts, proc macros, generated production source, and package builds may not use `#![feature]`, `-Z` flags, `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`, `tokio_unstable`, WGPU experimental features, or any nightly-only API.
- Rust 1.98.0 is scheduled to stabilize on 2026-08-20. Promotion to the stable release is a separate bounded PR tracked by [0018B]. Until that PR passes and merges, the date-pinned beta remains authoritative even after the moving beta channel advances to 1.99.
- Configure Cargo’s stable `[build] warnings = "deny"` policy for local workspace packages instead of relying on injected `RUSTFLAGS=-Dwarnings`. Dependency warnings remain governed separately.
- Use stable `resolver.lockfile-path` in repository-owned read-only source/worktree/offline validation flows where a lockfile must live outside the source tree. Ordinary development and package builds use the root `Cargo.lock`.
- Use rustdoc `--remap-path-prefix` for generated API/reference artifacts so private checkout paths do not enter docs, crash artifacts, or support bundles.
- Use `cfg(target_has_atomic_primitive_alignment)` when atomic-width capability changes implementation selection; never infer atomic support from OS or architecture names.
- Verify profilers, symbolizers, crash reporters, and support-bundle tooling demangle Rust v0 symbols before they are accepted.
- `Cargo.lock` is committed. Every normal command uses `--locked`; release-candidate, package, and offline validation additionally use `--frozen`.
- Direct production, development, and build dependencies use exact versions declared once at the workspace root.
- crates.io is the default source. Git dependencies are denied unless `quality/dependency-sources.toml` pins an immutable commit and records owner, purpose, replacement condition, and checksum evidence.
- Multiple major versions are allowed only when unavoidable. Duplicate minor/patch lines require a reviewed exception with an owning removal issue.
- Framework upgrades are capability-reviewed rather than merely version-bumped. The review records new APIs/features, selected uses, rejected or experimental features, migration impact, platform support, numerical implications, benchmark evidence, and follow-up issue IDs.
- Nightly-only tools and experiments remain isolated under [0018A]. Their artifacts cannot enter product or package dependency graphs or define durable behavior.
- Advisory handling uses RustSec IDs with expiry dates and owning issues. License allow/deny rules and notice generation remain out of scope.

## Implementation

- Add `rust-toolchain.toml` pinned to `beta-2026-07-17` with the minimal profile plus the exact rustfmt, Clippy, rust-src, llvm-tools, documentation, and supported target components required by repository commands.
- Set workspace/package `rust-version = "1.98"`, edition 2024, resolver 3, and the approved Cargo warning/resolver/path-remapping configuration.
- Add `quality/compiler-baseline.toml` containing the accepted beta archive date, release string, commit hash/date, LLVM version, Cargo/rustfmt/Clippy versions, components, targets, dist-manifest hashes, stabilization target date, and compiler-sentinel receipt hashes.
- Add generated `quality/ecosystem-baseline.toml` containing Rust, Cargo, Iced, WGPU, Tokio, Rayon, redb, Wasmtime, mlua, codec, and shader-tool versions plus selected feature flags.
- Add `quality/dependency-sources.toml` with versioned sections for approved registries, git exceptions, duplicate-version exceptions, and advisory exceptions.
- Implement `xtask ecosystem verify` and `xtask dependencies verify` using locked Cargo metadata and `cargo audit` JSON. Validate the exact compiler fingerprint, component/target set, `rust-version`, edition, dependency centralization, versions, sources, feature graph, checksums, advisories, exceptions, and absence of unstable product behavior.
- Implement `xtask ecosystem compiler-sentinels` to compile and execute the 1.97.1 LLVM regression reproducer, floating-point/codegen sentinels from [0130], FFI ABI sentinels, and platform smoke programs under the pinned beta before any product test result is accepted.
- Implement `xtask ecosystem capabilities` to emit a deterministic report of stable, beta, nightly, and proposed stabilization offerings classified as `AdoptPrimaryBeta`, `QualifyRollingBeta`, `NightlyLab`, `DeferUntilStable`, or `Reject`.
- Record the initial decisions: adopt the already-stable Cargo warning, alternate-lockfile, rustdoc path-remapping, and atomic-alignment capabilities; use the 1.98 beta panic-location and lint changes where useful; retain stable `half` storage with `f32` CPU arithmetic; qualify portable SIMD only in [0133A]; and defer pending allocator/raw-layout/optimization features until their stable forms and benchmark evidence exist.
- Verify every `Cargo.lock` change has a deterministic dependency/capability diff listing packages and source/version/feature/compiler-channel changes.
- Add `xtask dependencies update <package>` for one-package updates. Broad updates require `--all` and a dedicated maintenance issue.
- Add fixtures for beta archive/fingerprint drift, beta compiler regression, wrong `rust-version`, edition mismatch, warning-policy bypass, private-path leakage, floating versions, registry aliases, git branches/tags, stale exceptions, duplicate versions, unstable feature gates, `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`, `-Z` flags, WGPU experiments, and malformed capability/audit output.
- Run compiler/source/version/sentinel checks in pre-commit or PR validation according to budget. Run full advisory, numerical, package, and ecosystem-capability checks in merge validation.

## Failure and edge behavior

- A beta archive date, compiler commit, LLVM version, Cargo version, component, or target mismatch is a hard failure; a semver-only match is insufficient.
- If `beta-2026-07-17` is unavailable for a supported host or lacks a required component, the baseline issue remains blocked until a newer explicit 1.98 beta archive is reviewed and pinned. The implementation never replaces the date pin with the moving alias.
- A compiler ICE, miscompilation sentinel failure, numerical delta outside [0130], or platform regression blocks the baseline and emits a minimized reproducible finding.
- Stable 1.97.1 is not used automatically to keep CI green. Any temporary fallback requires a separate expiring emergency issue and cannot produce accepted package artifacts.
- A beta/nightly feature appearing in a product dependency, build script, proc macro, generated source, package flag, or release metadata fails with exact package/path/feature evidence.
- A beta-stable API is not adopted merely because it exists; the capability record must identify owner, call sites, impact, tests, and promotion behavior.
- Capability and compiler receipts are deterministic, path-independent, and never scrape unpinned web content during ordinary builds.

## Test matrix

- Golden tests cover the exact dated beta fingerprint, semver/prerelease handling, `rust-version = "1.98"`, edition/lint configuration, component/target closure, alternate lockfile paths, rustdoc path remapping, atomic cfg fixtures, and v0 demangling.
- Compiler sentinels cover the 1.97.1 LLVM miscompilation, FMA/contraction-sensitive code, integer and floating conversions, generics/monomorphization, async state machines, FFI layout/callbacks, and representative optimized image loops.
- Mutation fixtures substitute stable 1.97.1, moving `beta`, another beta date, nightly, missing components, feature gates, `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`, `-Z`, floating dependency versions, unregistered git sources, and stale exceptions.
- Offline and read-only-source scenarios prove the beta toolchain and root/alternate lockfile behavior without network access or source-tree mutation after prerequisite acquisition.
- Package provenance tests prove every binary, library, proc macro, build-script output, and symbol/debug artifact was produced by the accepted beta fingerprint.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo +beta-2026-07-17 xtask ecosystem verify --locked` passes.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-17 xtask dependencies verify --locked` passes.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-17 xtask ecosystem compiler-sentinels --all-platform-sentinels` passes, including the Rust 1.97.1 LLVM regression reproducer.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-17 xtask ecosystem capabilities --check` emits the reviewed compiler/framework decision artifact.
- Every main/package build surface reports the same beta archive and complete compiler fingerprint and contains no unstable product feature.
- Stable 1.97.1, moving beta, a different beta archive, nightly product code, and an unreviewed dependency feature each fail their dedicated mutation fixture.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0011], [0013], and [0016].

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the pinned Rust 1.98 beta compiler/framework baseline, dependency/source/advisory policy, compiler sentinels, capability inventory, verification/update commands, fixtures, and validation wiring. Rolling-channel and nightly-lab execution belongs to [0018A]; stable 1.98 promotion belongs to [0018B]; portable-SIMD experiments belong to [0133A]. Do not perform broad framework migrations, implement product features, add licensing/notices/SBOM work, or enable nightly-only behavior in product builds.
issues.title: Pin dependency sources and enforce advisory, version, and update policy

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Measure production Rust coverage on every merge and reject meaningful regressions without adding coverage instrumentation to the fast local or pull-request paths.

## Fixed decisions

- Use `cargo llvm-cov` with the pinned workspace toolchain. Coverage runs only in the merge workflow on Linux x86_64.
- Measure line, region, and function coverage for production crates; exclude `xtask`, `rusttable-testkit`, generated code, fixture generators, and platform-specific code that cannot compile on the runner.
- Store reviewed floors in `quality/coverage.toml`. Every production package has a line floor and a maximum permitted drop from its previous accepted baseline. Workspace totals cannot compensate for a package falling below its floor.
- Initial floors are set to the measured value on the integrating commit rounded down to the nearest whole percentage, capped at 90%; no package may start below 60% unless an explicit time-bounded exception names missing branches and a follow-up issue.

## Required implementation

- Add `xtask coverage run`, `xtask coverage verify`, and `xtask coverage summarize` around `cargo llvm-cov --workspace --all-features --lcov`.
- Normalize source paths, reject stale/non-workspace files, map generated exclusions through explicit markers, and produce stable JSON plus LCOV artifacts.
- Compare package metrics with `quality/coverage.toml`, detect deleted tests/source, new unmeasured packages, floor regressions, expired exceptions, and suspicious zero-hit production files.
- Add parser fixtures for Windows paths, renamed packages, generated modules, merged line records, malformed LCOV, new packages, deleted files, partial runs, and exception expiry.
- Add the merge-only job through [0016] with a 30-minute timeout and no repository writes.
- Keep a fast pre-commit schema/fixture test; do not invoke instrumentation outside merge validation.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Every production package appears exactly once in the report and policy.
- [ ] A deliberate covered-branch deletion fixture fails the package floor.
- [ ] New production crates fail until a reviewed floor is added.
- [ ] Coverage artifacts identify source SHA, toolchain, features, and package source set.
- [ ] PR validation duration is unchanged.

## Dependencies

Depends on #169, #172, and #174.

## One-PR boundary

Only coverage tooling, floors, fixtures, and merge workflow wiring. Do not add product tests merely to increase the initial number.
issues.title: Add merge-only Rust coverage reporting with reviewed package floors

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Goal

Prove on every merge that the committed lockfile describes all Rust sources required for a clean build and test run without registry or git access.

## Fixed decisions

- Use `cargo vendor --locked --versioned-dirs` to create a temporary source closure in the merge job; do not commit `vendor/` to the repository.
- Verify with a clean `CARGO_HOME`, empty target directory, generated source-replacement config, `CARGO_NET_OFFLINE=true`, and `cargo test --workspace --all-features --all-targets --frozen` followed by the release application build.
- The offline proof covers Rust source acquisition only. Operating-system libraries for approved native adapters are supplied by the runner image and recorded separately.
- No licensing, notice, SBOM, or bit-for-bit compiler-output claim is part of this issue.

## Required implementation

- Add `xtask dependencies vendor-closure` to generate the vendor tree, canonical manifest, source-replacement config, and SHA-256 tree digest in a temporary directory.
- Add `xtask dependencies verify-offline` to validate package count, package/version/source/checksum identity against `Cargo.lock`, reject unreferenced or missing packages, then execute the clean offline commands.
- Emit a stable receipt containing source SHA, lockfile hash, Rust/Cargo versions, target triple, package count, native prerequisite list, vendor-tree digest, commands, durations, and final binary hash.
- Add fake registry/git fixtures for missing package, incorrect checksum, git submodule, build-script download attempt, undeclared network access, path dependency escape, modified lockfile, and stale vendor entry.
- Wire a merge-only Linux job with a 40-minute timeout, read-only permissions, and no cache other than the pinned Rust toolchain.
- Keep parser/orchestration tests on fast surfaces; never run the full clean build in PR validation.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A clean merge runner completes tests and release build with network disabled after the vendor step.
- [ ] Removing one vendored package or enabling a build-time download fails with the exact package/command.
- [ ] The receipt reconciles exactly with `Cargo.lock` and the source policy from [0018].
- [ ] No generated vendor content is committed.
- [ ] Fast validation budgets are unaffected.

## Dependencies

Depends on #169, #172, and #174.

## One-PR boundary

Only temporary closure generation, offline verification, receipts, fixtures, and merge workflow wiring.
issues.title: Prove the locked workspace builds from a complete offline source closure

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the runtime-supported-platform registry and startup support decision used by every RustTable service. The application identifies OS/architecture/window environment, validates one machine-readable matrix, describes permitted WGPU backend order and minimum semantic compute envelope, and returns `SupportedGpuCandidate`, `SupportedCpuOnly`, or typed `UnsupportedPlatform` before persistent services mutate state. This issue defines product support policy/runtime identity; #290 owns real WGPU probing.

## Fixed decisions

- `architecture/platform-support.toml` is the sole supported-target/runner/backend-order matrix.
- Initial matrix:
  - Linux x86_64 GNU/glibc >=2.35, Wayland/X11, Vulkan then OpenGL, CPU fallback;
  - macOS Apple Silicon >=13, Metal, CPU fallback;
  - Windows x86_64 MSVC >=10 22H2/build 19045, D3D12 then Vulkan, CPU fallback.
- Current unsupported: Linux ARM64, Intel macOS, Windows ARM64, musl, WASM/mobile, D3D11, OpenCL product backend, unlisted OS/arch.
- Stable domain types normalize target/OS/version/libc/window/backend/support/requirements/fallback/identity/findings without hostname/user/home/locale/serial/network identifiers.
- Desktop missing window system is startup failure; explicit headless mode remains supported according to backend/CPU policy.
- Backend presence here is candidate policy only, not proof of adapter/device.
- Semantic `CoreComputeRequirements` contains compute/f32 storage/binding/copy/readback and optional texture capabilities without WGPU types.
- CI/workflows/packages/DAG/cfg/runtime derive from or verify against the same matrix.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `CMakeLists.txt`, `src/CMakeLists.txt`, and `cmake/modules/*`: compile/platform/architecture/OS/library capability detection, conditional source inclusion, OpenCL/macOS/windowing/package features, and target support assumptions.
- `src/main.c` and `src/common/darktable.c/.h`: process startup, platform-specific initialization, runtime CPU/thread capability setup, GUI/headless startup order, and fatal unsupported-environment handling.
- `src/gui/gtk.c` and `src/gui/gtk.h`: GTK display initialization, GDK backend/window environment behavior, monitor/display availability, and platform-conditioned UI startup.
- `src/osx/osx.mm`, `cmake/modules/FindMacIntegration.cmake`, and macOS source registration: macOS application integration and platform support surface. Detailed lifecycle/file-open remains #511.
- Windows-conditioned code in `src/common/darktable.c/.h`, `src/common/dlopencl.c`, `src/gui/gtk.c`, and build/packaging configuration: Windows runtime/library/window/GPU availability and support boundaries.
- Linux platform/window support in `src/gui/gtk.c`, CMake GTK/X11/Wayland configuration, and packaging/build rules: display/backend and libc/distribution assumptions.
- `src/common/opencl.c/.h`, `src/common/dlopencl.c/.h`, and `src/common/opencl_drivers_blacklist.h`: platform/device/backend discovery, dynamic OpenCL availability, driver exclusions, device priorities, CPU fallback, and support diagnostics. WGPU replaces this runtime in #290–#301.
- `src/common/cpu.c/.h` or CPU-feature initialization referenced from `src/common/darktable.c`: architecture/SIMD capability evidence; SIMD execution remains separate.
- darktable CI/packaging target definitions and platform-specific package scripts: supported build matrix evidence only; RustTable package implementation remains later release issues.

**Preserve:** explicit supported target matrix, early startup rejection before catalog mutation, GUI/headless distinction, display/window requirements, normalized platform/version evidence, backend preference, GPU-optional CPU operation, driver/backend qualification, and cross-platform package/CI agreement.

**Redesign:** replace CMake/runtime scattered `#ifdef` support decisions, GTK/OpenCL availability checks, dynamic OpenCL loader/blacklist policy, and platform string branches with one typed TOML matrix, narrow safe runtime identity adapters, semantic GPU requirements, deterministic evaluation/receipt, and WGPU candidates.

**Excluded:** actual adapter/device probing, shader qualification, WGPU resources, SIMD dispatch, platform lifecycle details, signing/package creation, and adding unsupported architectures. Named issues own them.

Rust ownership: platform domain in core; narrow OS adapters in app/platform crates; #290 consumes the backend/compute contract.

## Implementation

- Define domain/enums/version parsing/matrix schema/canonical identity/findings/receipt.
- Implement compile identity and narrow Linux libc/window, macOS version, Windows build, headless/desktop adapters.
- Implement registry evaluation and `PlatformCapabilityPort`/future `GpuCapabilityProbePort`.
- Add `xtask platform verify` for matrix/DAG/workflow/package/Iced cfg agreement and prohibited backends.
- Add synthetic boundary fixtures and hosted runtime receipts.
- Integrate startup preflight before catalog/config rewrite/GPU/window.
- Add architecture checks against duplicate matrices or direct OS string logic.
- Add source-accounting receipt mapping every listed platform/build/OpenCL decision to this issue or a named later owner.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject unknown schema/enums, duplicate target/runner/package, malformed/overflow version, contradictory window/backend/fallback, missing minimums, matrix drift.
- Required identity unavailable means unsupported, not guessed. Optional display/backend evidence may yield CPU/headless decision.
- Unsupported exits before state mutation; CPU-only is valid and not blocked by WGPU absence.
- Diagnostics contain normalized aliases/versions only.

## Test matrix

- All minimum/below/above/malformed/unknown OS/libc versions.
- Linux Wayland/X11/both/neither/headless and backend ordering.
- macOS ARM64 boundary/Intel rejection; Windows build/target/backend/D3D11 rejection.
- Unsupported architectures/libc/WASM/mobile.
- Matrix/DAG/workflow/package/Iced drift.
- Startup zero-mutation unsupported and CPU-only success.
- Receipt privacy/determinism and architecture mutation.
- Source-map mutation adding platform/OpenCL/build branch without owner.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-core -p rusttable-app platform_registry` passes matrix/adapters/startup/privacy/source tests.
- `cargo xtask platform verify --all-targets --runtime-current --verify-startup-preflight` emits deterministic receipts.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 177` proves darktable platform/backend support responsibilities are accounted for.
- Hosted supported targets match matrix; synthetic unsupported mutates no catalog; GPU unavailable yields CPU-capable decision.

## Dependencies

Depends on #159, #167, #168, #453, and #555’s accepted source-map schema.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements matrix/domain/runtime identity/evaluation/semantic GPU contract/preflight/source accounting/xtask/receipts/tests. Do not enumerate WGPU adapters/create devices/run shaders/resources/change packages/add platforms.

## Post-completion source audit

The completed implementation remains accepted only when #555 verifies that its matrix/runtime adapters cover the pinned darktable platform, window, startup, and OpenCL-discovery responsibilities listed above. #290 must consume this typed contract and may not introduce a second support matrix.
issues.title: Implement the supported platform and GPU capability registry

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the structured diagnostics core required by every RustTable product service: stable diagnostic codes, typed fields, privacy classification/redaction, local human and JSON logging, bounded recent-event delivery, correlation context, and panic capture. This P0 issue supplies the diagnostics service needed by configuration, lifecycle, import, processing, GPU, and UI without absorbing the later support-bundle product feature.

## Fixed decisions

- Implement in `rusttable-diagnostics`; product crates depend on its domain/API, while `rusttable-app` owns initialization and sink lifecycle.
- Use `tracing` with RustTable-owned event/domain types. Product code emits stable codes and typed fields, not ad hoc strings parsed as API.
- Every diagnostic event contains:
  - `DiagnosticCode` with permanent subsystem-scoped ASCII identity;
  - severity `Trace`, `Debug`, `Info`, `Warning`, `Error`, or `Critical`;
  - subsystem and operation/stage;
  - monotonic event sequence and timestamp;
  - optional correlation/request/photo/edit/task/device aliases;
  - typed bounded fields with privacy class;
  - source build identity and schema version.
- Privacy classes are `Public`, `Operational`, `Private`, `Secret`, and `Payload`. Sinks apply explicit policy:
  - human local log may show Public/Operational and redacted Private aliases;
  - JSON local log stores typed Public/Operational and redacted Private aliases;
  - Secret/Payload values are never formatted or serialized.
- Paths, filenames, URLs, email addresses, camera serials, owner names, GPS, metadata/free text, credential values, database records, pixel/image data, and native driver dumps are Private/Secret/Payload according to typed field constructors. Callers cannot downgrade classification after construction.
- Redacted correlation uses a per-process random keyed hash for local logs. The key is never persisted. Equal sensitive values can be correlated within one process only.
- Default local logs are enabled under the platform state/log directory:
  - compact UTF-8 human log;
  - newline-delimited canonical JSON log;
  - rotation at 10 MiB, retaining five completed files per sink;
  - create-new/atomic rotation and bounded flush.
- Logging failure is nonfatal to image processing/application state. Sink health becomes a typed degraded-service finding and a bounded stderr fallback is used only for Warning+ events.
- Keep an in-memory ring of the latest 2,000 presentation-safe events and maximum 2 MiB total encoded fields for diagnostics UI/service health. Oldest events evict first.
- Correlation context propagates explicitly through #180 task groups and application service calls. No thread-local/global mutable request identity is the public API.
- Panic hook captures one sanitized event with thread alias, payload classification, and a bounded backtrace when enabled. It flushes sinks within a 500 ms best-effort deadline and performs no network I/O.
- No telemetry, analytics, automatic crash upload, network sink, user identifier, machine fingerprint, support ZIP, or bundle manifest is implemented here. Support bundles are a separate P3 issue.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/common/darktable.h` — diagnostic macro `dt_print` is the typed debug-emission boundary.
- `src/control/control.h` — function `dt_control_log` is the user-visible control-log action.
- `src/control/progress.c` — function `dt_control_progress_create` creates bounded progress reporting.
- `src/common/system_signal_handling.c` — function `dt_set_signal_handler` is the fatal-signal registration boundary.
- `src/lua/print.c` — function `dt_lua_init_print` wires scripting print output into product diagnostics.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define diagnostic code/subsystem/severity/privacy/field/event/context/sink/health/receipt types with checked constructors and canonical encoding.
- Implement tracing layer adapters for typed RustTable events, human/JSON formatters, classification/redaction, rotation, bounded flush, and sink health.
- Implement explicit context propagation helpers and #180 service/task integration hooks.
- Implement bounded recent-event ring with subscription/snapshot APIs for later UI.
- Implement panic-hook chaining, sanitized backtrace capture, reentrancy protection, and best-effort flush.
- Add source/policy checks rejecting direct `println!/eprintln!` in product crates except approved bootstrap/fatal fallback sites, tracing fields containing raw sensitive types, and network logging dependencies.
- Emit a bounded diagnostics-service receipt containing sink aliases, schema, rotation policy, counts/health, build identity, and no user paths or event values.
- Add a source-map receipt proving every diagnostic/progress/signal responsibility listed above has a Rust owner or a named later UI/scripting consumer.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject invalid/duplicate codes, oversized field values/event, unsupported schema, classification mismatch, secret serialization attempt, path escape/symlink log root, and rotation arithmetic overflow.
- Sink open/write/flush/rotation failure marks that sink degraded, keeps other sinks active, and never blocks processing indefinitely.
- Invalid UTF-8/native messages are lossily bounded only after privacy classification and are never allowed to inject new JSON records.
- Recursive diagnostics/panic handling uses one bounded fallback line and avoids infinite recursion/deadlock.
- Shutdown stops new subscriptions, flushes within deadline, closes sinks, and leaves no background writer task.

## Test matrix

- Every severity/subsystem/field/privacy class, canonical JSON, compact human format, correlation propagation, redaction, within-process alias equality, and cross-process alias difference.
- Rotation exact/oversize boundary, concurrent writers, sink failure/degraded fallback, read-only/full disk simulation, invalid UTF-8, newline/control injection, and shutdown.
- Ring count/byte eviction, concurrent snapshot/subscription, slow subscriber backpressure, and cancellation.
- Panic capture, recursive panic/logging failure, bounded backtrace, and no network activity.
- Secret-leak scans across logs, errors, Debug/Display, receipts, recent-event snapshots, and stderr fallback.
- Source-accounting fixtures for `dt_print`, control log/progress, startup/shutdown, signal handling, and Lua print callers; removing an anchor or leaving an owner unassigned fails #555.
- Architecture mutation fixtures for raw sensitive fields, direct product prints, unbounded channels, network sink, or global correlation state.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-diagnostics product_diagnostics` passes formatting, privacy, rotation, concurrency, panic, failure, source-accounting, and architecture suites.
- `cargo xtask diagnostics qualify --all-privacy --rotate --inject-failures --panic --verify-no-secrets` emits deterministic bounded receipts.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 178` proves every listed upstream source responsibility is mapped.
- Configuration/lifecycle/import/pixelpipe/GPU services can emit typed correlated events without depending on file/log implementation details.
- Logging failure cannot crash or block the application and no tested sensitive value appears in any sink/output.

## Dependencies

Depends on #159, #173, #177, and #555’s accepted source-map schema. Implementation may proceed before #555 closes only when this exact source block validates against the pinned commit.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements diagnostics domain, typed tracing integration, privacy/redaction, local human/JSON rotation, recent-event ring, context propagation, panic capture, service health, source-accounting policy, and tests. Do not implement support bundles, diagnostics UI, telemetry, network upload, crash reporting service, or performance dashboards.
issues.title: Implement structured product diagnostics, redaction, and local logging

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the single typed, versioned configuration service consumed by the RustTable desktop application and product services. Configuration must load deterministically from compiled defaults, one user TOML file, explicitly registered environment overrides, and CLI/bootstrap overrides; validate before startup; publish immutable snapshots; migrate transactionally; and save atomically without exposing secrets or allowing each subsystem to parse its own settings.

## Fixed decisions

- Implement configuration domain/service in `rusttable-core::config` or a dedicated lower-level product crate approved by the workspace DAG; `rusttable-app` owns platform path resolution, startup integration, and change orchestration. Iced widgets do not parse TOML/environment variables.
- Persist one user configuration file named `config.toml` in the platform configuration directory returned by `directories::ProjectDirs` for qualifier `com`, organization `cgasgarth`, application `RustTable`.
- Configuration schema starts at `ConfigVersion(1)`. The on-disk document contains an explicit `schema_version = 1`; missing version is treated as a version-0 legacy/empty candidate and migrates only through a tested path.
- Layer precedence is fixed, lowest to highest:
  1. compiled defaults;
  2. migrated user TOML;
  3. registered `RUSTTABLE_*` environment overrides;
  4. explicit startup/CLI overrides.
  Catalog/edit/project state is not configuration and never enters this file.
- Only the following environment overrides exist in v1: `RUSTTABLE_CONFIG_FILE`, `RUSTTABLE_CATALOG_FILE`, `RUSTTABLE_LOG_LEVEL`, `RUSTTABLE_GPU_MODE`, `RUSTTABLE_CPU_THREADS`, and `RUSTTABLE_HOST_MEMORY_MIB`. Unknown `RUSTTABLE_*` variables are reported and ignored; arbitrary nested field mapping is prohibited.
- V1 sections and defaults are fixed:
  - `ui`: `theme = "system"`, `reduced_motion = false`, `sidebar_visible = true`, `preview_max_edge = 1536` (`256..=8192`);
  - `catalog`: optional path, platform-data default, `create_if_missing = true`, `checkpoint_interval_seconds = 60` (`5..=3600`);
  - `import`: `max_concurrent_items = 4` (`1..=16`), `mode = "reference_in_place"`;
  - `processing`: `cpu_threads = 0`, `host_memory_mib = 0`, `preview_quality = "balanced"`;
  - `gpu`: `mode = "auto"`, `power_preference = "high_performance"`, optional exact adapter alias, `hard_budget_mib = 0`;
  - `cache`: `host_cache_mib = 512`, `thumbnail_memory_entries = 200`;
  - `diagnostics`: `level = "info"`, human/JSON logs enabled, recent count 2000;
  - `export`: `default_png_size = "fit_4096"`, `allow_upscale = false`, `collision = "create_new"`;
  - `camera`: enabled;
  - `scripting`: Lua and WASM extensions enabled.
- Defaults are product values, not darktable compatibility promises. A field/default change requires migration review and snapshot-identity change.
- Secrets and credential material are prohibited. Configuration stores only future `CredentialRef` identities.
- Unknown nonsecret fields/tables are preserved losslessly in a bounded tree during read→migrate→write. Limit to 1 MiB, depth 16, 10,000 keys, key length 128 bytes, and string value 64 KiB.
- Duplicate keys, type mismatches, unsupported future schema, invalid enums, non-finite numbers, range errors, path-policy violations, inconsistent values, or unavailable strict GPU selection are typed failures.
- `gpu.mode = "gpu"` is strict but degrades to an explicit configuration/service finding while CPU/catalog UI remains usable. `auto` selects qualified GPU or CPU; `cpu` disables compute only.
- `cpu_threads = 0` resolves to available parallelism clamped `1..=32`; explicit values are accepted to 256 with a warning when above available threads.
- `host_memory_mib = 0` resolves to 25% of physical memory, clamped 512 MiB..8 GiB; detection failure uses 1 GiB and reports it.
- Writes use same-directory temporary file, restrictive permissions, complete write, flush, file sync, atomic replace, and parent-directory sync where supported.
- Concurrent writers use optimistic revision/hash comparison. A failed load/reload/save leaves the previous valid file and immutable active snapshot intact.
- Live reload is explicit through `reload()`; filesystem watching and preferences UI are later issues.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/control/conf.h` — typed accessor `dt_conf_get_bool` represents the global preference-read contract.
- `src/control/conf.c` — initializer `dt_conf_init` establishes persisted configuration/default loading.
- `data/darktableconfig.xml.in` — configuration sections `<dttab name="processing">` and `<section name="opencl">` are schema/default inventory inputs, not Rust runtime keys.
- `src/common/file_location.c` — function `dt_loc_get_user_config_dir` resolves the user configuration location.
- `src/common/resource_limits.c` — function `dt_set_rlimits` applies process resource limits.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_init` consumes the legacy GPU configuration boundary redesigned into RustTable policy.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define checked config version, v1 sections/enums/values, unresolved/resolved snapshots, layer provenance, override registry, unknown-field tree, migration request/result, revision/hash, findings, change events, and privacy-safe receipt types.
- Implement bounded TOML parsing with duplicate detection, deterministic canonical output, v0→v1 migration, future-version rejection, unknown preservation, layer merge, environment/CLI parsing, cross-field validation, and platform/default resolution.
- Implement `ConfigurationService` with `load_initial`, `snapshot`, `reload`, `save`, `save_if_revision`, and bounded change subscriptions.
- Integrate #178 diagnostics with typed codes/private wrappers and #177 platform/GPU validation; expose ports consumed by #180.
- Add source/DAG checks preventing product subsystems from reading environment variables, TOML files, `directories`, or ad hoc config paths outside approved adapters.
- Add a migration-source accounting table mapping selected darktable keys/defaults from `darktableconfig.xml.in` and configuration call sites to RustTable fields, #191 compatibility records, or explicit exclusion.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Handle missing/empty/corrupt files, invalid UTF-8, malformed/duplicate TOML, unsupported schema, migration failure, unknown-field limits, secret-like keys, invalid override, unavailable strict backend, path/symlink policy, permission/full-disk/short-write/sync/rename failures, conflicts, cancellation, and shutdown.
- Missing user file loads defaults and does not create a file until explicit save.
- A corrupt file is never overwritten automatically; safe defaults/overrides run with a finding until user-confirmed save.
- Environment/CLI values are not persisted unless an explicit typed save requests it.
- Reloaded memory/thread/backend policy affects newly created work only; #180 coordinates service reconfiguration while existing requests retain prior snapshots.
- Diagnostics contain aliases/field IDs only, never raw environment, full paths, secret values, or unknown-field contents.

## Test matrix

- Defaults/resolution on Linux/macOS/Windows; missing/empty/commented files; canonical serialization and round trip.
- Every field boundary/enum/default, v0 migration, future schema, unknown nested fields, bounds, and secret rejection.
- Layer precedence for all registered overrides; unknown/invalid values; environment values not persisted.
- GPU auto/cpu/strict against synthetic #177 snapshots; automatic/explicit CPU thread and memory resolution.
- Atomic create/update and failure injection at each file stage; old-file preservation and conflict handling.
- Reload/subscriber order, backpressure, cancellation, active-snapshot preservation, Unicode/path/symlink/invalid UTF-8, and deterministic hashes.
- Source-map fixtures proving each selected `darktableconfig.xml.in` key/call site is preserved, redesigned through #191, or excluded; removing an accounting entry fails #555.
- Privacy/leak scans and architecture mutations adding direct environment/config parsing.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-core -p rusttable-app configuration` passes schema, migration, layering, platform, atomicity, concurrency, privacy, source-accounting, and architecture suites.
- `cargo xtask configuration qualify --all-platforms --all-layers --migrations --failure-injection --verify-privacy` emits deterministic receipts.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 179` proves every listed darktable configuration responsibility is accounted for.
- #180 initializes all services from one immutable snapshot; no subsystem parses TOML/environment independently.
- Every failed load/reload/save leaves prior valid file and active snapshot intact.

## Dependencies

Depends on #177, #178, and #555’s accepted source-map schema. Coordinates with #191 for darktable preference migration.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the v1 typed configuration schema/defaults, bounded parse/preserve/migrate/merge/resolve/validate service, atomic optimistic persistence, immutable snapshots/subscriptions, diagnostics/capability integration, darktable-key accounting, architecture checks, and tests. Do not implement preferences UI, filesystem watching, credential storage, catalog/project state, or broad per-operation settings.
issues.title: Implement versioned configuration layering, validation, and atomic migration

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Replace darktable’s process-wide global aggregate, ad hoc subsystem initialization, GLib/control job ownership, and dynamically discovered service/module lifecycle with one explicit Rust application lifecycle. The lifecycle owns configuration, diagnostics, platform support, catalog access, job execution, processing, GPU, render/export, camera, scripting, and UI-facing services through structured, observable, cancellation-safe concurrency.

## Fixed decisions

- `rusttable-app` is the only composition root. Product crates expose constructors and ports but never look up global services.
- Runtime states are `Created`, `Starting`, `Ready`, `Degraded`, `Stopping`, and `Stopped`. Transitions are one-way except `Ready ↔ Degraded`; failed startup always proceeds through cleanup to `Stopped`.
- Startup order is diagnostics → configuration → platform probe → job runtime → catalog locator/store → processing registry → GPU service → render/export → camera/scripting → UI model. Shutdown runs in reverse dependency order with bounded per-service deadlines.
- Use Tokio only in the composition/runtime layer and asynchronous I/O adapters. CPU image work uses dedicated bounded Rayon pools; product-domain APIs remain runtime-agnostic.
- Every spawned Tokio task belongs to an explicit service-owned task group. Detached `tokio::spawn`, abandoned `JoinHandle`, and fire-and-forget background loops are prohibited.
- Service task groups use stable Tokio APIs such as `JoinSet`, abort handles, cancel-safe `select!`, and explicit draining. `tokio_unstable` instrumentation is not required for correctness.
- Cancellation uses a RustTable-owned hierarchical token abstraction. Parent shutdown cancels descendants; child cancellation never implicitly stops unrelated services.
- Blocking work is not run on Tokio worker threads. Short bounded adapters use `spawn_blocking`; image processing uses Rayon; long-lived native/platform callback threads are owned and joined by their adapter service.
- Services are passed as `Arc<dyn Port>` only where replacement/testing requires dynamic dispatch. Concrete services are preferred; no service locator, static singleton, or global mutable registry is allowed.
- Required services are diagnostics, configuration, platform, runtime, catalog, and canonical CPU render. GPU, camera, scripting/extensions, and optional integrations may degrade while preserving CPU/library operation.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/main.c` — entry function `main` selects process startup and final shutdown paths.
- `src/common/darktable.c` — functions `dt_init` and `dt_cleanup` define ordered application construction and reverse teardown.
- `src/control/control.c` — function `dt_control_init` initializes application-wide control state.
- `src/control/jobs.c` — function `dt_control_job_create` creates lifecycle-owned background jobs.
- `src/common/database.c` — function `dt_database_init` establishes catalog database startup ownership.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_init` is the optional accelerator initialization/degradation boundary.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define `ServiceId`, `ServiceState`, dependency declaration, start/stop traits, health result, task-group identity, cancellation tree, lifecycle/task events, and versioned lifecycle receipts.
- Implement topological startup, reverse shutdown, duplicate/missing dependency detection, startup rollback, cancellation propagation, deadline enforcement, degraded optional-service behavior, and panic-to-finding isolation.
- Implement `ServiceTaskGroup` over stable Tokio primitives with spawn, bounded spawn-blocking, child token, task receipt, graceful drain, abort-after-deadline, and leak detection. Dropping an undrained group is a test failure.
- Add immutable `AppServices` exposing only established ports and a `ServiceHealthSnapshot` suitable for #178 diagnostics and later UI.
- Add telemetry for active tasks by service/stage, queue depth, cancellation reason, shutdown latency, panics, forced aborts, and blocking-pool use without unstable Tokio internals.
- Add fake services for success, ordering, start/cleanup failure, timeout, cancellation, optional degradation, repeated stop, panic isolation, concurrent health reads, task leak, nested cancellation, spawn-after-stop, and blocking misuse.
- Add Loom or equivalent focused model tests for lifecycle transitions and cancellation races where practical.
- Add architecture checks rejecting `static mut`, global `OnceLock` service registries, direct adapter construction from UI modules, unowned `tokio::spawn`, direct use of product services through global handles, and blocking filesystem/network calls on async workers.
