issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the bounded CPU work scheduler used by pixelpipe execution, decoding, visible thumbnails, export, and later analysis. Interactive preview remains responsive under background load while task, worker, memory, dependency, cancellation, and failure ownership remain explicit. This is the Rust replacement for darktable’s control job queues, worker threads, OpenMP-based image work, mipmap prefetch priorities, and pixelpipe tile scheduling.

## Fixed decisions

- Scheduler domain in `rusttable-pixelpipe`; #180 owns lifecycle and supplies dedicated bounded Rayon pool plus coordination. Pixel work never runs on Tokio workers and no second global executor exists.
- Priority classes: InteractivePreview; VisibleFullView; UserExport; VisibleThumbnail; PrefetchThumbnail; BackgroundAnalysis; Maintenance.
- Task declares request/snapshot/generation, priority, enqueue sequence, dependencies, #268 estimate, #269 leases, #272 scope, work-unit count, publication target.
- Hard limits for total/per-class queue, active pipelines, workers, memory. Overload typed; no unbounded growth.
- Weighted deficit round robin weights 16,8,4,4,2,1,1 plus aging after 2 seconds. Aging never outranks a runnable interactive task at admission.
- Admitted task may use Rayon work stealing but has max parallelism tokens based on workers/priority/tiles/memory; one export cannot consume all workers.
- Reserve at least one worker and 20% memory admission for Interactive/VisibleFull when queued; one-worker systems use cooperative boundaries.
- Task DAG dependencies; dependents run only after passes; failed/cancelled dependencies skip deterministically.
- Admit memory before execution using estimate plus actual lease. Mismatch is failure/finding, not silent expansion.
- Cancellation removes queued and asks active stop at work-unit boundaries; cancelled tasks cannot publish/cache partial.
- Panic contained at task/work-unit boundary; resources returned and unrelated capacity survives.
- Worker-count changes apply to new work/controlled pool replacement; no migration of running Rayon work.
- Scheduling never enters pixel identity; #272 gates publication.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/control/jobs.h` — type `dt_job_t` defines job state, priority, callback, and progress ownership.
- `src/control/jobs.c` — function `dt_control_add_job` enqueues bounded background work.
- `src/control/jobs.c` — function `dt_control_job_cancel` cancels queued or active work.
- `src/develop/pixelpipe_hb.c` — function `dt_dev_pixelpipe_process` is the CPU pipeline work unit.
- `src/common/mipmap_cache.c` — function `dt_mipmap_cache_get` supplies preview/thumbnail acquisition work.
- `src/common/resource_limits.c` — function `dt_set_rlimits` supplies process resource ceilings to scheduling policy.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define task/priority/dependency/resource/work-unit/outcome/metrics/receipt/config types.
- Implement admission, DAG tracking, weighted/aged selection, memory/parallelism tokens, cancellation, panic isolation, shutdown drain/abort, bounded result channels.
- Add model/reference scheduler and real Rayon adapter through #180.
- Integrate fake node/tile tasks, #270 cache and #269 leases; algorithms later.
- Emit bounded privacy-safe queue/wait/run/admission/memory/worker/fairness metrics.
- Add source/architecture checks rejecting unowned Rayon/global/OpenMP-equivalent spawning, Tokio CPU work, unbounded channels, or bypassed admission.
- Add source-accounting matrix mapping each darktable job queue/caller/OpenMP work class to scheduler priority/service or later specialized owner.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject invalid limits/weights, duplicate IDs, dependency cycles/missing IDs, impossible memory, queue overflow, post-shutdown submit, work-unit overflow.
- Never-fit request fails immediately `ResourceRequestTooLarge`.
- Queued cancellation acquires no leases; active cancellation returns all before receipt.
- Shutdown stops admission, cancels low/background first, drains visible/user work to deadlines, then cancels/joins through #180.
- Failed task skips dependents but not unrelated branches unless root policy requires.
- Scheduler accounting corruption or worker panic marks service degraded and prevents unsafe new admission.

## Test matrix

- Deterministic selection/classes/weights/aging/single-worker/reservation/export contention.
- Queue/per-class overload, memory admit/mismatch, lease wait/cancel, impossible request, budget changes.
- Dependency chains/diamonds/cycles/pass/fail/cancel and independent branches.
- Rapid generations, queued/active cancel, panic, shutdown, worker-count changes, no stale publication.
- Model/property randomized operations and multithread stress for starvation/inversion/leaks.
- Source-map mutations adding job class/caller/parallel loop without owner.
- Architecture mutations for Tokio CPU work/global Rayon/unbounded channels/bypass.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-pixelpipe scheduler` passes model/fairness/resource/cancel/panic/shutdown/stress/source suites.
- `cargo xtask pixelpipe scheduler-matrix --interactive-under-load --memory-pressure --failures all --verify-bounds` emits receipts.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 273` proves darktable job/worker/parallel scheduling responsibilities are accounted for.
- Sustained background/export cannot indefinitely delay interactive; lower classes progress by policy; every accepted task has bounded ownership.

## Dependencies

Depends on #178, #179, #180, #268, #269, #272, #515, and #555’s accepted source-map schema.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements scheduler domain, bounded queues/resources, Rayon adapter, dependencies/fairness/cancellation/panic/shutdown, source accounting, metrics, architecture checks, and tests. Do not implement pixel execution, WGPU scheduling, operation algorithms, thumbnail persistence, or performance budgets.
issues.title: Implement bounded CPU worker scheduling and priority classes

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Define and implement deterministic pixelpipe failure classification, cleanup, retry, and backend fallback. CPU and later WGPU execution produce one complete validated result or one actionable failure without partial publication/cache, retry loops, or silent semantic degradation. This replaces darktable’s scattered pixelpipe/OpenCL/tiling/cache/imageio error and CPU fallback paths with one typed source-accounted policy.

## Fixed decisions

- Closed categories: invalid request/snapshot; source/decode; preparation; node execution; nonfinite policy; resource/allocation; cache; CPU panic; GPU validation/device loss/OOM/timeout; cancellation/deadline; publication; invariant.
- Failure records stage/node/tile/backend/scope, retryability, cleanup/cache action, quarantine hint, user impact, deterministic precedence.
- Primary precedence: source/snapshot invalid → cancellation/deadline → invariant/data validation → resource → execution/backend → publication. Secondary bounded evidence cannot mask primary.
- Bounded transitions:
  - GPU execution → same semantic CPU plan;
  - GPU OOM/allocation → smaller legal tile up to two, then same semantic CPU;
  - CPU/GPU tile allocation → smaller legal tile up to two;
  - Interactive only → one descriptor-approved #271 approximation;
  - transient single-flight owner loss → one clean rebuild.
- Full/Export never lower algorithm, precision, profile, mask, or operation inclusion. CPU fallback executes same #266 semantics.
- Attempt identity records original request/snapshot, backend/tile/approximation, number, failure, final implementation. Cache keys reflect actual completed implementation/precision.
- Validate node/tile result before downstream/cache: descriptor/dimensions/length/nonfinite/lease/cancellation.
- Failed entries never promote; valid upstream cache remains unless evidence says corrupted/device-specific.
- Device quarantine is typed hint for later health service; no persistence here.
- Panics are not retried in same implementation in production. Cancellation never becomes retryable.
- Each attempt gets fresh output/scratch leases; failed partial buffer never treated initialized.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/develop/pixelpipe_hb.c` — function `dt_dev_pixelpipe_process` is the node/pipeline failure and fallback boundary.
- `src/develop/tiling.c` — helper `_maximum_number_tiles` bounds finite tile retry candidates.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_events_flush` reports queued-device completion/errors before fallback.
- `src/common/cache.c` — type `dt_cache_entry_t` represents failed/invalid cache-producer state.
- `src/imageio/imageio.c` — function `dt_imageio_export` is the export failure/publication boundary.
- `src/control/jobs.c` — function `dt_control_job_cancel` distinguishes cancellation from job failure.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define failure/finding/stage/scope/precedence/retry/attempt/state/outcome/receipt/identity types.
- Implement bounded state machine over executor/tile/cache/publication hooks with deterministic action selection/cleanup.
- Integrate #268 candidates, #269 poison/return, #270 cache, #271 degradation, #272 cancellation, #273 outcomes.
- Add fake CPU/GPU/node/cache/publication implementations covering all categories/paths.
- Define output validation hooks before success/cache/publication.
- Emit attempt/cleanup/invalidation/fallback/final receipts without pixels/paths/unbounded native text.
- Add source/architecture checks rejecting ad hoc retries, silent fallback/defaulting, direct device disabling, or broad cache clear outside policy.
- Add source-accounting table mapping all darktable error/fallback/retry/cleanup call sites to categories/actions or named later owners.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject unknown tags, retry cycles/overflow, unavailable fallback, illegal degradation, repeated identical plan, cleanup failure, cancellation during planning.
- Cleanup uncertainty escalates to invariant and stops retry.
- Publication failure never automatically reruns pixel processing; completed immutable pixels may be explicitly republished.
- Export may GPU→CPU but never approximate/reduce quality.
- Exhaustion returns primary plus complete attempt history.
- Multiple failures use fixed precedence and preserve bounded secondary evidence.

## Test matrix

- Every category/scope/precedence and simultaneous failures.
- GPU→CPU, OOM→smaller tiles→CPU, preview approximation, cache rebuild, unavailable fallback, repeated failure/exhaustion.
- Full/export no-degradation.
- Cancellation around attempts; cleanup error; panic; poisoned/partial/malformed/nonfinite output; stale permit.
- Cache preservation/invalidation.
- Model/property finite deterministic attempt order.
- Source-map mutations adding new error code/retry branch/cleanup path without classification.
- Architecture mutation adding loop/silent fallback/direct retry.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-pixelpipe failure_policy` passes taxonomy/precedence/retry/cleanup/cache/cancel/model/source suites.
- `cargo xtask pixelpipe failure-matrix --all-failures --all-purposes --verify-bounded-retries --verify-export-exact` emits receipts.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 274` proves darktable failure/fallback/cleanup responsibilities are accounted for.
- No injected failure publishes/caches partial output; every attempt sequence terminates; CPU fallback preserves snapshot/purpose/output semantics.

## Dependencies

Depends on #268, #269, #270, #271, #272, #273, #495, and #555’s accepted source-map schema.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements failure taxonomy/precedence, bounded retry/fallback, cleanup/cache/publication integration, source accounting, fake faults, architecture checks, and tests. Do not implement CPU execution, WGPU resources/submission, persistent device health, algorithms, or UI.

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Define and enforce the repository-wide numerical contract for CPU scalar, auto-vectorized, architecture SIMD, nightly portable-SIMD experiments, WGPU, color transforms, masks, analysis, and export conversion under the pinned Rust 1.98 beta compiler, so compiler optimization cannot silently alter darktable compatibility, cache identity, clipping, non-finite behavior, or repeatability.

## Fixed decisions

- The authoritative compiler for canonical CPU results is `beta-2026-07-18` from [0018]. Every accepted scalar/SIMD numerical receipt records its complete compiler and LLVM fingerprint.
- Canonical operation implementations use `f32` unless an operation’s compatibility manifest explicitly requires `f64` planning or accumulation. Parameter decoding, matrix solving, geometry, and global-statistic planning may use `f64`; pixel buffers remain in their typed declared format.
- No global fast-math or algebraic-relaxation flag is allowed. `-ffast-math` equivalents, LLVM fast-math injection, nightly `float_algebraic`, WGPU unchecked fast-math substitutions, and dependency features that enable reassociation globally are prohibited.
- The canonical scalar expression order is part of an operation version whenever darktable output depends on it. Refactoring algebraically equivalent expressions requires differential evidence and a version/tolerance review.
- Fused multiply-add is explicit:
  - use `mul_add` only where the reviewed reference performs FMA or the operation’s numerical contract explicitly permits fused rounding;
  - use separate multiply/add where historical behavior requires two roundings;
  - prevent compiler contraction in strict paths where contraction would change the contract.
- Parallel reductions use deterministic fixed partitioning and merge trees. Rayon worker count, tile order, scheduler timing, and WGPU workgroup ordering may not change an accepted result beyond its named [0008] tolerance.
- Signed zero, NaN, infinities, subnormals, overflow, underflow, comparisons, min/max, clamp, interpolation, integer conversion, half conversion, and exceptional transcendental inputs each receive one typed subsystem policy. RustTable does not inherit unspecified behavior from codecs, libc, GPU drivers, or compiler optimization modes.
- Canonical CPU code does not alter the process floating-point environment or enable flush-to-zero/denormals-are-zero globally. Any local FTZ path is a separately named implementation, proves its [0008] tolerance, restores state on every exit, and never defines the scalar reference.
- WGPU numerical differences are explicit kernel capability/tolerance data. A shader cannot claim exact parity if a backend permits different subnormal, NaN, contraction, interpolation, or transcendental behavior.
- Nightly primitive `f16` is not used for canonical CPU arithmetic. Stable `half::f16` may be used for storage, serialization, or upload and is converted to `f32` for CPU arithmetic. Qualified WGPU shader-f16 remains governed by [0134].
- Nightly `portable_simd` is used only by [0133A]. It obeys this contract, cannot enable algebraic float operations, and is not a product dependency while unstable.
- Stable/beta `std::arch` implementations may use target instructions only behind runtime/build qualification and the operation-specific FMA/rounding policy.
- Preview approximations are never implicit. A preview algorithm has a distinct implementation ID, quality level, cache key, tolerance, and receipt; export/reference requests cannot select it.
- Stochastic-looking operations use counter-based deterministic integer streams. They never use process/system RNG state, thread-local iteration order, or floating reductions to derive persistent noise.
- Compiler PGO, LTO, codegen-unit changes, future stable `#[optimize]`, stack protection, debug-info compression, and AutoFDO may change code generation but not numerical contracts. Each build-profile change requires the numerical corpus and benchmark evidence.
- The primary beta is not assumed correct merely because it is newer. It must pass the Rust 1.97.1 LLVM-miscompilation reproducer and all compiler/codegen sentinels from [0018].
- `rolling-beta`, `previous-stable`, and `current-nightly` from [0018A] are comparison compilers. Their outputs can detect regressions but cannot replace the primary beta baseline or redefine tolerances.
- If `#[optimize(size|speed|none)]` becomes available without feature gates on the authoritative compiler, RustTable adopts it only through a focused benchmark/code-size issue identifying exact functions. `optimize(none)` is not used as a correctness mechanism.

## Implementation

- Define `NumericalContract`, `FloatDomainPolicy`, `NonFinitePolicy`, `SubnormalPolicy`, `FmaPolicy`, `ReductionPolicy`, `TranscendentalPolicy`, `ConversionPolicy`, and `ImplementationNumerics` in the lowest appropriate shared crate without coupling domain crates to WGPU.
- Map every [0008] tolerance class to exact checks, absolute/relative/perceptual metrics, non-finite-location rules, compiler/backend comparison rules, and acceptable implementation families.
- Provide shared helpers for checked/clamped conversions, canonical half conversion, ordered min/max/clamp, finite-safe division, circular values, deterministic reduction trees, ULP inspection, and receipt generation. Helpers must not hide operation-specific compatibility branches.
- Define the primary beta release profile: optimization level, LTO, codegen units, panic strategy, overflow checks, target CPU/features, debug assertions, symbol settings, and compiler fingerprint. Reject hidden `RUSTFLAGS`, environment, Cargo profile, build-script, or package-builder overrides that alter floating semantics.
- Add compile/codegen probes under `beta-2026-07-18` for contraction/FMA, target-feature dispatch, integer-to-float rounding, half conversion, subnormal handling, signed zero, min/max, vectorization-sensitive loops, and the 1.97.1 miscompilation reproducer on each supported architecture.
- Add a process-start floating-environment probe and adapter-boundary guard tests proving native libraries cannot leave MXCSR/FPCR or rounding modes modified.
- Add deterministic reduction infrastructure for histograms, sums, moments, covariance, k-means inputs, wavelet statistics, and other shared analysis; operation issues select the exact policy.
- Add WGSL numerical metadata/reflection fields for f16/f32 use, contraction assumptions, transcendental use, storage/texture sampling, atomics/reductions, and assigned tolerance.
- Add `xtask numerics verify` to inspect Cargo/rustc flags, compiler fingerprint, dependency features, WGSL manifests, implementation registrations, and receipts; reject unregistered fast-math, algebraic, FTZ, FMA, or compiler-baseline choices.
- Add `xtask numerics compare-compilers` covering primary beta, rolling beta, previous stable sentinels, current nightly stable-source builds, debug/release/LTO variants, CPU feature variants, and [0133A] without treating comparison output as canonical.
- Compiler-comparison receipts include compiler/LLVM hashes, source and implementation hashes, build profile, target features, platform, exact fixture set, and result/tolerance deltas.

## Failure and edge behavior

- An operation or kernel without a complete numerical contract is not executable or registrable.
- A result produced by a compiler other than the accepted primary-beta fingerprint cannot populate canonical baseline artifacts or caches used as parity evidence.
- Non-finite values are never silently converted to zero, clipped, serialized, or passed to integer/texture indices unless the selected policy explicitly requires it and records a counter.
- A native adapter changing rounding/denormal state fails its boundary test and is disabled; RustTable does not tolerate process-global FP-state corruption.
- A CPU/GPU/SIMD/compiler implementation exceeding tolerance is disabled or blocked for the exact operation/device/compiler/implementation hash rather than widening a global tolerance.
- A compiler-channel or build-profile comparison that changes strict output produces a blocking regression receipt and minimized fixture.
- Unsupported hardware feature paths cannot be selected by configuration or cache reuse.
- A primary-beta miscompilation sentinel failure blocks all numerical acceptance, even when ordinary unit tests happen to pass.

## Test matrix

- Adversarial vectors cover normal boundaries, ±0, smallest/largest normal and subnormal, NaN payload classes, ±Inf, cancellation, overflow/underflow, half-way rounding, integer extremes, FMA-sensitive triples, min/max NaNs, clamp inversions, and transcendental domain edges.
- Tests compare scalar, auto-vectorized, x86, AArch64, portable-SIMD lab, WGPU tiers/backends, primary/rolling beta, previous stable sentinels, current nightly, debug/release/LTO profiles, thread counts, tile grids, and cache reuse under their declared contracts.
- Deterministic-reduction tests randomize input partitions, Rayon scheduling, tile order, and worker count while requiring identical receipts/results according to policy.
- Native-adapter tests deliberately alter the floating environment and prove guard detection/restoration.
- Mutation fixtures inject algebraic float methods, global target-feature/fast-math flags, hidden FMA, unregistered f16 arithmetic, nondeterministic reductions, tolerance widening, compiler-fingerprint substitution, and baseline generation under the wrong compiler.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask numerics verify --all-implementations --all-profiles` passes.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask ecosystem compiler-sentinels --numerics` passes, including the 1.97.1 LLVM regression reproducer.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask numerics compare-compilers --primary-beta --rolling-beta --previous-stable --current-nightly` emits complete comparison receipts.
- The adversarial corpus passes scalar/SIMD/WGPU/build-profile comparisons on supported platform runners.
- Every optimized implementation names its scalar reference, compiler/implementation hash, numerical policies, and [0008] tolerance.
- No accepted Cargo/rustc/WGSL configuration enables unregistered algebraic, fast-math, FTZ, or unstable float behavior.

## Dependencies

Depends on #164, #166, #174, #427, #177, and #180.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements numerical policy/types/helpers, primary-beta compiler/profile/WGSL checks, deterministic reduction primitives, adversarial fixtures, compiler-comparison tooling, and verification. Do not implement operation algorithms, architecture SIMD kernels, the portable-SIMD lab, GPU operations, or broad performance tuning.
issues.title: Define deterministic floating-point and fast-math policy

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Create the Rust 1.98-beta CPU kernel layer and deterministic runtime dispatch used by image operations, color, masks, resampling, and analysis, with a canonical scalar implementation always available and architecture-specialized implementations selected only after complete capability and numerical validation.

## Fixed decisions

- Product code compiles with the authoritative `beta-2026-07-18` toolchain from [0018], edition 2024, and `rust-version = "1.98"`.
- The beta compiler is used only through APIs available without feature gates. This issue does not use `std::simd`, `#![feature(portable_simd)]`, `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`, nightly ABIs/APIs, unstable Cargo flags, or compiler-internal interfaces.
- `rusttable-simd` owns shared kernel contracts, implementation registration, feature qualification, forced-dispatch test controls, and dispatch receipts. Operation semantics remain in operation/color/mask crates.
- Every kernel family has one explicit scalar implementation that defines expression order and [0130] behavior under the primary beta compiler. Optimized implementations use the same checked typed views and cannot bypass format, stride, ROI, aliasing, or compiler-fingerprint validation.
- Shared kernel families are limited to proven reusable behavior: point transforms, matrix/vector transforms, LUT sampling, format/bit-depth conversion, mask arithmetic, interpolation primitives, separable-convolution building blocks, histogram/reduction building blocks, and copy/fill/alpha helpers. Operation-specific algorithms are not generalized prematurely.
- Compiler auto-vectorization may be used for portable loops under the primary beta, but the implementation remains `PortableRust`; it is not assumed to vectorize and cannot require a target feature beyond the baseline target.
- Architecture features are detected once per process through Rust target-feature APIs available on the primary compiler and normalized into a `CpuCapabilitySnapshot`. Dispatch never infers features from CPU model or operating-system strings.
- Compile-time and runtime target features are distinct. A function annotated with a target feature is callable only through a generated checked wrapper whose precondition is satisfied by the snapshot.
- Dispatch is selected per kernel family, element/layout contract, numerical policy, compiler/implementation hash, alignment, stride, tail capability, and quality. A single global AVX2/NEON switch is insufficient.
- Dispatch order is fixed and benchmark-informed. A higher feature tier may be rejected for a kernel when parity, downclocking, tail overhead, setup cost, thermal behavior, or representative image sizes make a lower tier preferable.
- Dispatch results are immutable for a process capability/compiler generation and observable in receipts. They do not enter persisted edits, but implementation/compiler IDs participate in performance and parity evidence.
- All implementations support arbitrary legal row stride, declared alignment/unaligned starts, odd dimensions, scalar tails, bounded cancellation points, and aliasing rules from `rusttable-image`.
- Input/output aliasing is explicit per kernel. In-place, overlapping, and disjoint contracts are different types; an implementation cannot assume restrict-like behavior without proof from the contract.
- The nightly portable-SIMD comparison is isolated to [0133A]. It consumes public/test contracts and scalar fixtures but is not reachable from product dispatch.
- A rolling-beta refresh cannot silently change accepted dispatch thresholds or implementation qualification. Compiler changes invalidate benchmark/codegen evidence and require [0018A] refresh receipts before the primary beta pin can move.

## Implementation

- Define typed kernel descriptors, sealed traits, or function-table records for each approved family, including element/channel/layout, read/write aliasing, ROI, alignment, stride, support radius, numerical contract, cancellation granularity, compiler identity, feature requirements, and implementation ID.
- Define `CpuCapabilitySnapshot` for x86_64 and AArch64 baseline/optional features used by [0132]/[0133], plus cache-line/page size and atomic-alignment capabilities needed by infrastructure.
- Implement one-time capability probing and deterministic normalization. Tests inject synthetic snapshots; product code cannot mutate the live snapshot.
- Implement `KernelRegistry` and `KernelDispatchPlan` with unique IDs, duplicate/conflicting registration checks, stable priority rules, qualified benchmark selection data, accepted compiler fingerprints, and exact rejection reasons.
- Implement scalar and portable-Rust versions of approved shared primitives using typed image/mask views, checked extent arithmetic, deterministic tails, and [0130] helpers.
- Generate target-feature wrapper skeletons consumed by [0132]/[0133]. Raw target-feature functions remain private and callable only through wrappers carrying proof/guard checks in debug/test builds.
- Add test-only forced selection modes: `ScalarOnly`, `PortableRustOnly`, `ArchitecturePreferred`, and exact implementation ID. Forced unsupported selection returns a typed error rather than executing illegal instructions.
- Add dispatch micro-size thresholds so small tiles may remain scalar when SIMD setup/tail cost loses. Thresholds are versioned performance policy generated from accepted primary-beta benchmark evidence and never alter numerical semantics.
- Integrate cancellation, execution receipts, benchmark counters, support-bundle summaries, compiler identity, and cache implementation identity where relevant.
- Add architecture checks preventing operation crates from using `std::arch`, custom target-feature wrappers, independent feature tables, compiler intrinsics, or nightly SIMD outside `rusttable-simd` and [0133A].
- Add rolling-beta compile/codegen comparison hooks from [0018A] so a future beta snapshot that changes vectorization, target-feature lowering, or ABI behavior is detected before baseline refresh.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Unsupported or unqualified implementations are absent from candidate sets and cannot be forced in product builds.
- A kernel receipt or qualification generated by a compiler other than the accepted primary-beta fingerprint cannot enter production dispatch policy.
- Capability-probe ambiguity, virtualization inconsistencies, or OS-disabled extended state conservatively remove affected tiers.
- Misaligned or unsupported stride/layout requests select a compatible implementation or scalar path; they never reinterpret memory or copy silently unless the caller requested materialization.
- A parity or guard failure disables only the exact implementation/kernel/feature/compiler combination and emits bounded evidence; tolerances are not widened.
- Cancellation publishes no partial output or cache entry. Kernels declare whether scratch is required before publication.
- Panics are forbidden inside hot callbacks. Planning detects dimensions, strides, offsets, and byte-size overflow before dispatch.
- A moving beta or nightly result never replaces the primary dispatch table automatically.

## Test matrix

- Unit tests cover descriptor validation, compiler identity, synthetic capability snapshots, candidate ranking, threshold selection, unsupported forcing, target-feature wrappers, implementation IDs, aliasing contracts, cancellation, and error redaction.
- Scalar/portable primitives are tested with aligned and every relevant unaligned start, arbitrary/padded strides, odd/tiny/large bounded extents, invalid zero-size cases, every vector-width tail, overlapping/in-place/disjoint buffers, non-finite values, and guard pages.
- Differential tests compare every implementation against scalar using [0130]/[0008], randomized data, representative photographic distributions, and operation fixtures under `beta-2026-07-18`.
- Compiler-comparison tests run portable/scalar kernels under primary beta, rolling beta, previous-stable sentinels where compatible, and current nightly, recording codegen/numerical deltas without changing canonical results.
- Multi-thread tests prove registry/probe initialization and dispatch are race-free and deterministic.
- Mutation tests reject direct `std::arch` outside approved modules, unwrapped target-feature functions, missing tails, unregistered IDs, hidden alignment assumptions, wrong-compiler qualification, nightly feature gates, and moving-beta dispatch publication.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 test -p rusttable-simd kernel_dispatch --all-features` passes.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask cpu-kernels verify --scalar --portable --all-layouts --all-tails` emits compiler, capability, dispatch, parity, and guard receipts.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask cpu-kernels compare-compilers --rolling-beta --current-nightly` reports deltas without modifying the accepted registry.
- Synthetic unsupported-feature and wrong-compiler fixtures cannot execute or publish target-specific implementations.
- Every shared kernel family has a scalar reference, complete [0130] contract, deterministic dispatch, primary-beta qualification, and no nightly product dependency.

## Dependencies

Depends on #174, #177, #222, #223, #267, and #286.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements shared kernel contracts, scalar/portable-Rust primitives, capability snapshot, registry/dispatch, target-feature wrapper skeletons, forced-dispatch testing, compiler qualification, and architecture checks. x86_64 kernels belong to [0132], AArch64 kernels to [0133], and nightly portable-SIMD qualification to [0133A]. Do not port operation-specific algorithms or add WGPU code.
issues.title: Implement portable CPU kernels and architecture feature dispatch

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Turn `rusttable-gpu` into the sole WGPU instance/adapter/device/queue owner and capability service. RustTable initializes headless compute on supported backends, deterministically selects and behaviorally qualifies an adapter, requests only reviewed features, exposes immutable capability/fault snapshots, and returns `CpuOnly` without disrupting #539. This is the explicit WGPU replacement for darktable’s OpenCL loader/platform/device/queue/capability/fault manager.

## Fixed decisions

- Use pinned WGPU 30.x stable APIs only. ExperimentalFeatures, passthrough, `as_hal`, mesh/ray/cooperative-matrix, and backend escape hatches prohibited.
- GPU crate may depend on WGPU/runtime ports, not Iced state, persistence, operation-specific code, or native darktable/OpenCL.
- Backend policy from #177: Linux Vulkan→GL; macOS Metal; Windows D3D12→Vulkan; CPU-only always valid.
- Surface-independent device initialization; presentation outside.
- Execution tiers: CoreCompute; EnhancedCompute; SubgroupCompute; CpuOnly.
- Product API exposes semantic `GpuCapabilitySnapshot`/`GpuFeaturePlan`, never raw WGPU features.
- Deterministic ranking: platform backend order; hardware over software; envelope; power preference; conservative limit score; hashed identity tie.
- Rejected adapters retained with bounded reasons; driver/device names privacy classified.
- Request minimum features; optional only after advertised support + behavioral probe.
- Core probes: compute/storage buffers, RGBA16F sampled/render attachment, R32F storage texture where available or buffer path, binding/alignment, copy/map readback.
- Optional probes: f16, 16-bit formats, subgroups, float atomics, immediate data, timestamps, adapter-specific format use, pipeline cache.
- Tiny reviewed bounded probe shaders only.
- Async #180 startup with 10s deadlines/cancel; CpuOnly is Ready/Degraded.
- Fault states generation-based: Healthy/Degraded/Lost/DisabledForSession; loss/OOM/validation/submission invalidates resources.
- At most one recreation after 5s cooldown; repeated loss disables session.
- Pipeline-cache blobs untrusted performance artifacts keyed by complete device/shader/layout/version identity; corrupt discarded.
- No OpenCL dependency/backend.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/common/opencl.h` — types `dt_opencl_t` and `dt_opencl_device_t` model global accelerator and per-device capability state.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — functions `dt_opencl_init` and `dt_opencl_cleanup` define optional device lifecycle.
- `src/common/dlopencl.c` — function `dt_dlopencl_init` loads the legacy OpenCL runtime.
- `src/common/opencl_drivers_blacklist.h` — function `_opencl_check_driver_blacklist` rejects known-bad driver identities.
- `src/control/conf.h` — accessor `dt_conf_get_bool` reads legacy OpenCL enablement policy.
- `src/develop/pixelpipe_hb.c` — identifier `dt_opencl` is the pipeline dispatch capability boundary.
- `data/kernels/programs.conf` — program entry `basic` is a kernel-registry configuration key.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define backend/adapter/device identities, preference/envelope, advertised/requested/qualified features, limits/formats/memory hints, tiers, plans, selection/probe/fault/cache receipts/errors.
- Implement WGPU instance/enumeration/normalization/ranking/request calculation/device+queue/callbacks/deadlines/cancel/lifecycle.
- Implement bounded probes and canonical immutable snapshot hash.
- Implement semantic `for_kernel` returning variant/tier or CpuRequired.
- Implement fault generation/event stream/one recreation/resource invalidation notifications.
- Implement pipeline-cache identity/header/validation/atomic storage/size eviction skeleton.
- Add synthetic adapters/probes/faults/cache plus real hosted smokes.
- Integrate exact WGPU/version/features with #517/#453 and #180 health.
- Add source/architecture checks rejecting extra device creation, raw feature switches, OpenCL/experimental/as_hal, or WGPU leakage.
- Add source-accounting table mapping every darktable OpenCL manager/config/program/host-kernel responsibility to this issue or #292–#301/per-operation owner.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Distinguish unsupported platform/backend, no adapter, feature/limit mismatch, timeout/cancel, probe mismatch, loss, OOM, uncaptured error, cache invalid/corrupt, planning error.
- GPU failure yields explicit CpuOnly when app otherwise supports; catalog/#539 remain usable.
- Unsupported feature is caught before device request.
- Optional probe failure disables only optional capability unless core behavior fails.
- Device/driver change invalidates evidence/cache.
- No private paths/unbounded driver text in receipts.

## Test matrix

- Supported platform/backend ranking/fallback/hardware/software/ties/power/headless/no adapter/unsupported.
- Minimum request and every probe pass/fail/misadvertisement/format/copy-map behavior.
- Timeout/cancel/validation/loss/OOM/recreation/repeated disable/shutdown/generation.
- Pipeline-cache identity/corrupt/mismatch/atomic/eviction.
- Real Vulkan/Metal/D3D12 and GL fallback smoke with CpuOnly acceptable.
- Source-map mutation adding OpenCL program/device/config/driver rule without owner.
- Architecture mutation for extra device/raw feature/experimental/OpenCL/leak.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-gpu device` passes selection/capability/probes/faults/cache/source/architecture.
- `cargo xtask gpu qualify --platform current --all-adapters --all-stable-probes --verify-cpu-fallback --verify-loss` emits receipts.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 290` proves the darktable OpenCL manager responsibilities and delegated program/kernel owners are complete.
- Hosted backends yield qualified snapshot or CpuOnly; every kernel requests semantics without raw adapter inspection.

## Dependencies

Depends on #177, #180, #517, #539, #555’s accepted source-map schema.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements WGPU instance/adapter/device/queue ownership, semantic probing/selection/plans, fault/recreation, cache-policy skeleton, lifecycle/source accounting/smokes/tests. Do not implement resource pools, shader registry, transfers, dispatch, submission, tiled execution, surfaces, or operation kernels.
issues.title: Create the rusttable-gpu crate and WGPU instance/device selection
Implement GPU capability probing, limits, and device policy

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the reviewed WGSL source, validation, reflection, and identity system used by every RustTable compute shader. Product code binds only generated, validated layouts tied to a stable shader/program/entry-point identity; user-provided or dynamically assembled shader source is prohibited. This is the source-accounted WGPU replacement for darktable’s OpenCL program registry, kernel-source assembly, compilation/cache, kernel creation, host binding, and operation dispatch contracts.

The first production shader set is concrete: scalar point kernels for transfer decode/encode, exposure, linear offset, and RGB gain matching the #539 CPU pixelpipe. Exposure is a bounded direct source-equivalent kernel. Transfer helpers, linear offset, RGB gain, copy, and probe kernels are RustTable infrastructure and must not claim darktable operation compatibility identities. More complex operation kernels remain their own issues.

## Fixed decisions

- Shader ownership lives in `rusttable-gpu::shader`; operation definitions reference stable shader entry IDs through #265 GPU bindings but cannot embed WGSL strings or WGPU binding layouts.
- WGSL source is checked into `crates/rusttable-gpu/shaders/` as UTF-8 LF text under repository policy. Includes use a bounded RustTable preprocessor supporting only reviewed relative include IDs and constant/template substitutions; no filesystem path, environment, script, macro language, or network input.
- One `ShaderProgram` may contain multiple entry points. Stable identity fields are program ID/version, entry-point ID/version, source tree hash, generated WGSL hash, reflection schema, numerical class, required #290 feature plan, and owning operation/kernel IDs.
- Source/version changes that can affect code generation or pixels require an entry implementation-version bump and necessarily change pipeline/cache identity.
- Use Naga/WGPU validation from the pinned WGPU ecosystem. Reflection is generated from the parsed validated module, not duplicated by hand.
- Generated reflection includes:
  - bind groups/bindings and resource type/access;
  - address spaces, minimum binding sizes, dynamic-offset/alignment requirements;
  - texture/storage formats and dimensions;
  - push/immediate/uniform/storage parameter records;
  - workgroup size and overrides/specialization constants;
  - entry-point stage and required capabilities;
  - source spans mapped to stable source aliases;
  - numerical metadata.
- Numerical metadata is per entry point and explicit: f32/f16 use, `mul_add`/contraction assumption, transcendental operations, texture filtering/sampling, atomics/reductions, subnormal/non-finite policy, assigned schema-3 #516 tolerance, and canonical CPU reference ID. This issue does not require the broad #286 repository numerical-policy project.
- Initial point kernels use `CoreCompute`, f32 storage buffers, no f16, no atomics, no subgroup, no filtered texture sampling, one-dimensional invocation over RGBA pixels, bounds checks for tail invocations, and fixed scalar expression order matching CPU known-answer tests.
- Binding layout for the initial kernels is fixed:
  - group 0 binding 0: read-only input `array<vec4<f32>>`;
  - group 0 binding 1: read-write output `array<vec4<f32>>`;
  - group 0 binding 2: uniform parameter block with pixel count and operation parameters, padded to WGPU alignment;
  - workgroup size 256, with exact tail bounds check.
- Alpha is passed through unchanged by transfer/exposure/offset/gain point kernels unless the operation contract explicitly says otherwise. No shader transfer-decodes alpha.
- No runtime shader compilation from scripts, WASM extensions, catalog data, files selected by users, or network packages.
- Validation errors are normalized and bounded; raw driver paths/source roots are not serialized.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `data/kernels/programs.conf` — program entry `basic` is a canonical OpenCL program-registry key.
- `data/kernels/basic.cl` — kernel `exposure` is the direct initial shader responsibility.
- `data/kernels/colorspaces.cl` — kernel/helper `Lab_to_XYZ` represents shared color-kernel source that requires an explicit owner.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_create_kernel` binds a program/entry to a host kernel handle.
- `src/iop/exposure.c` — field `dt_iop_exposure_global_data_t::kernel_exposure` and callback `process_cl` bind the exposure kernel.
- `src/develop/pixelpipe_hb.c` — identifier `process_cl` is the operation GPU-dispatch selection boundary.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define shader/program/entry/source/include/template/specialization/feature/numerical/reflection/identity/receipt models and typed failures.
- Implement deterministic include/template expansion, Naga parse/validation, reflection extraction, canonical manifest generation, source/span aliasing, and checked-in generated manifest verification.
- Generate Rust binding-layout and typed parameter-packing code from reflection. Product code cannot hand-author a parallel layout for registered shaders.
- Implement `cargo xtask shaders generate`, `check`, and `smoke` using #290-qualified devices where available and Naga-only validation otherwise.
- Add the initial transfer decode/encode, exposure, linear-offset, RGB-gain, and byte-copy/probe shaders plus invalid fixtures.
- Cross-check each production entry against its #265 GPU binding, #263 capability declaration, #290 feature plan, CPU reference ID, #495 source program/kernel evidence, owning issue, and #516 tolerance.
- Add backend compilation/pipeline-creation smoke tests for qualified Vulkan, Metal, D3D12, and GL fallback where available; absence produces explicit CPU-only/unavailable evidence.
- Add source/DAG checks rejecting inline WGSL, raw WGPU layout declarations for registered entries, unchecked source generation, dynamic user shader paths, OpenCL source loading, and reflection drift.
- Add a complete generated source-accounting table joining `programs.conf`, transitive kernel sources/includes, host creation/argument/dispatch call sites, #495 operation manifest entries, and Rust shader/operation owners. Unported entries remain explicit `Deferred` with owner issue, never silently absent.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject duplicate IDs/versions, path traversal/includes cycles, unknown substitutions, malformed/invalid WGSL, reflection/layout ambiguity, unsupported feature/format, missing CPU reference/tolerance/owner, source-manifest drift, unowned OpenCL program/kernel/call site, noncanonical generated output, and excessive source/manifest size.
- A shader failing backend creation is unavailable on that exact WGPU/backend/device/driver/source identity and does not affect CPU processing.
- Error normalization preserves stable source alias/line/column and bounded message class while removing absolute paths and unbounded driver text.
- Generated files are updated atomically and `check` never mutates.
- A Rust-only bootstrap shader cannot be presented as darktable compatibility evidence. A direct compatibility kernel cannot omit its exact source program/entry/host call-site evidence.

## Test matrix

- Deterministic expansion/reflection/codegen, include order/cycle/traversal, substitutions, overrides, all binding/resource kinds needed now, and manifest/hash mutation.
- Invalid syntax/type/access/layout/workgroup/feature/format/min-size fixtures.
- Exact `programs.conf` inventory, transitive include closure, host call-site program/entry/argument joins, duplicate/unowned/stale program/kernel, and source drift.
- Initial point kernel CPU known-answer comparisons for finite, extended-range, alpha, odd/tail counts, and schema-3 tolerance.
- Direct exposure source/host binding parity and explicit no-analogue classification for transfer/offset/gain/copy/probe entries.
- Backend creation smokes, capability rejection, driver error normalization, and CPU-only host behavior.
- Architecture mutations for inline/dynamic WGSL, handwritten drifted layouts, OpenCL loading/mechanical translation, missing owner/reference/tolerance, or experimental feature use.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-gpu shader` passes parser, reflection, codegen, identity, source-accounting, point-kernel, and architecture suites.
- `cargo xtask shaders check --all` reports no source/reflection/binding/program/kernel/owner drift.
- `cargo xtask shaders smoke --qualified-backends --cpu-parity` creates and executes the initial point entries where GPU is available and records explicit unavailable results otherwise.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 292` proves every darktable program/kernel/include/host-binding responsibility is owned or explicitly deferred to its operation/common issue.
- Changing any source/layout/specialization/numerical contract changes the implementation/cache identity and cannot leave stale generated binding code.

## Dependencies

Depends on #263, #265, #290, #495, #516, #517, and #555’s accepted source-map schema.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements shader source policy, bounded expansion, complete OpenCL-to-WGSL source accounting, Naga/WGPU validation, generated reflection/bindings/manifests, initial point shaders, identity/numerical metadata, backend smokes, and tests. Do not implement general resource pools, transfers, compute dispatch scheduling, operation-specific complex kernels, user shaders, or viewport presentation.
issues.title: Implement shader source registry, validation, and reflection manifests

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement reusable WGPU buffer, texture, view, bind-group, staging, and readback pools under conservative per-device budgets. Resources have explicit ownership and submission lifetime and cannot be reused while commands reference them; OOM returns cleanly to #274 CPU fallback. This replaces darktable’s OpenCL memory objects, pinned host/device allocation accounting, per-device memory limits, and manual event-dependent release.

## Fixed decisions

- Ownership only in `rusttable-gpu`; callers receive typed leases/opaque IDs, not pool internals/global raw handles.
- Exact classes by device generation, type, size/dimensions, format, usage, mip/sample, alignment, mapping, compatibility. Broader usage is not interchangeable unless explicit rule.
- States: Creating, ExclusiveLeased, ImmutableLeased, InFlight, Pooled, Mapped, Evicted, Lost, Poisoned; checked transitions.
- One writer for mutable/exclusive; immutable sharing only after valid initialization and no write/map transition.
- Submission token owns in-flight lifetime; return only after #296 completion.
- Budget includes resident/idle/staging/readback/views/bind groups plus conservative backend overhead.
- Hard budget min(config cap, #290 memory hint, addressable/limits); default 1 GiB discrete, 512 MiB unified. Soft 80%.
- Soft triggers idle eviction; hard rejects after legal unpinned eviction.
- Eviction: mapped/readback idle, largest staging, probationary, protected. Active/in-flight/pinned never revoked.
- Init policy: full-overwrite uninitialized, zeroed, uploaded, preserved immutable. Partial writes initialize untouched regions before publication.
- Device loss invalidates generation/wakes waiters; no cross-generation resources.
- Oversize one-shot within hard budget destroyed after retirement.
- Bind-group/view cache exact resource generation/layout/subresource and holds ownership until retirement.
- No upload strategy, dispatch, tile residency, or viewport surfaces here.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/common/opencl.h` — type `dt_opencl_device_t` contains per-device memory/resource state.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_alloc_device` allocates a device memory object.
- `src/develop/pixelpipe_hb.c` — function `dt_opencl_alloc_device` is used for per-pipeline device resources.
- `src/develop/tiling.c` — function `dt_opencl_alloc_device` is used for tile resource allocation and cleanup.

- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_events_reset` gates resource retirement after queued work.

## Implementation

- Define descriptors/IDs/generations/leases/states/budgets/accounting/allocation/eviction/fence hooks/metrics/receipts/errors.
- Implement thread-safe lookup/create/lease/freeze/map/unmap/submit/retire/poison/evict/loss/shutdown.
- Integrate #268 admission, #290 limits/faults, #292 reflection, #274 OOM reporting.
- Add deterministic abstract model and real small buffer/texture/view/bind/map smokes.
- Emit bounded state/bytes/budget/reuse/eviction/submission/loss receipts.
- Add architecture checks rejecting direct WGPU resource creation outside approved pool/device/probe modules.

## Failure and edge behavior

- OOM cleans partial resources and reports estimate/actual/budget.

- Poisoned/lost destroyed, never reused.
- Shutdown blocks new leases, waits boundedly for retirement, reports leaks, closes pool.

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement byte-exact, bounded host↔GPU transfers for typed image tiles, operation parameters, LUTs, masks, and result readback. Transfer planning must honor #222 descriptors, WGPU copy alignment, #293 resource lifetimes/budgets, #272 cancellation, and #290 device capability without whole-image hidden copies or benchmark prerequisites. This is the source-accounted WGPU replacement for darktable’s direct and pinned OpenCL host/image/buffer copy, mapping, row-pitch, and tile-transfer paths.

## Fixed decisions

- Transfers live in `rusttable-gpu::transfer`; callers submit typed requests and leases, not raw queue writes, map calls, or copy commands.
- Supported initial transfer payloads are:
  - tightly packed/padded RGBA `f32` host buffers ↔ storage buffers;
  - bounded typed uniform blocks;
  - raw byte/LUT buffers with explicit element/count/alignment;
  - RGBA16Float/R32Float texture rows where #290 qualifies the format.
- Host descriptors specify dimensions, row stride, sample/layout/alpha/color identity, byte range, and source lease. GPU descriptors specify resource/subresource, offset, size, format, usage, row pitch, rows-per-image, and device generation.
- Strategy is explicit:
  - queue write for parameter/small payloads up to 64 KiB;
  - pooled staging buffer and copy for larger uploads;
  - pooled readback buffer plus async mapping for reads;
  - unified-memory hints may alter thresholds only through a versioned policy and never bypass lifetime/alignment checks.
- WGPU `COPY_BYTES_PER_ROW_ALIGNMENT` padding is calculated with checked arithmetic. Padding bytes are deterministic zero and excluded from logical content hashes.
- Partial tiles/regions copy only declared rows/columns, with exact edge behavior. Unsupported implicit format conversion is rejected; conversion belongs to a reviewed shader/CPU stage.
- Transfer request identity includes source/target descriptors, logical region, device/resource generation, strategy policy version, and content identity where available. Scheduling/timing does not affect pixel identity.
- Upload ownership: the source host lease remains valid until staging/queue consumption is complete according to the selected strategy. Staging/GPU leases enter in-flight state before submission.
- Readback ownership: mapped bytes are validated/copied into a destination #269 lease before unmapping/retiring the GPU resource. Raw mapped ranges do not escape the transfer service.
- Cancellation before command submission releases all leases and submits nothing. After submission, resources retire normally and #272 prevents stale publication; mapping may still complete solely for cleanup.
- Transfer receipts record logical/physical bytes, padding, strategy, source/target identities, mapping/submission IDs, duration, and cancellation, not pixel contents or paths.
- No compute pipeline, multi-node residency, compressed texture format, zero-copy surface sharing, or performance gate is implemented here.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/common/opencl.c` — functions `dt_opencl_copy_image_to_host` and `dt_opencl_read_host_from_image_raw` define image readback actions.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — functions `dt_opencl_write_host_to_image` and `dt_opencl_map_buffer` define upload/mapped-staging actions.
- `src/develop/pixelpipe_hb.c` — function `dt_opencl_copy_image_to_host` is the final pipeline readback handoff.
- `src/develop/tiling.c` — type `dt_iop_roi_t` is consumed for per-tile transfer regions.
- `src/iop/exposure.c` — callback `process_cl` demonstrates typed operation parameter/resource transfer into a kernel.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_events_flush` supplies completion evidence before transfer leases retire.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define upload/readback/copy descriptors, regions, strategy policy, packing plans, parameter blocks, completion results, metrics, receipts, and typed errors.
- Implement checked row/offset/size/alignment planning and deterministic host↔staging packing/unpacking.
- Implement small queue writes, staging uploads/copies, async map/readback, cancellation, completion callbacks, and #293 lease/fence transitions.
- Integrate #290 device limits/feature/format plans, #292 generated parameter/resource layouts, and #272 scopes/publication hooks.
- Add mock transfer backend/model tests and real backend round-trip smokes for every supported initial format/strategy.
- Add architecture checks rejecting direct `queue.write_*`, `map_async`, transfer-purpose command encoding, raw mapped-range escape, or OpenCL/native transfer reuse outside approved GPU modules.
- Add source-accounting generation joining every pinned OpenCL copy/map/write/read call site to a Rust transfer class, payload owner, and later dispatch/retirement owner.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject descriptor/region mismatch, out-of-bounds offset, arithmetic overflow, unqualified format/usage, invalid row pitch, source length mismatch, device generation mismatch, mapped-state conflict, budget/allocation failure, copy validation error, map timeout/failure, device loss, and cancellation.
- A failed upload/readback returns/poisons resources according to actual write state and never exposes partial logical output.
- Readback padding is never copied into logical image rows.
- Late map completion after cancellation is drained and retired without publication.
- CPU-only hosts return explicit transfer unavailability and leave #539 usable.
- A newly discovered darktable transfer helper or operation-local copy path without class/owner/strategy mapping fails #555.

## Test matrix

- Tight/padded/arbitrary legal host strides; small/large thresholds; partial/edge/odd/tiny regions; every required alignment/tail.
- RGBA f32 buffers, uniform/LUT/profile/mask bytes, qualified RGBA16Float/R32Float textures, and unsupported conversions/formats.
- Exact host→GPU→host round trips with independent expected bytes and content hashes.
- Direct/staging/pinned-policy equivalents, device-side image/buffer copies, map/unmap state, blocking/asynchronous completion, and logical versus physical byte accounting.
- Cancellation before/after submission, map timeout/failure, device loss/generation change, OOM/budget pressure, and concurrent transfers.
- Backend smokes on qualified Vulkan/Metal/D3D12/GL plus CPU-only evidence.
- Source-map mutations adding OpenCL transfer API/call site/pinned path without owner.
- Architecture mutations for direct queue/map bypass, raw pointer/mapped range escape, or operation-local transfer planning.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-gpu transfer` passes packing, model, cancellation, lifetime, source-accounting, failure, and round-trip suites.
- `cargo xtask gpu transfer-matrix --all-strides --all-regions --all-strategies --roundtrip --verify-bounds` emits deterministic receipts.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 294` proves every listed darktable host/device transfer, mapping, region, pinned/unified, and call-site responsibility is owned or explicitly delegated.
- Every supported round trip matches logical source bytes exactly and no resource is unmapped/reused before completion.
- No dependency on benchmark #455 is required; transfer counters are available for later benchmarks.

## Dependencies

Depends on #222, #223, #269, #272, #290, #292, #293, #495, and #555’s accepted source-map schema.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements typed transfer requests/plans, source-accounted copy/map strategies, packing/padding, staging/queue-write/readback, leases/fences/cancellation, round-trip/backend tests, metrics, and architecture checks. Do not implement compute dispatch, submission scheduling, tile residency, operation kernels beyond transfer probes, viewport surfaces, or performance thresholds.

Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement one checked WGPU compute-dispatch boundary binding #265 operation GPU definitions to #292 reflection, #293 resources, #294 transfers, #267/#268 ROI/tile plans, and #290 capabilities. Initial production dispatches accelerate #539’s exact transfer decode/encode, exposure, linear offset, and RGB gain. This replaces darktable’s host-side OpenCL kernel creation, argument packing, work-size calculation, and enqueue boundary.

## Fixed decisions

- Dispatch only in `rusttable-gpu::dispatch`; application/pixelpipe/operation/UI cannot create pipelines/bind groups/encoders or dispatch directly.
- Immutable `PreparedGpuKernel`: operation/instance/implementation, #292 entry/reflection, #290 plan/generation, typed params, descriptors, ROI/tile, grid, binding plan, #516 tolerance/CPU reference.
- Pipeline identity includes device/backend/driver generation, WGPU, shader/reflection/entry/layout/overrides/numerical class. Cache blob is performance input only.
- Bind groups generated from reflection; exact binding type/access/usage/format/dimension/min size/offset/alignment/generation.
- Initial storage-buffer layout, one invocation/RGBA pixel, workgroup 256, checked ceil/tail and device limits.
- Generated parameter records with zero padding; never Rust struct layout.
- GPU definition requires canonical CPU executor/parity fixture.
- All-or-nothing encoding: validate/acquire/create all dependencies before adding dispatch; failure submits nothing.
- Pipeline/bind caches exact/bounded/single-flight; failed creation not success cache.
- Produces encoded batches/dependencies for #296; no `queue.submit`.
- Batch may contain ordered compatible dispatches; no shader fusion.
- No f16/subgroup/atomic/complex/user/multi-adapter/viewport pipelines.

- `data/kernels/basic.cl` — kernel `exposure` declares a concrete entry-point signature.
- `src/iop/exposure.c` — field `dt_iop_exposure_global_data_t::kernel_exposure` identifies the host-bound kernel.
- `src/develop/pixelpipe_hb.c` — callback `process_cl` is the ordered pipeline dispatch invocation boundary.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

## Implementation

- Implement reflection-driven packing/validation, grid planning, pipeline/bind single-flight caches, command encoder, stable debug labels, batch finalization.
- Bind initial point shaders to #265 definitions.
- Integrate ROI/tile/capability/reflection/resources/transfers/cancel/failure.
- Add fake invalid cases and real backend execute smokes via test helper until #296.
- Emit bounded identities/hashes/grid/cache outcomes.
- Add architecture checks rejecting direct pipeline/bind/encoder/dispatch or handwritten registered layouts.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject unavailable features, reflection drift, unknown entry, parameter mismatch, resource mismatch, bounds/alignment/format/usage, generation, grid overflow/undercoverage, pipeline/cache failure, cancel/loss.

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement bounded asynchronous WGPU command submission, dependency ordering, completion/fence tracking, cancellation/stale-result handling, resource retirement, and typed failure propagation. Encoded batches from #295 remain alive exactly until GPU completion and can publish only through #272 generation permits. This is the source-accounted WGPU replacement for darktable’s OpenCL command queues, event lists/tags, async pixelpipe mode, finish/flush synchronization, and event-dependent memory release.

## Fixed decisions

- Submission ownership lives in `rusttable-gpu::submission`; callers submit immutable one-shot #295 batches and receive typed completion handles/events, not direct queue callbacks or polling loops.
- Each submission has stable ID, device generation, request/snapshot/generation, priority, dependency IDs, command batch identity, owned #293 resource leases, upload/readback plans, expected result descriptors, deadline, and publication/cache targets.
- One WGPU queue per selected device is used initially. Semantic order is expressed by batch command order and explicit submission dependencies; no second hidden queue scheduler is introduced.
- In-flight limits are hard: default maximum 8 submissions and configurable conservative bytes/resources derived from #293. Admission waits/cancels or returns typed overload; it never queues unbounded work.
- Priority classes mirror #273. Submission selection uses the same weighted ordering principles, but already submitted hardware work is not preempted. Interactive work is protected by admission reserves rather than unsafe cancellation.
- Dependencies form an acyclic graph. A submission enters the queue only when dependencies complete successfully; failed/cancelled dependencies produce skipped outcomes.
- Compatible encoded batches for the same request/device may be combined before submit only when #295 marks them combinable and resource/dependency order remains exact. Combining changes submission identity/metrics, not shader/pixel identity.
- Completion is detected through WGPU submission index/device polling integrated with #180-owned task lifecycle. Busy spin is prohibited; polling/wake intervals are bounded and shutdown-aware.
- Cancellation before queue submission releases resources and submits nothing. Cancellation after submit marks the consumer/result obsolete, retains all resources until completion, skips readback/publication unless cleanup requires it, and records `CompletedObsolete`.
- Timeout after submit does not assume hardware cancellation. It reports timeout, marks result obsolete, and continues bounded cleanup/polling; device-loss escalation follows #290/#274 policy.
- Completion validates device generation, WGPU error scopes/uncaptured errors, expected output resource state, and optional timestamp data before releasing resources or enabling readback/publication.
- #293 resources transition to `InFlight` atomically before `queue.submit` and retire exactly once after completion/failure/device loss.
- A successful compute result is not current merely because GPU completed. Cache/UI/output promotion requires a fresh #272 publication permit and independent result validation/readback where applicable.
- Timestamp queries are recorded only when #290 qualified and the request enabled diagnostics; their absence never changes correctness or algorithm selection.
- No GPU tile orchestration, multi-adapter assignment, persistent quarantine, or operation-specific shader work beyond the initial point batches is implemented here.

## Upstream darktable source anchors

Canonical source: `darktable-org/darktable@cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad`.

- `src/common/opencl.h` — type `dt_opencl_device_t` contains command queue and event-list state.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_events_flush` consolidates queued event completion.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_finish_sync_pipe` enforces synchronous completion for an exact pipeline purpose.
- `src/common/opencl.c` — function `dt_opencl_events_reset` retires per-device event accounting.
- `src/develop/pixelpipe_hb.c` — function `dt_opencl_events_flush` is the asynchronous pipeline completion check.
- `src/imageio/imageio.c` — function `dt_imageio_export` is the synchronous export handoff that cannot publish before completion.


**Preserve:** the listed upstream responsibilities, their observable ordering/default/failure semantics, and the explicitly named acceptance oracles; any behavior not yet represented by a typed anchor remains blocked migration debt.

**Redesign:** implement the responsibility in safe Rust at this issue’s Rust-owned boundaries, without copying, compiling, linking, or invoking darktable C/C++/OpenCL code.

**Excluded:** unanchored upstream behavior, source-text translation, and any product scope outside this issue’s one-PR boundary.

Rust ownership: the Rust crates/modules and tests named by this issue’s implementation and acceptance contracts own the replacement behavior.
## Implementation

- Define submission/dependency/priority/admission/completion/failure/timing/resource/publication receipt types and checked configuration.
- Implement bounded priority/dependency queue, one-shot batch admission, resource in-flight transition, queue submit, async poll/completion loop, error-scope/uncaptured-error collection, cancellation/timeout/shutdown, and exact retirement.
- Integrate #272 scopes/gates, #273 priority intent, #274 failures, #290 device generation/fault events, #293 resources, #294 transfers, and #295 encoded batches.
- Add a deterministic abstract submission model plus real backend point-kernel smokes.
- Emit bounded receipts for admission/wait/submit/complete/obsolete/failure, dependencies, resource counts/bytes, timing availability, and publication outcome without driver dumps or pixels.
- Add architecture checks rejecting direct `queue.submit`, custom poll loops, independent event/fence models, or resource retirement outside approved GPU modules.
- Add source-accounting generation mapping every OpenCL enqueue/event/flush/finish/async/export/lifetime call site to #295 encoding, #296 submission, #293/#294 resources, #272 publication, or a named operation owner.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject duplicate IDs, dependency cycle/missing/wrong device, already-submitted batch, admission overflow, resource transition failure, submit validation error, device generation mismatch/loss, deadline/cancellation, output-state mismatch, double retirement, and publication without permit.
- Queue submission is the commit point. Any failure before it submits nothing; any failure after it retires every owned resource through the completion/loss path.
- Callback/poll panic is contained by #180, marks affected submissions failed, and cannot leak resources or stop CPU processing.
- Device loss resolves all in-flight submissions once, retires/loss-invalidates resources, and reports fallback eligibility through #274.
- Shutdown stops admission, cancels unsubmitted batches, drains in-flight work within deadline, then closes/loss-invalidates the device service.
- A newly discovered darktable enqueue/event/finish/async/lifetime branch without an explicit source owner fails #555.

## Test matrix

- Dependency chains/diamonds/cycles, priority/admission reserves, in-flight limits, batch combining, and deterministic receipt order.
- Cancellation before/after submit, rapid generations, late completion, timeout, shutdown, device loss, validation/uncaptured errors, and polling failure.
- Asynchronous preview versus synchronous export, event reset/flush/finish equivalents, late OpenCL-style error, lost event, and complete-pipeline fallback.
- Resource lifetime/no premature reuse/exact retirement/double-retire prevention.
- Successful, failed, skipped, obsolete, and publication-permit outcomes.
- Initial point kernels on qualified Vulkan/Metal/D3D12/GL plus CPU-only unavailable evidence.
- Source-map mutations adding enqueue/event/finish/async/export branch without owner.
- Model/property tests and architecture mutations for direct submit/poll/retirement bypass.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-gpu submission` passes model, dependency, admission, cancellation, lifetime, fault, shutdown, source-accounting, and backend suites.
- `cargo xtask gpu submission-matrix --basic-kernels --dependencies --rapid-generations --faults all --verify-resource-retirement` emits deterministic receipts.
- `cargo xtask migration source-map verify --issue 296` proves every listed darktable command-queue/event/async/finish/export/resource-lifetime responsibility is owned or delegated.
- Every submitted resource remains alive until completion and returns exactly once; obsolete results never publish.
- CPU-only and GPU-failure cases leave #539 available through #274.

## Dependencies

Depends on #178, #180, #272, #273, #274, #290, #293, #294, #295, #495, and #555’s accepted source-map schema.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements source-accounted bounded GPU submission/dependency scheduling, completion/polling/fences, cancellation/timeout/stale publication, resource retirement, fault propagation, model/backend tests, receipts, and architecture checks. Do not implement GPU tile planning/OOM retries, multi-adapter scheduling, persistent quarantine, viewport surfaces, or complex operation kernels.
