Deliver the production `email` destination for opening the user’s configured desktop mail composer with one or more fully rendered export artifacts attached, while preserving queue staging, bundle integrity, privacy, bounded attachment size, and explicit handoff semantics. RustTable must not implement SMTP, store mail-server credentials, or claim delivery success it cannot observe.

## Fixed decisions

- Destination ID is exactly `email`; implement orchestration in `rusttable-export::destinations::email` with platform adapters in isolated crates/modules:
  - Linux: `xdg-email`/desktop portal attachment handoff, preferring the XDG desktop portal when available;
  - macOS: native share/mail compose service through a narrow Objective-C adapter;
  - Windows: Simple MAPI or the supported shell mail-composer handoff through a narrow Win32 adapter.
- No SMTP, OAuth mail provider integration, embedded webmail, browser automation, or credential storage is included.
- A successful destination result means `ComposerOpened` and the operating system accepted the attachment handoff. It does not mean the message was sent, delivered, or accepted by a server.
- Settings are versioned: optional `to`, `cc`, `bcc`, subject template, body template, attachment filename template reference, one-artifact-per-message or bundle mode, maximum total attachment bytes, and whether to open the containing folder as a fallback when no composer capability exists.
- Recipient fields are explicit RFC mailbox strings validated without making network/DNS assumptions. They are never inferred from image metadata.
- Subject/body templating uses the bounded [0253] engine with a text-only variable registry. Shell expansion, HTML execution, environment variables, and unrestricted metadata access are prohibited.
- Attachments are copied from queue staging into an app-owned handoff directory with randomized unguessable directory name, strict permissions, canonical filenames, exact hashes, and a lease expiration. The OS composer receives only these immutable copies.
- The handoff directory is retained until the user closes RustTable or the configured expiry elapses because desktop composers may read attachments lazily. Cleanup occurs only for RustTable-owned directories with a valid ownership manifest.
- The queue job reaches terminal `Succeeded` with result `ComposerOpened` after the platform call returns acceptance. User cancellation inside the mail application cannot be observed and is not treated as a RustTable queue failure.
- If no composer exists, capability validation reports unavailable. An explicit recipe fallback may open the handoff folder and copy subject/body text to the clipboard; it is recorded as `FolderFallback`, not email success.
- Secrets and recipient/body text are redacted from general logs and receipts. Receipts store field hashes, counts, byte lengths, attachment names/hashes, platform adapter, and result.

## Implementation

- Add settings DTO/migrations, descriptor/capability probe, validation, canonical hash, and [0238] destination interface.
- Implement mailbox-list parser/validator with quoted display names, Unicode, comma separation, and bounded counts/lengths; reject control/header-injection characters.
- Implement subject/body evaluation using the text-safe template context and newline normalization.
- Implement app-owned handoff directory creation, permission checks, attachment copy/hash verification, bundle manifest, lease database, startup reconciliation, and expiry cleanup.
- Implement Linux portal/xdg-email, macOS, and Windows adapters with typed capability/result/error codes, argument/URL encoding, cancellation boundaries, and no shell command construction. External commands use argv arrays only.
- Implement attachment count/total/per-file size limits and platform capability negotiation before copying.
- Emit privacy-safe receipt with adapter/capability, recipients count/hash, subject/body hash/length, attachment manifest, handoff lease, result, timing, and cleanup state.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject invalid/header-injection recipient strings, invalid templates, disallowed variables, attachment count/size above recipe or platform limits, unsupported composer, failed secure handoff directory, hash/copy mismatch, platform invocation error, cancellation before handoff, and cleanup-manifest corruption.
- Platform invocation never receives private source paths—only app-owned attachment copies.
- Failure before platform acceptance leaves the queue job retryable/permanent according to typed error and removes the unconsumed handoff directory when safe.
- Failure or cancellation after platform acceptance does not delete attachments immediately; it records the handoff lease and lets expiry cleanup handle them.
- Startup cleanup never deletes a directory lacking a matching ownership manifest and database lease.
- The fallback folder path and message text are exposed only to the user-facing UI/clipboard service, not general logs.

## Test matrix

- Unit tests for settings/migrations, recipient parsing/injection rejection, text template context/redaction, attachment names/counts/sizes, handoff manifests/leases/permissions, capability/result mapping, cleanup safety, and validation.
- Adapter tests use fake portal/MAPI/native services to cover accepted, unavailable, user-service error, malformed attachment, timeout, and cancellation paths without sending mail.
- Platform integration tests verify a real composer receives exact attachment bytes/names in opt-in CI/manual jobs; no automated message sending occurs.
- Fault-injection tests cover copy/hash/manifest/database/adapter/cleanup boundaries and process restart.
- Property tests prove no shell interpolation, no source path handoff, attachment hash equality, lease cleanup only removes owned paths, and logs/receipts omit recipient/body contents.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-export email_destination` plus platform-adapter/fault-injection suites pass.
- `cargo xtask destination-fixture --destination email --fake-composer all --verify-privacy --verify-cleanup` produces capability, template, attachment, lease, adapter, and result receipts.
- Supported platforms open a composer with exact attachments; unavailable platforms fail before export commit or use only the explicit folder fallback; RustTable never stores SMTP credentials or claims message delivery; and temporary data is bounded and safely cleaned.

## Dependencies

Depends on #398, #413, #414, and the platform integration foundations in #1, #2, #159, #160, #161, #162, #163, #164, #165, #166, #167, #168, #169, #170, #171, #172, #173, #174, #175, #176, #177, #178, #179, #180, #181.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the system email-composer destination, secure attachment handoff/leases, platform adapters, text templating, tests, and receipts. Do not implement SMTP/provider APIs, address-book UI, webmail, generic share sheets beyond mail, encoders, or production export UI.
issues.title: Implement system email-composer export destination

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Deliver the production `gallery` destination for generating and atomically committing a self-contained static HTML gallery bundle from queued photos. The bundle must have deterministic ordering and filenames, responsive image variants, thumbnails, captions and metadata governed by export privacy policy, accessible keyboard/screen-reader behavior, no runtime server or external CDN, and crash-safe bundle publication.

## Fixed decisions

- Destination ID is exactly `gallery`; implement in `rusttable-export::destinations::gallery` using Rust-owned HTML/CSS/JavaScript templates checked into the repository as immutable hashed assets.
- Output is a directory bundle containing `index.html`, local CSS/JS, one manifest JSON, thumbnail/preview/full image artifacts requested through [0236], and no network-loaded fonts, scripts, analytics, tracking pixels, or external assets.
- Gallery ordering is frozen at queue insertion from explicit batch order, with a stable per-item sequence and photo/edit/request hashes. Retries and queue reordering do not change gallery order.
- Generate exactly three image roles by default: thumbnail, display preview, and optional download/full asset. Each role is a normal typed export request with an explicit encoder/size/profile/metadata policy. The gallery builder does not decode or resize images itself.
- Built-in default uses JPEG display assets with sRGB output, 4:4:4 thumbnails/previews, metadata stripped except permitted caption/rights text, and optional original/full download disabled. Recipes may select supported encoders independently for each role.
- Captions and visible metadata come only from the canonical [0252] packet after gallery-specific allowlisting. GPS, people, source paths, hidden tags, and edit history are absent unless the recipe explicitly includes them. The manifest contains only public values selected for display.
- HTML/text is escaped through a typed renderer. User metadata is never inserted as raw HTML, CSS, JavaScript, URL, or attribute syntax. Markdown is unsupported in 1.0.
- CSS and JavaScript are fixed bundled assets. JavaScript is progressive enhancement for modal navigation, preloading, and keyboard controls; the gallery remains navigable as plain HTML when scripts are disabled.
- Accessibility requirements are fixed: semantic landmarks, ordered list or figure structure, unique headings, alt text from explicit metadata fallback rules, visible focus, keyboard previous/next/close, reduced-motion respect, sufficient default contrast, and no focus trap.
- Thumbnail/preview dimensions and `srcset`/sizes attributes are deterministic. EXIF orientation is already canonical. Width/height attributes are emitted to prevent layout shifts.
- The bundle manifest includes schema/version, ordered item IDs, public text, artifact relative paths/hashes/dimensions, theme ID/hash, and no private paths/credentials.
- Commit uses the [0254] secure disk bundle protocol: build in a hidden sibling staging directory, verify every artifact and internal reference, then promote atomically or via final manifest marker. Existing gallery replacement follows the selected collision policy; unrelated files are never merged implicitly.
- Themes are immutable packaged `light`, `dark`, and `system` variants with fixed CSS variable sets. Arbitrary custom JavaScript/CSS/template execution is outside 1.0.

## Implementation

- Add settings DTO/migrations, descriptor/capabilities, image-role recipe references/settings, theme enum, metadata/alt/caption policy, download toggle, page title/description, validation, canonical hash, and [0238] destination integration.
- Define versioned gallery manifest/domain types and canonical JSON encoding.
- Implement deterministic batch child-request expansion for all image roles, stable artifact naming through [0253], dependency tracking, progress aggregation, and bounded concurrency through the export queue.
- Implement a typed HTML renderer with compile-time template structures, correct escaping for text/attribute/URL/JSON contexts, local asset hashing, and CSP meta policy forbidding external network/script sources.
- Implement responsive layout, semantic markup, keyboard/modal script, reduced-motion behavior, theme assets, and public manifest generation.
- Add a bundle validator that parses generated HTML/JSON, verifies relative references/hash/dimensions, rejects traversal/absolute/external URLs, verifies no private fields, checks duplicate IDs, and runs an accessibility ruleset.
- Integrate disk bundle staging/promotion, idempotency, replacement, receipts, cancellation, restart recovery, and cleanup.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject empty selections, invalid child-role recipes, unsupported encoder/profile/metadata combinations, unsafe/malformed visible text, missing required alt fallback, duplicate logical names, path/reference traversal, external URL generation, artifact/hash mismatch, accessibility validation failure, bundle limits, cancellation, and disk commit failure without publishing the new gallery.
- One item’s render failure follows the explicit recipe policy: fail the complete gallery or omit the item with an omission receipt. Default is fail complete; silent partial galleries are prohibited.
- Replacement keeps the prior complete gallery visible until the new bundle is fully validated and promoted.
- Custom/user assets are unsupported and cannot bypass escaping or CSP.
- Cancellation/restart leaves only RustTable-owned staging, which queue/disk recovery reconciles safely.

## Test matrix

- Unit tests for settings/migrations, ordering, child-request generation, theme/assets hashes, caption/alt fallbacks, every escape context, manifest canonicalization, relative URL construction, CSP, accessibility markup/keyboard model, bundle validation, and privacy redaction.
- Gallery fixtures cover 1/many/large-bounded item counts, varied aspect/orientation/dimensions, Unicode/HTML/script-like metadata, missing titles/descriptions, duplicate basenames, all themes, optional downloads, multiple image encoders, and omitted sensitive categories.
- Browser tests in a pinned headless browser verify responsive layout, modal/keyboard/focus/reduced-motion behavior, no external requests, image references/dimensions, script-disabled navigation, and accessibility audits.
- Fault-injection tests cover child render, template, write, reference verification, staging, promotion, replacement, restart, and cleanup boundaries.
- Property tests prove deterministic bundle bytes/hashes for identical snapshots, no external/traversal URL, all user text escaped, no sensitive value in HTML/JSON, stable order across retries, and zero/one complete visible bundle.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-export gallery_destination` plus browser/accessibility/fault-injection suites pass.
- `cargo xtask gallery-fixture --themes all --items fixtures/gallery --verify-browser --verify-a11y --verify-privacy --repeat 2` produces child-request, bundle, reference, browser, accessibility, privacy, idempotency, and commit receipts.
- Identical inputs produce identical self-contained galleries; no external network request or unescaped metadata exists; keyboard/screen-reader requirements pass; and crashes/failures leave the previous complete bundle or no new bundle, never a partial gallery.

## Dependencies

Depends on #396, #397, #398, #413, #414, #415, and the required child encoders.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the static gallery destination, immutable themes/assets, child-request expansion, safe HTML/JSON rendering, accessibility/browser validation, bundle commit, tests, and receipts. Do not implement a gallery server, upload hosting, arbitrary themes/scripts, gallery editor UI, or generic website publishing.
issues.title: Implement deterministic static web-gallery destination

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Deliver the production `latex` destination for generating a deterministic, self-contained LaTeX project containing a contact sheet or image sequence, rendered image assets, captions, optional metadata table, and a reproducible build manifest. RustTable must not invoke TeX during normal export; it generates validated `.tex` and assets that the user may compile separately.

## Fixed decisions

- Destination ID is exactly `latex`; implement in `rusttable-export::destinations::latex` using Rust-owned templates/assets and the [0254] atomic bundle protocol.
- Output bundle contains `contact-sheet.tex`, image assets, optional local style file, a canonical JSON manifest, and a short generated `BUILD.txt` with fixed compiler command text. No TeX binary is executed by product code.
- Document class is fixed to `article` for 1.0 with packaged `rusttable-contactsheet.sty`; arbitrary preambles/packages/macros/user TeX are unsupported to prevent code injection and portability drift.
- Page size is explicit ISO/US/custom using the same unit table as [0250]. Settings include orientation, margins, rows/columns, cell gap, image fit (`Contain` or `FillCrop`), caption position, title/footer/page numbers, metadata fields, image-role recipe, and whether to create one page per fixed grid or a continuous sequence.
- Item order and sequence are snapshotted at queue insertion. Layout is deterministic row-major with optional explicit column-major mode; retries/reorder do not change it.
- Every image asset is a child [0236] request with exact dimensions/profile/metadata policy. Default is sRGB JPEG with metadata stripped; the LaTeX generator does not decode/resample images.
- Captions/metadata are sourced only from [0252] after an explicit public-field allowlist. Every inserted value is escaped for TeX text context using a fixed mapping for `\ { } $ & # ^ _ % ~` and Unicode handling. Raw TeX, shell escape, file paths, environment variables, and unrestricted metadata are prohibited.
- Filenames come from [0253], are relative and portable, and are inserted through a safe `\detokenize`/generated macro contract. No absolute paths or traversal.
- The generated style uses standard packages selected and pinned in the template (`graphicx`, `geometry`, `xcolor`, `longtable` only as needed) and does not load remote resources. Theme is fixed `light` or `dark-print` with packaged color definitions.
- A test-only pinned TeX engine compiles fixtures in CI with shell escape disabled. Runtime output is the project bundle only.
- Commit is atomic as a directory bundle. Existing projects are replaced only under the selected collision policy; unrelated files are never merged.

## Implementation

- Add settings DTO/migrations, descriptor/capabilities, page/grid/theme/caption/metadata fields, validation, canonical hash, and [0238] integration.
- Define versioned project manifest and child-request expansion, stable ordering, artifact dependencies, progress aggregation, and bounded concurrency.
- Implement checked page/grid/cell geometry in TeX points, image size/request derivation, caption truncation policy, page breaking, and deterministic identifiers.
- Implement typed TeX renderer/escaper for text, paths, numeric dimensions, colors, and JSON manifest; do not use string-concatenated raw templates with user data.
- Generate packaged `.sty`, main `.tex`, asset references, `BUILD.txt`, manifest, and optional thumbnail/contact metadata table.
- Add validator that parses the generated subset/references, verifies no dangerous primitives (`\write18`, `\input` outside packaged/local files, `\openout`, shell escape, absolute paths), checks all assets/hashes/dimensions, and runs test-only compilation/accessibility/readability smoke checks.
- Integrate disk bundle staging/promotion, idempotency, cancellation, restart recovery, privacy-safe receipts, and cleanup.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject empty selections, invalid page/grid/margins/cell geometry, unsupported child recipe, caption/metadata values above bounds, unsafe raw TeX/settings, duplicate/invalid paths, missing assets, reference/hash mismatch, validation/test-compile failure in CI fixtures, bundle limits, cancellation, and disk commit failure without publication.
- User metadata is always escaped; there is no “trust raw TeX” option.
- One item’s render failure follows explicit all-or-nothing/omit policy; default is fail complete with no partial visible project.
- Replacement preserves the previous complete bundle until promotion.
- Runtime does not claim the project compiles on an arbitrary user TeX distribution; the manifest records the tested package/compiler baseline.

## Test matrix

- Unit tests for settings/migrations, page/grid math, row/column order, child requests, every TeX escape, Unicode, paths, captions/metadata allowlist, renderer determinism, dangerous-command scanner, references, manifest, and limits.
- Fixtures cover varied page/grid/aspect/counts, Unicode and all TeX metacharacters, malicious-looking metadata, duplicate basenames, missing captions, themes, optional tables, and multiple child encoders.
- Test-only compilation runs with shell escape disabled and verifies page count, references, no missing assets/fonts/packages under the pinned environment, and rendered-page image placement.
- Fault-injection tests cover child render/template/write/validation/staging/promotion/restart/cleanup.
- Property tests prove identical input yields identical project bytes, all user text is escaped, no absolute/traversal path or dangerous primitive exists, stable order across retries, and zero/one complete visible bundle.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-export latex_destination` plus test-only TeX compilation and fault-injection suites pass.
- `cargo xtask latex-fixture --layouts all --verify-compile --verify-safety --repeat 2` produces layout, child-request, escape, compile, bundle, privacy, and commit receipts.
- Generated projects are deterministic, self-contained, safe with shell escape disabled, compile under the pinned baseline, contain exact referenced assets, and are published atomically without runtime TeX execution.

## Dependencies

Depends on #396, #397, #398, #413, #414, #415, and required child encoders.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the LaTeX project/contact-sheet destination, packaged templates/style, safe renderer, child-request expansion, validation/test compilation, atomic bundle commit, tests, and receipts. Do not implement runtime TeX/PDF compilation, print spooling, arbitrary user macros/templates, UI, or general document authoring.
issues.title: Implement LaTeX contact-sheet project destination

## Why

Move high-signal Rust failures into the local 60-second pre-commit tier while keeping exhaustive PR/main validation intact.

## What

- Run locked workspace check, all-target/all-feature warnings-denied Clippy, and a measured fast workspace test slice in parallel with deterministic repository and policy checks.
- Aggregate bounded labeled failure excerpts and retain process-tree cleanup on success, failure, interrupt, and timeout.
- Raise the pre-commit contract to 60 seconds while preserving 60-second pre-push and 150-second PR budgets.
- Add deterministic fixtures for labels, parallelism, failure aggregation, timeout behavior, and orphan cleanup.
- Update validation guidance and the task contract.

## Validation

- `bash scripts/test-precommit-fast.sh`

- `bash scripts/precommit-fast.sh` — 16s / 60s

Closes #405

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Deliver the production Iced 0.15.0-dev application shell that binds RustTable’s lifecycle and immutable view models to explicit multi-window state, abortable/progress-reporting UI tasks, subscriptions, persisted window placement, and the custom WGPU viewport boundary without moving domain behavior into widgets.

## Fixed decisions

- Use pinned Iced 0.15.0-dev.x with the function/builder-style `application`/`daemon` APIs, `Task`, `Subscription`, and the WGPU `Shader` widget. Do not build on deprecated `Command` APIs or a custom event loop.
- Use `iced::daemon` as the process-level host because RustTable may keep background imports/exports/camera jobs alive when ordinary windows close. Process exit is an explicit lifecycle command, not an implicit last-window close.
- Every open window has a stable `WindowId`, role, workspace identity, monitor identity, scale factor, bounds/state, focus owner, modal stack, and generation. Main library, darkroom, secondary preview, preferences, progress, and tool windows are explicit roles.
- Iced `Task`s are UI orchestration only. Long-running work is submitted to application services and observed through typed progress/event streams. Use abortable task handles and `Task::sip`/stream integration for UI-owned transient work such as debounced searches, previews, and dialogs; service jobs remain owned by [0024]/domain schedulers.
- Subscriptions are declarative and keyed by stable identity. Window, keyboard, pointer, timer, service-event, filesystem-notification, and device-change streams are deduplicated by subscription identity and cancelled when their owning state disappears.
- The image viewport uses Iced’s WGPU `Shader` widget with a RustTable-owned `Program`/`Primitive` that consumes textures and presentation receipts from `rusttable-render`. It does not compile processing shaders or own the WGPU device.
- `Task::abortable` cancellation is advisory and generation-checked. A late result from an old generation cannot mutate current window/workspace state.
- Multi-window boot presets are used in deterministic UI tests to reproduce window/monitor/layout state. Product startup does not depend on test presets.
- Accessibility names, roles, relationships, focus order, keyboard routing, high-DPI scaling, and reduced-motion/theme state are part of the shell contract from the first UI PR.

## Implementation

- Create `rusttable-ui` shell modules and the Iced entrypoint in `rusttable-app`; define `AppUiState`, `WindowState`, `WindowRole`, `WorkspaceState`, `UiMessage`, and pure update/view/subscription functions.
- Integrate the [0024] application lifecycle: boot only after required services are ready, expose degraded capability banners, request explicit shutdown, and keep the daemon alive while durable background jobs require it.
- Implement window open/close/focus/move/resize/scale-factor commands, persisted/restored placement with monitor-safe clamping, and deterministic fallback when a monitor disappears.
- Implement a typed bridge from service events/progress to Iced subscriptions with bounded coalescing/backpressure. The UI must not poll large job lists every frame.
- Implement a reusable abortable generation-task helper for debounced queries, thumbnail batches, color-managed preview requests, and other UI-transient work.
- Implement the custom shader viewport skeleton with texture generation checks, viewport/scissor information, resize, device-loss placeholder, and no CPU pixel copy when a compatible GPU texture is available.
- Add design tokens for spacing, typography, focus rings, motion, density, and system/light/dark theme selection without yet building complete product screens.
- Add architecture checks forbidding catalog/redb, processing operation, encoder, native-adapter, or raw WGPU device construction from UI modules.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Startup service failure shows a bounded fatal/recovery window or exits with a typed result; it never presents a half-connected product shell.
- Closing all windows while durable work continues leaves the daemon running according to explicit preference and exposes a platform status/notification path later; test mode can force exit.
- Late/duplicate task and subscription events are ignored by generation/identity. Cancellation never applies stale data.
- Invalid saved bounds, removed monitors, scale changes, or unavailable GPU presentation use deterministic safe placement/CPU-placeholder behavior.
- A viewport/device failure degrades only the affected presentation and reports the GPU fault state; it does not terminate catalog/export services.

## Test matrix

- Pure update tests cover every window role, open/close/focus, last-window behavior, explicit exit, degraded services, generation-stale results, task cancellation, subscription identity, and saved-layout restoration.
- Iced boot-preset tests cover one/multiple monitors, high-DPI, removed monitor, light/dark/system theme, reduced motion, accessibility tree basics, and deterministic multi-window state.
- Viewport tests use fake texture/presentation receipts for resize, stale generation, device loss, CPU placeholder, and zero-copy-compatible paths.
- Integration tests run the daemon with fake services and verify background work survives window closure, shutdown drains [0024] task groups, and no UI module opens storage/native/GPU services directly.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-ui -p rusttable-app iced_shell` passes.
- `cargo xtask ui-shell --presets all --verify-a11y --verify-window-lifecycle` emits window, task, subscription, viewport, and shutdown receipts.
- The shell opens/restores multiple windows deterministically, remains alive or exits only by explicit policy, ignores stale async results, presents a custom shader viewport boundary, and contains no product-domain implementation in widgets.

## Dependencies

Depends on #174, #177, #180, #290, #291, #292, #293, #294, #295, #296, #297, #298, #299, #300, and the processing/export service gates through #426.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the Iced application/daemon shell, window/runtime state, task/subscription bridge, shader viewport skeleton, design tokens, architecture checks, and tests. Do not implement lighttable, darkroom controls, masks, export screens, map/tethering/print/slideshow workflows, or complete visual design.
issues.title: Build the Iced 0.15-dev application shell and multi-window runtime

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Deliver RustTable’s versioned extension host using Wasmtime 46.x, the WebAssembly Component Model, WIT interfaces, and WASIp2 capability-scoped services. Extensions must be portable, resource-bounded, asynchronously cancellable, cacheable, and unable to access catalog, files, network, GPU, camera, or UI services without explicit grants.

## Fixed decisions

- Implement in `rusttable-plugin-api` and `rusttable-app` integration using Wasmtime’s component API. Unstable Rust dynamic-library/plugin ABIs and direct loading of extension native code are prohibited.
- Use WIT worlds and typed component bindings. Core Wasm modules and ad hoc JSON-over-stdin protocols are not the production extension ABI.
- Use WASIp2 only for explicitly granted standard capabilities. WASIp3 is experimental and excluded from the 1.0 contract.
- Compile components ahead of invocation and cache serialized compiled artifacts keyed by Wasmtime version, target, engine configuration, component bytes, WIT world version, and feature policy. Deserialization is allowed only for RustTable-created cache artifacts with verified provenance; untrusted precompiled artifacts are rejected.
- Each active extension invocation owns a short-lived `Store`. Stores are never reused across unrelated extensions and never accumulate unbounded instances.
- Apply `StoreLimitsBuilder`, bounded `ResourceTable`, fuel/epoch interruption, async-yield configuration, wall-clock deadlines, output/event limits, and host-call quotas. Default Wasmtime resource limits are not accepted as product policy.
- Use async component host functions and Tokio integration for cancellable I/O. Wasmtime does not own a separate application thread pool; execution remains under [0024] service task ownership.
- Evaluate pooling allocation only for repeated trusted component instantiation after benchmarks prove lower latency/memory on all supported platforms. Default first implementation uses on-demand allocation with explicit limits; pooling is an optional qualified tier, not an assumed optimization.
- Preopened directories use capability handles rooted in extension-owned storage or user-granted locations through `cap-std`/WASIp2. Ambient filesystem, environment, process spawning, inherited stdio, and unrestricted clocks/random/network are denied by default.
- Network capability is host-mediated by allowlisted endpoint/scheme/byte/time policies; raw socket access is not part of the 1.0 extension world.
- Traps, fuel exhaustion, deadline expiry, resource-limit failures, and guest panics are isolated to the invocation. Coredumps and detailed guest paths are disabled in production diagnostics unless an explicit developer mode stores them in a private support bundle.

## Implementation

- Define versioned WIT packages/worlds for extension metadata, initialization, commands, catalog queries over safe DTOs, selection snapshots, metadata proposals, export hooks, notifications, progress, extension storage, and capability requests.
- Generate Rust host/guest bindings and stable semantic version negotiation. Unknown required interfaces block activation; optional interfaces are discovered explicitly.
- Implement extension package manifest/signature/hash model, install registry, enable/disable state, compatible world range, requested permissions, storage quota, and compiled-artifact cache.
- Build `ExtensionStoreState` with `WasiCtx`, bounded `ResourceTable`, `StoreLimits`, cancellation/deadline/fuel state, deterministic/random policy, permission grants, and privacy-safe diagnostics context.
- Configure Wasmtime engine features minimally: component model/runtime/async/cache as required; disable proposals/features not needed by the WIT contract and record the exact engine configuration hash.
- Implement capability-scoped host resources for catalog snapshots, export-job proposals, UI notifications, and extension storage. Guest handles are typed component resources with parent/child lifetime rules.
- Implement invocation scheduling, concurrent-instance limits, cancellation, progress, trap classification, quarantine after repeated faults, cache invalidation, and startup reconciliation.
- Add an extension conformance kit with Rust guest examples, malformed/hostile components, WIT compatibility fixtures, and deterministic receipts.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject malformed packages/components, incompatible WIT worlds, unknown required permissions, unverified serialized artifacts, resource declarations above policy, unsupported Wasm proposals, and path/network grants outside capability roots.
- Fuel, epoch, wall-clock, memory, table, instance, resource-handle, output, host-call, and storage limits fail closed with stable codes.
- Cancellation stops host futures and interrupts guest execution; no late guest callback may mutate current state.
- A trapped/quarantined extension cannot crash or poison the application runtime, catalog transaction, export queue, or other extension stores.
- Extension upgrades are transactional. Existing storage remains versioned and is not destructively migrated without an explicit extension-owned migration invocation and backup.

## Test matrix

- Unit tests cover manifests, WIT/version negotiation, permission resolution, Store/ResourceTable limits, engine config/cache keys, fuel/epoch/deadline cancellation, typed resources, storage roots, and trap classification.
- Hostile fixtures attempt memory/table/instance/resource exhaustion, infinite loops, output floods, path traversal, ambient environment access, unauthorized network/socket/process access, forged handles, stale handles, and cache artifact substitution.
- Integration tests run multiple extensions concurrently, cancel/upgrade/quarantine them, restart the application, and prove catalog/export/service isolation.
- Benchmarks compare on-demand and pooling allocation for qualified workloads without making pooling a release requirement.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-plugin-api -p rusttable-app extension_host` passes.
- `cargo xtask extension-conformance --all-fixtures --verify-isolation --verify-limits` emits WIT, capability, store, resource, cache, trap, and cancellation receipts.
- Extensions execute only through typed WIT capabilities, remain within explicit limits, cannot obtain ambient authority, and cannot destabilize RustTable when malformed, slow, or hostile.

## Dependencies

Depends on #168, #174, #180, #396, #397, #398, and #427, #428, #429, #430, #431, #432, #433, #434, #435, #436, #437, #438, #439, #440, #441, #442, #443, #444, #445, #446, #447, #448, #449, #450, #451, #452, #453, #454, #455, #456, #457, #458, #459, #460, #461.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the Wasmtime engine/store host, WIT base worlds, package/capability model, compiled cache, limits/isolation, conformance fixtures, tests, and receipts. Do not implement a public extension marketplace, arbitrary UI embedding, native extensions, broad extension APIs, or Lua compatibility.
issues.title: Implement the Wasmtime Component Model extension host

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Deliver RustTable’s Lua-compatible scripting host using pinned mlua with Lua 5.4, typed Rust-owned objects, asynchronous host calls, serde-backed DTO conversion, deterministic event delivery, explicit permissions, instruction/memory/time limits, and complete isolation from native pointers and internal service types.

## Fixed decisions

- Implement in `rusttable-scripting` using pinned `mlua` with Lua 5.4, `async`, `serialize`, and required vendored/system feature policy fixed by packaging. LuaJIT and multiple selectable Lua engines are outside 1.0.
- The native Lua runtime is contained in the approved `rusttable-lua-native` boundary only where mlua’s selected build requires it; product scripting APIs remain safe Rust types.
- Scripts run in per-script Lua states or a rigorously isolated state pool. Unrelated scripts never share globals, registries, package loaders, mutable modules, or userdata handles.
- Use `mlua` async functions/threads to bridge host futures to Tokio-owned service tasks. Lua execution never blocks Tokio worker threads; CPU-heavy pure-Lua work runs on a bounded scripting executor and is preempted by instruction hooks/deadlines.
- Use `LuaSerdeExt` only for bounded, versioned DTOs. Arbitrary internal Rust structs, redb values, WGPU handles, catalog transactions, paths, secrets, or native handles are never serialized into Lua.
- The standard library allowlist is explicit. `io`, unrestricted `os`, `package.loadlib`, native module loading, shell execution, environment enumeration, debug mutation, and arbitrary filesystem/network access are denied by default.
- Require explicit capabilities for catalog read snapshots, metadata/edit command proposals, selection, export queue submission, notifications, extension storage, and approved network/file roots. Scripts submit commands through the same validated application ports as UI/CLI.
- Script callbacks receive immutable snapshot/event DTOs and return proposals/results. They cannot retain live catalog locks or mutate objects through interior references.
- Apply instruction, wall-clock, memory, recursion, stack, result-size, event-queue, concurrent-call, and storage quotas. Limits are per script and invocation, not process-global.
- Event delivery is ordered by durable application event sequence, with bounded coalescing for high-frequency preview/progress events. Reentrant delivery is prohibited unless the API explicitly documents it.
- Panic/unwind and Lua errors are contained. Repeated faults quarantine the script without affecting other scripts or the application.

## Implementation

- Define versioned script manifest, API compatibility range, permissions, settings, storage identity, enabled/quarantined state, and canonical hashes.
- Build `LuaHost`, per-script state/executor, safe standard-library setup, module searcher for packaged RustTable modules only, instruction/deadline hooks, allocator/memory accounting, cancellation, and error classification.
- Define typed Lua-facing modules for application/version, catalog snapshot queries, selections, metadata values, edit/style/export command proposals, jobs/progress, notifications, script storage, and event subscriptions.
- Implement async function adapters with generation/cancellation checks and bounded serde conversion. Late futures cannot call into a replaced/reloaded script state.
- Implement script install/load/reload/disable/quarantine, version negotiation, transactional storage migration, and startup reconciliation.
- Implement deterministic event subscription/filtering/ordering, callback deadlines, backpressure, missed-event findings, and no recursive callback entry.
- Add a conformance testkit with representative darktable-compatible script patterns where feasible, hostile scripts, API golden fixtures, and privacy-safe receipts.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject malformed manifests/scripts, incompatible API ranges, denied libraries/modules, unknown required permissions, oversized source/bytecode, and unsafe package search paths.
- Instruction/deadline/memory/stack/queue/storage limits fail closed with stable error codes and release all pending host futures/resources.
- Script bytecode is not accepted as portable trusted input unless produced and signed by the exact packaged Lua build; source is the default durable form.
- Reload/disable cancels pending calls and increments the script generation. Late results/events are discarded.
- Script errors, panics, or quarantine never roll back unrelated committed application work or leave a catalog transaction open.
- Diagnostics redact source paths, metadata values, credentials, and user content; developer mode may attach bounded script traces to a private support bundle.

## Test matrix

- Unit tests cover manifests/version negotiation, library allowlist, module loading, every permission, serde DTO bounds, async calls/cancellation, instruction/deadline/memory/stack limits, event order/coalescing, storage migration, reload generations, quarantine, and redaction.
- Hostile fixtures attempt shell/native loading, path traversal, environment/secrets access, infinite loops, recursion, allocation floods, callback recursion, event floods, stale userdata/futures, and malformed serialized values.
- Integration tests run multiple scripts concurrently, submit validated commands/jobs, reload/disable/quarantine them, restart the app, and prove isolation from Wasmtime extensions and product services.
- Compatibility fixtures cover selected darktable Lua workflows only where the RustTable API intentionally supports them; unsupported APIs return explicit findings.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-scripting -p rusttable-app lua_host` passes.
- `cargo xtask lua-conformance --all-fixtures --verify-isolation --verify-limits --verify-events` emits API, permission, limit, async, event, storage, and quarantine receipts.
- Scripts can perform supported asynchronous workflows through typed APIs, cannot obtain ambient/native authority, respect all quotas, and cannot destabilize RustTable or another script.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0012], [0018], [0024], [0236]–[0238], [0266]–[0300], and [0301] for shared capability terminology only.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the mlua/Lua 5.4 host, base typed API modules, permissions/limits, async/event/storage lifecycle, conformance fixtures, tests, and receipts. Do not implement every darktable Lua API, arbitrary native modules, script marketplace/distribution, script-authored Iced widgets, or Wasmtime components.
issues.title: Implement the bounded Lua 5.4 compatibility host with mlua

issues.body:
## Why

Fixture inputs need deterministic identity, bounded resource use, privacy-safe diagnostics, and complete registration before they can support reliable RustTable tests. This implements issue #162 without adding licensing or attribution gates.

## What

- Adds `rusttable-testkit::fixtures` with typed TOML manifests, portable/canonical path resolution, SHA-256 and exact-size checks, governed-root registration checks, hidden-file rejection, duplicate-content detection, deterministic listing, and local external-cache verification.
- Adds bounded gzip/ZIP preflight checks for decompression size and compression ratio before downstream inspection.
- Adds value-free deterministic privacy scanning for EXIF/TIFF strings, XMP, IPTC, JPEG comments, PNG text chunks, and filename/path leaks, with explicit allowed field paths for intentional metadata fixtures.
- Registers the existing RustTable image-IO fixtures in `fixtures/manifest.toml` and adds the external-cache manifest schema.
- Does not add `xtask` commands because this branch has no xtask architecture; the library API is available immediately as requested.

## Validation

- `cargo test -p rusttable-testkit --all-targets --all-features --locked`
- `cargo clippy -p rusttable-testkit --all-targets --all-features --locked -- -D warnings`
- `scripts/precommit-fast.sh` (16s)
- `scripts/prepush-fast.sh` (29s)
- `bun run check:workspace-layout`
- `scripts/main-ci.sh` started; doctests and release tests passed through the observed portion, then the unrelated exhaustive run was stopped to prioritize publication.
- All handwritten Rust files remain below 1,000 lines.

Closes #162

issues.body:
## Why

RustTable needs an executable, reviewable feature-parity inventory derived from the pinned darktable reference tree so new or removed registrations cannot silently escape the rewrite plan.

## What

- Add the reusable `rusttable-parity` scanner library and temporary `rusttable-parity scan-darktable` CLI.
- Discover registered IOPs, libraries, views, image formats, storage modules, Lua initializers, build options, and OpenCL programs without compiling or copying native sources.
- Generate normalized `architecture/darktable-capabilities.toml` with stable IDs, categories, statuses, issue sequences, test evidence, redesign notes, and deterministic phase/milestone summaries.
- Add narrow reviewed cross-cutting overrides with source-path/reason validation.
- Fail closed for duplicate IDs, missing paths, invalid statuses, stale issue sequences, unmapped modules, unregistered OpenCL programs, and masking overrides.

## Validation

- `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
- `cargo check -p rusttable-parity --all-targets --all-features --locked`
- `cargo test -p rusttable-parity --all-targets --all-features --locked` (7 tests)

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Evidence

PR #424 passed the internal `pr-ci` validation budget in 127 seconds, but the hosted job completed in 151 seconds because `actions/cache` saved a 1.1 GB `target` cache during post-job cleanup. The repository contract is a hard 150-second PR ceiling.

## Goal

Make PR validation restore-only and consume the cache published by merge-to-main validation, so post-job cache writes cannot push a passing PR over the hosted budget.

## Required implementation

- Replace the PR workflow cache action with `actions/cache/restore`.
- Restore the latest compatible main-branch Rust cache using a stable `rust-main-*` key and restore prefix.
- Keep cache writes in the merge-to-main workflow only.
- Preserve exact toolchain/Bun pins, required check name, strict validation, and the 150-second hard budget.
- Add deterministic workflow fixtures proving PR validation has no cache-save path and the main workflow remains the cache writer.

## Acceptance criteria

- PR workflow cannot save or upload a Cargo/target cache.
- Main validation continues to publish the cache needed by later PRs.
- A cold-cache PR stays under 150 seconds in hosted validation, with measured evidence.
- Existing workflow policy and pre-commit checks pass.

## One-PR boundary

Only PR/main cache workflow policy, fixtures, and documentation updates required for this budget fix. Do not change product code or merge #424 as part of this issue.
issues.title: ci: keep pull-request validation under 150 seconds including cache cleanup

issues.body:
## Why

PR validation previously crossed the hard 150-second hosted budget when `actions/cache` uploaded the large Cargo/target cache during post-job cleanup. Main validation already owns the compatible cache publication, so PR validation should not write it.

## What

- Switch `rust-pr.yml` to pinned `actions/cache/restore` with the compatible `rust-main-*` key and restore prefix.
- Keep `rust-main.yml` as the cache writer.
- Add deterministic fixtures and policy checks proving PR has no cache-save path while main does.
- Run the cache policy through PR, pre-commit, and pre-push validation without changing product code or existing pins/check/timer contracts.

Closes #425

## Validation

- `bun run test:cache-workflow-policy`
- `bun run check:cache-workflow-policy`
- `bash scripts/test-pr-ci.sh`
- `bash scripts/test-precommit-fast.sh`
- `bash scripts/test-validation-budgets.sh`
- `bash scripts/precommit-fast.sh` — 23s/60s
- `bash scripts/prepush-fast.sh` — 23s/60s
- bounded local PR validation — 14s/150s
- `git diff --check`
issues.title: ci: keep pull-request validation under 150 seconds including cache cleanup

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Add reproducible compiler-channel qualification around the date-pinned Rust 1.98 beta baseline from [0018], so RustTable can refresh safely within the beta branch, detect upcoming nightly breakage, compare compiler numerics against Rust 1.97.1, and evaluate high-value nightly tools/features without allowing unstable behavior into product or package artifacts.

## Fixed decisions

- The authoritative product compiler is `primary-beta`: exact `beta-2026-07-18` from [0018]. It builds all main-branch, benchmark, release-candidate, and package artifacts until [0018B] promotes the project to Rust 1.98.0 stable.
- Track five distinct compiler identities:
  - `primary-beta`: exact date-pinned `beta-2026-07-18`, release line 1.98;
  - `rolling-beta`: the moving `beta` channel used only to qualify newer 1.98 beta branch snapshots before the stable release;
  - `previous-stable`: exact Rust 1.97.1 used for compiler-regression sentinels and source subsets that remain compatible, never for accepted product/package artifacts;
  - `current-nightly`: the moving Rust 1.99 nightly channel for early compiler, lint, trait-solver, rustdoc, and dependency breakage detection;
  - `pinned-nightly-lab`: an exact date+commit nightly used only by registered unstable-feature or dynamic-analysis laboratories, initially `nightly-2026-07-15`.
- `rolling-beta` and `current-nightly` are monitoring toolchains. Every run records complete `rustc -vV`, Cargo/rustfmt/Clippy versions, LLVM version, components, targets, and dist-manifest hashes so a failure can be reproduced by an archive pin.
- Refreshing `primary-beta` to a newer beta archive is allowed only through `xtask ecosystem channels refresh` and a focused PR that passes compiler sentinels, dependency resolution, full numerical parity, supported-platform builds, and package provenance. The moving alias is never written into `rust-toolchain.toml`.
- The Rust 1.97.1 lane does not define an MSRV or veto use of 1.98 APIs. It runs the compiler regression corpus, ABI/FFI sentinels, numerical fixtures, and any explicitly compatible core crates. Full-workspace stable compilation may use `--ignore-rust-version` only as a diagnostic and is not required once 1.98 APIs are adopted.
- `current-nightly` compiles the stable/beta product source without product feature gates. It detects future incompatibilities but does not authorize nightly APIs in product crates.
- `pinned-nightly-lab` may enable only gates listed in `quality/nightly-labs.toml`. The initial approved lab is `portable_simd` for [0133A]. Miri, sanitizers, `-Zub-checks`, and rustdoc JSON may also use this toolchain when an owning issue registers the exact command.
- No lab crate or unstable artifact is part of default workspace members, default features, release dependency closure, product build scripts/proc macros, generated production source, package examples, or package/debug-symbol artifacts.
- `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP` is prohibited for all values. Nightly experiments use an actual pinned nightly and explicit registered gates.
- Channel jobs are added to the existing merge workflow from [0014]. Do not create a third workflow, schedule, privileged trigger, or independent release path.
- `rolling-beta` runs the full stable product graph with no feature gates, focused tests, rustdoc, Clippy, dependency/architecture checks, compiler sentinels, and representative numerical/image corpus.
- `current-nightly` runs the same stable-source compatibility surface plus selected future-incompatibility lints. It never silently switches product source to nightly implementations.
- `previous-stable` runs the minimized LLVM regression, numerical/codegen/FFI sentinels, and a declared compatible crate subset; failures are classified as expected beta-baseline differences or genuine cross-compiler regressions.
- `pinned-nightly-lab` builds/tests only registered lab packages/targets and registered Miri/sanitizer/rustdoc-JSON commands.
- Compiler-channel exceptions require exact fingerprint, command/package, minimized reproducer or upstream issue, local owner, first-seen date, affected platforms, risk classification, and expiry.
- A `rolling-beta` exception expires within seven days or at the 1.98 stable release, whichever is sooner. A current-nightly exception expires within 30 days. A pinned-lab exception is not allowed: update, fix, or disable the lab through a dedicated issue.
- A newer beta regression does not automatically move the primary compiler backwards or forwards. `primary-beta` remains fixed until an explicit refresh PR succeeds.
- Promotion of beta-stable or nightly-lab code into product use requires the API to be available on the authoritative product compiler, [0018] to classify it as adopted, and all platform/parity/benchmark requirements to pass. Lab code is deleted or converted in the same promotion PR.

## Implementation

- Add `quality/rust-channels.toml` containing all five identities, archive/channel source, expected release line, stabilization date, required components/targets, commands, gating level, and update cadence.
- Add `quality/nightly-labs.toml` with lab ID, owner issue, feature gates, package/target, exact commands, outputs, platforms, resource limits, promotion/removal condition, and forbidden product edges.
- Add `quality/compiler-channel-exceptions.toml` with the bounded exception schema and expiry validation.
- Implement `xtask ecosystem channels verify` to resolve/validate each toolchain, capture fingerprints, verify graph isolation, reject unregistered features/flags/`RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`, execute channel-specific commands, normalize diagnostics, compare expiring exceptions, and emit canonical receipts.
- Implement `xtask ecosystem channels refresh` to resolve the current rolling beta/nightly, compare them with the pinned identities, ingest the reviewed releases.rs/official release-note snapshot from [0018], emit source/API/compiler/dependency/numerical deltas, and generate—but never apply—a proposed primary-beta pin update.
- A proposed beta refresh must run the Rust 1.97.1 LLVM regression reproducer, [0130] adversarial numerics, representative operation pipelines, native-adapter ABI tests, complete workspace tests/Clippy/rustdoc, offline source closure, and supported package builds before it is eligible.
- Add merge-workflow jobs for `rolling-beta`, `previous-stable-sentinels`, `current-nightly`, and `pinned-nightly-lab` using existing permissions/concurrency. Cache by exact compiler fingerprint, target, profile, and lockfile.
- Add package/release guards proving every accepted artifact was produced by `primary-beta` until [0018B] changes the authoritative identity and that no lab crate/runtime/metadata is present.
- Add issue-summary output suitable for filing focused local/upstream compiler findings without private paths, environment values, or unbounded logs.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Failure to resolve a moving channel is `ChannelUnavailable`, not a source regression. Bounded retry is allowed; the job never substitutes another channel while claiming success.
- Compiler ICE, miscompilation, numerical change, lint/source break, dependency incompatibility, tool regression, missing component, and archive mismatch are distinct finding classes.
- An expired, fingerprint-mismatched, unused, or overly broad exception fails verification.
- A lab crate or unstable flag becoming reachable from product/package metadata is an immediate hard failure.
- Moving-channel runs cannot mutate `Cargo.lock`, generated production source, fixture baselines, or the primary compiler pin.
- Channel receipts remap checkout paths and redact environment variables, tokens, and host-user details.
- If moving beta advances to the 1.99 line after 1.98 stabilizes, it is not used to refresh `primary-beta`; [0018B] first promotes to 1.98.0 stable and then updates channel roles explicitly.

## Test matrix

- Fixtures cover primary-beta success, newer-beta source break, newer-beta numerical regression, previous-stable expected API failure, nightly lint/trait-solver break, compiler ICE, unavailable channel, moving fingerprint, stale/unused exception, unregistered feature gate, hidden lab dependency, build-script/proc-macro leakage, `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`, encoded `-Z` flags, and wrong-compiler package provenance.
- Verify rolling failures reproduce from captured fingerprints and pinned-lab configurations are identical across reruns.
- Verify rolling beta/nightly compile the product source graph without enabling lab features.
- Verify previous-stable results cannot overwrite beta baselines or package artifacts.
- Verify channel refresh emits an explicit delta/proposal and never changes `rust-toolchain.toml`, `Cargo.lock`, or compiler baselines automatically.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask ecosystem channels verify --channel primary-beta` passes.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask ecosystem channels verify --channel rolling-beta --channel current-nightly` produces compatibility receipts.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask ecosystem channels verify --channel previous-stable` produces compiler-regression and numerical comparison receipts.
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask ecosystem channels verify --channel pinned-nightly-lab` invokes the exact pinned nightly and only registered labs.
- A deliberately linked lab crate, unstable product feature, `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`, moving-beta package, or 1.97.1-built package fails the provenance guard.
- Existing `.github/workflows/rust-main.yml` contains the channel jobs; workflow inventory and permissions remain unchanged.

## Dependencies

Depends on [0014], [0018], and [0020].

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements compiler-channel configuration, rolling-beta/stable/nightly comparison lanes, refresh/verification commands, exceptions, package guards, fixtures, and receipts. Do not change the primary beta pin automatically, promote to stable, implement portable SIMD itself, broadly update dependencies, add a workflow, or make moving-channel output durable product behavior.
issues.title: Add rolling beta and nightly comparison lanes with unstable-feature qualification

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Use the pinned nightly laboratory from [0018A] to implement and rigorously qualify `std::simd`/`core::simd` variants of RustTable’s shared CPU kernels against the production kernels built by the pinned Rust 1.98 beta, determining which kernels should migrate to portable SIMD after stabilization without making nightly Rust or unstable APIs part of product binaries.

## Fixed decisions

- This is a non-shipping qualification lab. Production remains on `beta-2026-07-18` with scalar, portable-Rust, x86_64 `std::arch`, and AArch64 `std::arch` implementations from [0131]–[0133].
- Use the exact `pinned-nightly-lab` toolchain from [0018A], initially `nightly-2026-07-15`, with only `#![feature(portable_simd)]`. Primitive `f16`, `allocator_api`, `float_algebraic`, unrelated gates, `RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP`, and nightly Cargo features are prohibited.
- Implement the lab under `labs/rusttable-portable-simd` or an equivalent isolated package excluded from default workspace members and every product/package dependency closure.
- The lab depends only on public/test contracts from `rusttable-simd` and `rusttable-testkit`; no product crate depends on the lab.
- The production scalar and architecture implementations used for comparison are built with the exact primary-beta compiler fingerprint. The lab receipt records both primary-beta and pinned-nightly fingerprints.
- Test these representative shared kernel classes:
  - RGBA pointwise affine/exposure-style transforms;
  - 3×3 and 3×4 matrix transforms;
  - one-dimensional LUT lookup with explicit interpolation;
  - mask multiply/add/min/max/invert/opacity;
  - copy/fill/alpha and finite-safe clamp helpers;
  - integer/float and `half::f16` storage conversion with `f32` arithmetic;
  - counter-based deterministic noise/hash lanes where integer SIMD semantics are exact;
  - separable-convolution inner loops where portable gather/shuffle support is sufficient.
- Do not force portable SIMD onto algorithms requiring expensive gathers, irregular control flow, architecture-specific approximations, or unsuitable operations. Classify those kernels with evidence.
- Vector widths are explicit variants (`f32x4`, `f32x8`, `f32x16` and corresponding integer/mask widths); the widest type is not assumed fastest.
- Portable-SIMD operations follow [0130] exactly. Use the reviewed min/max/select and FMA behavior and never call algebraic float methods.
- Canonical CPU arithmetic remains `f32`; primitive nightly `f16` is excluded. Stable `half::f16` values may be loaded/converted only as storage inputs.
- Tails use checked masked loads/stores or scalar fallback with no out-of-bounds speculative access. Alignment is never assumed merely from `Simd` preferred alignment.
- The portable-SIMD subnormal caveat is recorded. Qualification targets supported x86_64 and AArch64 systems and still measures subnormal behavior on each runner.
- A kernel receives exactly one classification:
  - `PromoteWhenStable`: parity passes, portable SIMD is at least 1.20× scalar on representative large buffers, and is no more than 10% slower than the accepted best architecture-specific implementation across x86_64/AArch64 geometric-mean scenarios;
  - `UsefulFallbackWhenStable`: it materially beats scalar on both architectures but remains more than 10% behind a specialized implementation;
  - `ArchitectureSpecificPreferred`: specialized intrinsics materially outperform it;
  - `ScalarPreferred`: setup, tail, code-size, or compiler behavior erases the benefit;
  - `BlockedByUnstableApi`: a required portable operation is absent or semantically unsuitable.
- Qualification does not modify production dispatch. Promotion requires portable SIMD to be available without feature gates on the authoritative product compiler, [0018] to classify it as adopted, and a separate one-PR migration that converts or deletes the lab implementation.
- A primary-beta refresh invalidates production benchmark/codegen comparisons until the lab is rerun or the receipt explicitly proves the compiler change cannot affect the compared kernels.

## Implementation

- Register the lab and exact feature gate in `quality/nightly-labs.toml`; add graph checks proving one-way lab→product test-contract dependencies only.
- Implement portable-SIMD variants through adapters over [0131] typed views/descriptors. Organize by kernel family and vector width; do not copy operation algorithms.
- Add compile-time API probes so nightly API drift produces one focused `PortableSimdApiDrift` finding.
- Add parity harnesses consuming the complete [0130] adversarial vectors and [0131] randomized/layout/tail fixtures. Compare primary-beta scalar, portable-Rust, x86/AArch specialized, and each nightly portable-SIMD width.
- Add code-generation inspection using repository commands over MIR/LLVM/assembly. Record instruction families, vector widths, scalarization, gather/shuffle expansion, spills, masked-tail strategy, and code size without asserting fragile exact assembly.
- Add repository-standard benchmark scenarios for tiny tiles, interactive preview tiles, full scanlines, 24/45/100-megapixel buffers, aligned/unaligned rows, padded strides, and every tail remainder.
- Measure throughput, latency, p95, instructions/cycles where available, code size, thermal/downclock behavior for long runs, and compile time. Record host/OS/CPU state and compiler fingerprints.
- Generate `quality/portable-simd-qualification.json` containing primary-beta/nightly fingerprints, portable-SIMD API fingerprint, source/implementation hashes, platform/CPU, parity receipts, benchmark statistics, codegen observations, and fixed classification per kernel/width.
- Add drift comparison that updates the pinned nightly only through [0018A], reruns qualification, and highlights API/performance/parity changes.
- Add package guards proving lab rlibs, metadata, feature gates, source paths, compiler runtimes, and generated artifacts are absent from primary-beta product packages.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Nightly API breakage fails only the lab with `PortableSimdApiDrift`; it does not block ordinary primary-beta product builds or trigger bootstrap workarounds.
- A parity failure records exact kernel/width/input class and rejects that variant; tolerances are not widened.
- A benchmark without sufficient samples, controlled conditions, both supported CPU architectures, and complete compiler fingerprints cannot earn `PromoteWhenStable`.
- Unsupported operations fall back inside the lab only when the implementation identity records the fallback. Silent compiler scalarization is detected through codegen/performance evidence.
- Lab crashes, ICEs, sanitizer failures, or suspected miscompilations produce minimized upstream-ready fixtures and cannot publish product artifacts.
- Changing primary-beta or pinned-nightly fingerprints invalidates affected qualification until receipts are regenerated.

## Test matrix

- Run every kernel/width over aligned and relevant unaligned offsets, arbitrary/padded strides, odd/tiny/large dimensions, all tails, in-place/disjoint contracts, NaN/Inf/±0/subnormals, half-conversion boundaries, and deterministic integer-noise vectors.
- Compare debug/release, LTO/no-LTO, target baseline and qualified hosts, x86_64 Linux/Windows, Apple Silicon macOS, and AArch64 Linux where available.
- Property tests prove masked tails never access outside buffers, lane results match [0130], deterministic integer lanes are bit-identical, and repeated runs produce stable receipts.
- Mutation fixtures enable `float_algebraic`, primitive f16, an unregistered gate, lab→product dependency, assumed alignment, unchecked tails, widened tolerance, wrong primary-beta comparison artifacts, and package leakage; each fails.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo +nightly-2026-07-15 test --manifest-path labs/rusttable-portable-simd/Cargo.toml` passes under [0018A].
- `cargo +beta-2026-07-18 xtask cpu-kernels qualify-portable-simd --pinned-nightly --all-kernels --all-widths` emits parity, codegen, benchmark, compiler, and classification receipts for x86_64 and AArch64.
- Primary-beta package graph checks prove no lab dependency, nightly artifact, or unstable runtime is present.
- Every tested kernel has one fixed quantitative classification; no production dispatch behavior changes in this PR.

## Dependencies

Depends on #164, #166, #174, #427, and #286, #287, #288, #289.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the isolated portable-SIMD lab, representative shared-kernel variants, primary-beta comparison, parity/codegen/benchmark qualification, classifications, drift tooling, and package guards. Do not change the product compiler, modify production dispatch, replace stable architecture kernels, use nightly f16/allocator/fast-math features, or port operation-specific algorithms.

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome
Make `bun run install:computer-use` install exactly one canonical macOS app named `rusttable - latest`, with a stable bundle identity and deterministic destination. Re-running the installer must replace the existing canonical app and clean up duplicate legacy RustTable installs/registrations without deleting unrelated applications.

## Scope
- Use a dedicated app identity/display name for the latest RustTable Computer Use app.
- Replace the canonical bundle transactionally and safely on rerun.
- Discover and remove only repository-owned legacy bundles and stale LaunchServices registrations; prefer recoverable cleanup where appropriate.
- Add focused tests for naming, identity, rerun replacement, duplicate cleanup, stale registration removal, and refusal to mutate unrelated bundles.
- Update `AGENTS.md` with the canonical Computer Use install/replacement rule.

## Acceptance criteria
- `bun run install:computer-use` leaves one canonical app at its documented path.
- The app is displayed as `rusttable - latest` and has a stable RustTable-specific bundle identifier.
- A second run replaces the first app rather than creating another app registration.
- Existing duplicate RustTable bundles created by prior installer runs are cleaned up safely.
- `bun run test:computer-use` passes, including idempotence and safety regressions.
- One ready-for-review PR, squash-mergeable and eligible for auto-merge.

## Constraints
- Work from a dedicated worktree under `/Users/cgas/Documents/RustTable/worktrees`.
- Do not touch the darktable reference clone.
- Keep hand-written files at or below 1,000 lines.
issues.title: Make Computer Use installation idempotent and publish rusttable - latest

## Implementation

- Implemented cargo xtask repo verify-files with Git index mode inspection, stable path/rule diagnostics, UTF-8/EOL/NUL checks, NFC and case collision detection, binary short-circuiting, symlink rejection, and manifest escape checks.
- Wired repository-files into every existing validation surface and normalized only the governed files required for a clean checkout.
