Compiler baseline changes, broad legacy-tree renormalization, and unrelated migration work remain separate issues.

Closes #173
issues.title: [0017] Enforce cross-platform text, path, and executable-bit normalization

issues.body:
## Why
RustTable was still advertising and executing a Rust 1.95 baseline even though the migration plan now requires Rust 1.98. Pinning the verified dated beta makes local development, strict diagnostics, CI, artifact identity, and package validation converge on one reproducible compiler line.

## Implementation

- Pin `beta-2026-07-17` with the required strict-validation components and set the workspace MSRV to `1.98`.
- Replace Rust 1.95 CI/cache/artifact fixtures with the verified `1.98.0-beta.4` baseline.
- Record compiler, ecosystem, and dependency-source policy receipts.
- Adopt beta-compatible Clippy guidance (`as_chunks`/`as_chunks_mut`) and keep warnings denied through Cargo configuration.

## Validation

- `cargo +beta-2026-07-17 clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features --locked -- -D warnings`
- Full workspace all-target tests.
- `cargo xtask ci precommit`.
- `cargo xtask --format json ci prepush`.

## Out of scope

- Stable 1.98 promotion, rolling beta/nightly comparison lanes, portable-SIMD qualification, and broad framework migrations remain separate issues.

Closes #174
issues.title: [0018] Pin RustTable to Rust 1.98 beta

issues.body:
## Why
Computer Use needs one stable, discoverable RustTable app. The installer now publishes the exact `rusttable - latest` identity at `~/Applications/rusttable - latest.app`, replaces it transactionally on rerun, and cleans only repository-owned legacy duplicates and registrations.

## Implementation

- Adds the dedicated `com.cgasgarth.rusttable.latest` bundle identity and exact display/name metadata.
- Uses a deterministic canonical path and rejects path overrides.
- Stages and validates bundles before transactional replacement, with rollback if registration fails.
- Moves valid repository-owned legacy/duplicate bundles to recoverable user-trash cleanup, and unregisters only repository-scoped stale LaunchServices paths.
- Refuses unrelated, path-boundary, malformed, and symlink targets.
- Documents the contract in `AGENTS.md` and the README, with focused regression coverage.

## Validation

- `bun run test:computer-use` — 17 passed.
- `bun run test:macos-artifact-identity` — 22 passed.
- `bash scripts/test-pr-ci.sh` — passed.
- `bash scripts/prepush-fast.sh` — passed within 60 seconds.
- `git diff --check` — passed.

## Out of scope

- Product migration work, compiler-baseline changes, and unrelated packaging surfaces remain separate issues.

Closes #439
issues.title: Make Computer Use installation idempotent

issues.body:
## Why

Cargo manifests are not a sufficient architecture contract: aliases, target-specific dependencies, optional features, build dependencies, and dev dependencies can silently change ownership. This makes the committed workspace DAG fail closed against the actual locked graph.

## Implementation

- Added authoritative `architecture/workspace-dag.toml` with package roles, composition-root policy, explicit edges, and supported target platforms.
- Implemented `cargo xtask repo verify-dag` over locked default/all-feature, Linux-filtered, macOS-filtered, and feature-specific metadata contexts.
- Added stable JSON receipts for package inventory, allowed/discovered edges, feature context, violations, and topological order.
- Added deterministic fixtures/tests for renamed packages, aliases, target/build/dev edges, optional features, cycles, tooling leaks, reverse edges, and new packages.
- Replaced the partial crate-boundary checks and wired the authoritative verifier into precommit, prepush, PR, and main validation.

## Validation

- `cargo test -p xtask --all-targets --all-features --locked`
- `cargo clippy -p xtask --all-targets --all-features --locked -- -D warnings`
- `cargo xtask --format json repo verify-dag`
- `cargo xtask --format json ci precommit` (30s surface)
- `cargo xtask --format json ci prepush` (60s surface)
- `cargo xtask --format json ci pr` (150s surface)

## Out of scope

No compiler-baseline changes, crate moves, or product dependencies.

Closes #169
issues.title: [0013] Enforce the complete workspace dependency DAG against Cargo metadata

issues.body:
Parent: #158

Superseded by #452, which owns the complete validation-surface contract, including the authoritative 60-second pre-push budget, scheduling, timeout margin, platform filtering, prerequisites, severity, artifact verification, network enforcement, and process-tree cleanup.

This issue incorrectly specified a 150-second pre-push contract and would create overlapping validation ownership. No implementation should target this issue.
issues.title: Keep pre-push validation within the configured budget

issues.body:
Parent: #158
Issue: #444

## Why

The pre-push surface was organized around exhaustive all-target Rust checks and used a 60-second contract that was too tight for clean-cache contention. This change makes the requested 150-second (2.5-minute) pre-push contract reliable without weakening strict diagnostics or local technical coverage. PR validation remains technical/build/test/workflow focused; issue linkage and PR-body conventions are human/process guidance only.

## Implementation

- Set the validation contract and repository guidance to 30/150/150 seconds for pre-commit/pre-push/PR GHA.
- Keep formatting, policy, workspace metadata/DAG/layout, library check, warnings-denied library Clippy, and library tests on local pre-push and PR technical surfaces.
- Give pre-push/PR library checks a 90-second per-check cold-cache ceiling while preserving the 30-second pre-commit slice.
- Move exhaustive all-target/all-feature test and Clippy checks to merge-only `main`.
- Preserve strict locked Rust diagnostics, sorted deterministic receipts, sibling cancellation, and process-tree cleanup; add contract tests for budgets, ownership, merge-only placement, per-surface ceilings, receipt ordering, and cancellation.
- Remove only the GitHub Actions PR invocation of `verify-pr-contract`; the reusable manual xtask GitHub command, module, fixtures, and tests remain available.

## Validation

- Clean-target pre-push: 28.0s / 150s; warm pre-push: 5.1s / 150s on the technical change before the final manual-module restoration; the corrected branch also passes its clean rebuild and warm run.
- Warm pre-commit: 5.7s / 30s; PR-equivalent technical surface: 2.2s / 150s.
- `cargo test -p xtask --tests`: 40 unit tests and 4 CLI tests passed, including the restored manual GitHub contract fixtures.
- Budget helper, PR delegation/gate-absence, workflow policy, file policy, formatting, strict library check/Clippy/test, and staged diff checks passed.
- Exhaustive all-target/all-feature checks remain enforced on merge-only `main`.

## Out of scope

Product code, README/TASK budget edits, heavyweight packaging/corpus/benchmark validation, and any blocking GitHub check for issue linkage or PR-body structure.

Closes #444
issues.title: [0303] Keep pre-push validation within 150 seconds

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Replace sequence numbers embedded in issue and pull-request titles with mutable GitHub priority labels and explicit dependency links. After this PR, implementation order is determined by readiness and priority rather than a title prefix, so priority can change without renaming the backlog.

## Fixed decisions

- Use exactly one priority label on every open child issue under #158:
  - `priority: P0` — blocks further work or protects correctness/data integrity.
  - `priority: P1` — next critical-path work after all ready P0 issues.
  - `priority: P2` — core product implementation required for the target release.
  - `priority: P3` — product-completion work that consumes established core services.
  - `priority: P4` — release, optional integration, polish, or deferred work.
- Closed completed or superseded issues may retain the priority they had when active, but priority selection considers open issues only.
- Exact ordering within one priority is determined by explicit `Depends on #<issue>` links and then GitHub issue number as a deterministic tie-breaker. Titles never encode order.
- Issue titles contain only the capability or corrective outcome. Bracketed numeric sequence prefixes and suffix sequence variants are prohibited.
- PR titles contain the issue outcome and do not contain a sequence token. The PR body still contains exactly one canonical `Closes #<issue>` line.
- Branch names remain `issue-<issue-number>-<slug>` because the immutable GitHub issue number is sufficient identity.
- Milestones retain broad delivery grouping but do not imply priority.

## Implementation

- Rewrite the master issue #158 to define label-based priority, readiness, dependencies, phase gates, and one-issue/one-PR rules without sequence ranges.
- Update every child issue title under #158 to remove a leading `[NNNN]` or `[NNNNA]` token while preserving the remaining title exactly.
- Add exactly one priority label to every open child issue. Reject missing priority and multiple priority labels.
- Replace sequence references in dependency sections with direct GitHub issue references. Existing historical body references may remain only when clearly identified as legacy history; active dependency fields must use issue numbers.
- Replace generated capability and operation manifest ownership fields that store sequence strings with stable GitHub issue numbers.
- Update `xtask github verify-pr-contract` and its fixtures so it validates:
  - one open child issue under #158;
  - one milestone;
  - exactly one priority label;
  - no numeric title prefix;
  - PR title equal to or beginning with the normalized issue title;
  - branch identity by issue number;
  - exactly one canonical closing line;
  - no second issue-closing reference.
- Add a read-only queue verifier that fetches all open children and rejects duplicate active outcome titles, missing or multiple priority labels, invalid dependency references, dependency cycles, dependencies on open superseded issues, and P1–P4 work selected while a ready P0 issue exists.
- Emit a stable queue receipt containing issue number, title, milestone, priority, dependency set, readiness, and blocking reason. Do not create a second hand-maintained queue file.

## Failure and edge behavior

- GitHub API unavailability is an infrastructure failure, never a pass.
- An issue with no priority, two priority labels, a dependency cycle, a dependency outside the repository, or a numeric title prefix fails validation with the exact issue number and rule.
- A priority change is a label-only mutation and must not require issue or PR renaming.
- Closed completed dependencies satisfy readiness; closed `not planned` or duplicate dependencies do not unless the issue body identifies the completed replacement.
- Existing merged PR titles are historical and are not rewritten or used as active queue identity.

## Test matrix

- One valid issue/PR fixture for each priority.
- Missing, duplicate, unknown, and case-variant priority labels.
- Numeric and suffix-prefixed titles.
- Dependency chain, diamond, cycle, missing issue, cross-repository issue, completed dependency, not-planned dependency, and superseded replacement.
- Two open PRs targeting one issue.
- Two open issues with the same normalized outcome title.
- Priority change without title or branch change.
- Ready P0 blocking P1 selection and blocked P0 allowing the next ready priority.

## Acceptance evidence

- All open #158 children have exactly one valid priority label and no numeric title prefix.
- `cargo xtask github verify-pr-contract` accepts a real unnumbered test PR and rejects numbered-title fixtures.
- The queue receipt has no duplicate or unprioritized active issue.
- Current required PR check retains its name and read-only permissions.

## Dependencies

No product dependency. This issue blocks all new implementation PRs until the repository contract and backlog agree.

## One-PR boundary

Only backlog/PR governance, master-plan migration, issue-ownership identifiers, fixtures, and validation wiring. Do not implement product features or repair the substantive foundation defects tracked by separate corrective issues.
issues.title: Migrate backlog governance to priority labels and dependency links

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Replace the current image-difference implementation with a numerically correct, bounded, profile-explicit oracle that can safely gate RustTable-versus-darktable, CPU-versus-WGPU, tiled-versus-full-frame, and serialization parity.

## Fixed decisions

- The comparison API accepts images already converted into one explicit canonical comparison color space. It does not infer profiles or silently convert mismatched declarations.
- Canonical comparison buffers are linear-light `f32` RGBA in a named RGB color space with straight alpha. A separate normalization step converts encoded or premultiplied inputs before comparison.
- RGB and alpha are independent domains. Alpha is linear scalar coverage: never transfer-decoded, color-transformed, or divided by itself.
- The implementation retains the named classes `Exact`, `Transfer`, `Pointwise`, `Neighborhood`, and `LegacyGpu`; every built-in class must validate successfully.
- CIEDE2000 uses D50 Lab derived from the declared canonical RGB primaries and white point. Do not hard-code sRGB matrices while claiming arbitrary profiles.
- Neighborhood tolerance searches reference pixels within the configured radius for the minimum legal match. It is not implemented by inspecting whether neighboring source samples are finite.
- Report memory is bounded. Keep only the worst 32 outliers during traversal with a fixed-capacity top-K structure; do not collect and sort every failed pixel.
- Heatmap and blink artifacts are actual versioned image/artifact formats whose byte lengths and dimensions are mechanically validated.

## Implementation

- Replace ambiguous `ComparisonProfile` fields with a closed canonical profile identifier or a typed matrix/white-point descriptor supplied by the color subsystem.
- Add a normalization API that:
  - validates dimensions, channel count, stride, alpha mode, profile identity, and buffer length;
  - unpremultiplies RGB only;
  - defines zero-alpha RGB normalization deterministically;
  - decodes the declared transfer function only for RGB;
  - converts RGB to the canonical profile when an approved converter is explicitly supplied;
  - emits straight-alpha linear output.
- Make `compare` reject noncanonical or mismatched images rather than assuming sRGB.
- Define exact non-finite behavior:
  - matching same-sign infinities may compare equal only when the policy explicitly permits it;
  - NaN never compares equal;
  - any finite/non-finite location mismatch fails;
  - non-finite positions are counted and reported deterministically.
- Implement per-pixel absolute/relative error with alpha weighted independently from RGB.
- Define RMSE channel weighting and PSNR peak. For extended-range/HDR data, report PSNR only when the policy supplies a finite peak; otherwise emit `None` rather than a misleading value based on peak 1.0.
- Implement CIEDE2000 from normalized linear RGB using independently verified formulas and known-answer vectors.
- Implement directional or symmetric neighborhood matching explicitly in the policy. For this project use symmetric matching: source-to-reference and reference-to-source must both satisfy the radius/tolerance contract.
- Implement fixed-capacity top-32 outlier retention with deterministic severity and coordinate tie-breaking.
- Define artifact schemas:
  - heatmap: full-dimension RGBA8 pixels, row-major, one severity mapping documented and tested;
  - blink: a manifest plus two canonical image planes, or two separately named image artifacts; never concatenate unframed raw buffers.
- Increment the diff receipt schema version and provide a fail-closed decoder for prior receipts; prior receipts cannot satisfy new parity gates.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Dimension, channel, stride, alpha-mode, canonical-profile, and buffer-length mismatches are typed errors, not metric failures.
- Integer overflow in dimensions or artifact allocation fails before allocation.
- A neighborhood radius above the bounded maximum is rejected.
- Artifact generation failure cannot turn a failed comparison into a pass; metrics remain available with an explicit artifact failure.
- Comparison result is deterministic across thread counts and tile traversal orders.

## Test matrix

- All built-in policies validate and round-trip through stable serialization.
- Exact identity for linear straight-alpha values, including negative and values above 1.0.
- Alpha-only changes for straight and premultiplied inputs, including alpha 0, subnormal alpha, and alpha 1.
- Premultiplied RGB normalization with zero alpha and differing hidden RGB.
- Linear, sRGB, gamma, and HDR transfer fixtures with explicit normalization.
- Profile mismatch rejection and explicit conversion to the same canonical profile.
- Published CIEDE2000 known-answer vectors from an independent source.
- One-pixel, edge, corner, tile-boundary, and one-pixel spatial-shift neighborhood cases.
- NaN, positive/negative infinity, finite/non-finite mismatch, and signed zero.
- More than one million failing pixels while proving retained outlier storage never exceeds 32.
- Heatmap byte length equals `width * height * 4`; blink artifacts parse through their own schema.
- Symmetry, deterministic ordering, monotonic error growth, and tiled/full traversal equivalence.

## Acceptance evidence

- Independent known-answer tests pass without using the implementation under test to generate expected values.
- Memory instrumentation or a bounded-container invariant proves outlier memory is constant.
- Existing RustTable/darktable and CPU/WGPU parity callers use the corrected API.
- No current schema-v1 diff receipt is accepted as completion evidence by parity gates.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446. This issue must complete before operation, GPU, tiling, or codec parity claims are accepted.

## One-PR boundary

Only image normalization/comparison types, algorithms, artifacts, receipt migration, callers, and tests. Do not implement image operations, WGPU kernels, or general color-management features.

## Post-completion audit

PR #464 materially corrected the original alpha/profile/neighborhood/top-K defects, but the current schema-2 code still applies `alpha_weight` twice to RMSE, accepts invalid gamma declarations that normalize to NaN, and serializes complete heatmap/blink image planes inside the durable JSON receipt. #516 is the required corrective implementation. #494, #495, #496, and all new parity evidence must use the schema-3 result from #516 rather than treating schema 2 as final.
issues.title: Correct the differential image oracle for alpha, profiles, neighborhoods, and bounded reports

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Produce a compatibility manifest that records only source-evidenced darktable operation identities, parameter layouts, migration paths, processing contracts, presets, and GPU references. The manifest must be reliable enough to drive persisted-history decoding and operation acceptance tests.

## Fixed decisions

- The existing text scanner is retained only as a discovery aid. It cannot invent persisted versions, migration edges, parameter sizes, color spaces, tiling behavior, mask support, preset identities, or GPU programs.
- The pinned darktable source commit and the reference executable identity are one shared reference identity. Structural and behavioral evidence may not describe different commits.
- Current C/C++ parameter layouts are extracted from a target-specific preprocessed/AST representation. Use Clang AST JSON or an equivalently exact parser; regular-expression/source-text hashing is not a layout identity.
- Historical parameter layouts and migration edges are recorded only when evidenced by historical structs, `legacy_params` branches, persisted fixtures, or reviewed overrides with exact source citations.
- A parameterized operation with unknown size/layout is explicitly `unresolved-blocking`; zero byte size is valid only for a proven zero-parameter operation.
- Operation ownership uses immutable GitHub issue numbers, not title sequence tokens.
- Every override includes operation, field, exact source commit/path/line or fixture ID, reason, reviewer, and evidence hash. Overrides cannot merely replace inconvenient generated values.

## Implementation

- Introduce a versioned reference identity shared with the reference runner: source commit, build version, executable hash, data bundle hash, target triple, C ABI model, and build-option hash.
- Discover the complete registered IOP set from the actual build/registration graph, including conditional registrations under the accepted reference build options.
- Preprocess each operation translation unit with the accepted reference compile definitions and include paths.
- Extract for the current parameter type:
  - field name, declared C type, enum identity/numeric values, array length, offset, size, alignment, padding ranges, total size, and target ABI;
  - module version and introspection type;
  - whether the operation truly has no parameters.
- Generate a layout identity from the canonical field/offset/size/alignment/enum representation, never from unrelated source text.
- Extract historical layouts from explicit versioned structs, generated introspection metadata, and reviewed fixture decoders. Do not create `1..=current` entries automatically.
- Parse `legacy_params` control flow or reviewed migration declarations to record only actual accepted source versions and actual destination versions. Include whether the migration is direct, chained, lossy, or opaque-preserving.
- Extract operation flags from the operation callback/registration implementation, including one/multi-instance, style inclusion, blending, raster-mask behavior, tiling, fast-pipe, distortion, and ROI requirements.
- Determine input/output color spaces by evaluating the declared callback contract for representative parameter/config states. Record conditional behavior explicitly; do not search for nonexistent token names.
- Determine ROI and tiling behavior from callback presence plus reviewed semantics: identity, crop/reduce, expand/overlap, full-image analysis, distortion, scale-changing, or non-tileable.
- Extract CPU entry points and OpenCL references from actual kernel creation and program registry calls. Validate every program against `programs.conf` and every kernel against the referenced source; type names and comments cannot become programs.
- Extract presets as stable records containing preset identity, localized/display key, parameter version, exact parameter bytes or canonical values, enabled state, and source evidence. Source lines containing the word `preset` are not preset records.
- Record exact history/XMP/database operation, instance, order, blend, and parameter fields with schema/source evidence.
- Increment the manifest schema and mark the prior heuristic schema ineligible for compatibility completion.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Missing compile commands, unresolved macros, unsupported target ABI, ambiguous parameter type, unknown enum layout, or conditional behavior without evidence creates an explicit blocking finding and fails the foundation gate.
- Generated output fails when a discovered operation lacks an entry, a parameterized current version has zero size, a historical version lacks evidence, a migration is synthesized, a GPU reference is unknown, or an ownership issue does not exist.
- The generator never executes product code and never mutates the reference tree.
- Output is byte-deterministic for one reference identity and target.

## Test matrix

- Real darktable golden cases for:
  - proven zero-parameter operation;
  - simple scalar parameters;
  - enums and arrays;
  - multiple historical structs;
  - custom `legacy_params` branching;
  - direct and chained migrations;
  - multi-instance and mask-supporting operation;
  - conditional color-space callback;
  - ROI-expanding neighborhood operation;
  - geometric distortion/scale operation;
  - shared OpenCL program with multiple kernels;
  - presets with exact parameter payloads.
- Synthetic ABI fixtures for padding, alignment, enum representation, arrays, bitfields if present, and target differences.
- Mutation tests that alter a field offset, enum value, migration branch, operation flag, kernel name, or registration and prove a deterministic manifest change/failure.
- Negative tests for zero-sized parameterized operations, fabricated versions, source-code hashes presented as layout hashes, comments interpreted as presets, and type names interpreted as kernels/programs.

## Acceptance evidence

- Every operation in the pinned accepted reference build has exactly one entry.
- Every current parameterized operation has nonzero exact size and canonical field layout.
- Every recorded historical version and migration has a source/fixture evidence reference.
- The manifest and reference CLI runner report the same reference identity.
- A manual audit sample across all operation classes agrees field-for-field with the AST output.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446 and the shared-reference-identity corrective issue. Blocks persisted-history migration and every operation parity issue.

## One-PR boundary

Only reference preprocessing/AST extraction, manifest schema/data, evidence-backed overrides, validation, and tests. Do not implement Rust image operations or migrate user catalogs.

## Post-completion audit

PR #476 established useful Clang/AST layout infrastructure, but the generated schema-2 manifest is not complete acceptance evidence. It reports a darktable commit different from #449, covers only `aarch64-apple-darwin`, leaves executable current parameter versions opaque, retains unknown color spaces, records contradictory blending semantics, and accepts at least one C type name as an OpenCL program. #495 is the required semantic/decoder/multi-ABI correction. Persisted-history migration and operation acceptance must use #495 output, not treat #448 closure as sufficient.
issues.title: Replace the heuristic operation manifest with evidence-backed compatibility data

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement one fail-closed identity and resolution contract for the external darktable source tree, `darktable-cli` executable, data directory, OpenCL bundle, build configuration, target, native-library closure, and CLI argument surface. This PR establishes how a reference is described and verified locally; #494 provisions and qualifies the real bundle and executes required renders.

## Fixed decisions

- Store the canonical schema at `fixtures/reference/darktable.toml` and use the same typed identity in capability scanning, operation scanning, reference execution, corpus receipts, and differential evidence.
- The selected reference version/commit is `5.7.0` / `cfe57f3bbf5269bfacf31e832267279caa6938ad` unless this issue is explicitly amended before merge.
- Identity fields include source commit, build version, executable SHA-256/size, data-tree hash, OpenCL-bundle hash, target triple/architecture, build-option hash, compiler identity, native-library identity, normalized-log ruleset, and CLI command-contract hash.
- Source, executable, data, and kernel paths are resolution inputs only. Durable receipts contain stable aliases and hashes, not absolute developer paths.
- Resolution is explicit and ordered: command arguments/configuration, controlled external fixture cache, then a configured developer reference root. Unqualified PATH lookup and implicit system data directories are prohibited.
- A template or fixture may represent `UnprovisionedReference`, but it must be typed as unavailable and cannot pass identity verification, scanner generation, differential tests, or the foundation gate. Zero hashes, `unprovisioned-*`, and `local-source` are never qualified values.
- Structural scanners must verify source HEAD against the identity before reading registrations or layouts. Behavioral runners must verify the executable and data bundle before spawning.
- CLI flags and positional argument order are represented by a versioned command contract derived from the pinned executable’s help/man-page evidence. Callers do not assemble ad hoc argument sequences.
- CPU mode explicitly disables OpenCL. GPU mode explicitly requests OpenCL and records requested versus observed backend; actual GPU qualification is performed by #494.
- The resolver and runner remain test/tooling-only. Product crates cannot depend on, invoke, or feature-gate them.

## Implementation

- Replace independent scanner/runner pins with shared `ReferenceIdentity`, `ReferenceLocation`, `ReferenceResolution`, `ReferenceAvailability`, and `ReferenceCommandContract` types.
- Add canonical serialization, content hashing, schema validation, and privacy-safe receipt projection.
- Implement source-tree verification, executable hashing/probing, bounded data/OpenCL tree hashing, build/native identity comparison, and exact target validation.
- Implement explicit resolution from configured locations with typed unavailable reasons. The default repository checkout may remain unprovisioned, but every command must fail closed rather than fabricating hashes.
- Make capability and operation scanners consume the identity and reject a different source commit or build configuration.
- Make the reference runner consume the same identity, validate the command contract, build isolated HOME/XDG/library/cache/output roots, and delegate process ownership to #452.
- Normalize logs through a versioned ruleset and redact path roots using stable aliases.
- Add architecture tests proving no product package depends on reference modules.
- Add `cargo xtask reference inspect`, `verify-identity`, and `resolve` commands. `verify-identity` succeeds only for a fully provisioned identity; `resolve` may return a typed unavailable result.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject unknown schema, zero/malformed hashes in a qualified identity, source/executable/data/OpenCL/build/native mismatch, unsupported target, unsupported CLI contract, mutable PATH-only resolution, absolute path in durable receipt, or product dependency leakage.
- Missing local assets return `UnprovisionedReference` with required component identities; they do not pass or silently skip a required qualification.
- A source tree and executable from different commits/builds cannot be combined even when version strings match.
- Hashing/probing is bounded and cancellation-safe. Temporary roots are removed after complete process cleanup.

## Test matrix

- Canonical identity round trip and hash stability.
- Source/executable/data/OpenCL/build/compiler/native/target drift independently and in combination.
- Configured path, external cache, missing asset, wrong artifact type, symlink/path escape, and PATH-only rejection.
- CLI contract parsing, unsupported flags, positional ordering, CPU/GPU request construction, and log/path normalization.
- Different checkout/temp roots produce identical durable receipts.
- Product-boundary tests prohibit reference dependencies and invocations.

## Acceptance evidence

- Capability and operation scanners consume exactly one shared identity type and reject a mismatched source tree.
- The runner refuses zero/unprovisioned identities and accepts a fully populated synthetic qualification fixture.
- `cargo xtask reference resolve` reports all missing components explicitly on an unprovisioned machine.
- No durable receipt contains an absolute source, XMP, configuration, cache, executable, or data path.
- Real bundle hashes and real CPU/OpenCL execution are intentionally owned by #494 and are not claimed by this issue.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446, #450, and #452.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements shared identity/resolution/command-contract models, scanner/runner integration, fail-closed probing, privacy-safe receipts, commands, and tests. Do not build or distribute the real darktable bundle, claim real CPU/OpenCL parity, or modify product code; those are owned by #494.
issues.title: Unify darktable source, executable, data, and CLI reference identity resolution

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Convert the current fixture corpus into an evidence-bearing test corpus in which every positive decoder, metadata, color, database, XMP, and compatibility cell references a structurally valid artifact that is accepted by the intended parser. Descriptor text remains available only as descriptor fixtures with non-container extensions.

## Fixed decisions

- A file extension never establishes fixture validity. Positive support requires parser acceptance plus asserted semantic properties.
- Every corpus artifact has one explicit class:
  - `descriptor` — structured metadata used only to test registry/policy plumbing;
  - `valid-binary` — a parseable, structurally valid positive input;
  - `malformed-binary` — a bounded mutation of a relevant valid container used to test rejection;
  - `external-required` — a hash-pinned real asset required in merge/corpus jobs;
  - `external-optional` — a hash-pinned asset used only in explicitly optional local experiments.
- Descriptor fixtures use `.toml`, `.json`, or `.fixture`; they may not masquerade as `.png`, `.jpg`, `.raw`, `.icc`, `.db`, `.xmp`, `.avif`, `.heif`, `.exr`, or another production container.
- Every `supported` corpus matrix cell references `valid-binary` or `external-required`. `descriptor` cannot satisfy decoder, metadata parser, color profile, database migration, XMP history, or reference-render acceptance.
- Malformed fixtures derive from an identified valid seed with one documented mutation. Plain arbitrary text with a target extension is not a malformed container unless the tested behavior is specifically signature rejection.
- Large real/vendor RAW assets remain outside ordinary clones, addressed by exact size and SHA-256, and are mandatory in merge corpus validation when their matrix cell is marked supported.

## Implementation

- Extend fixture and corpus schemas with artifact class, format/container identity, generator/source recipe, seed fixture for mutations, expected parser, expected semantic assertions, and external-required policy.
- Rename all current `RUSTTABLE-SYNTHETIC` descriptor files that use production extensions to descriptor extensions and update consumers that legitimately test registry metadata.
- Audit every positive matrix cell. Replace it with:
  - a minimal valid generated container committed to the repository when small and deterministic; or
  - a required external asset entry with exact provenance-neutral identity, size, and SHA-256.
- Produce small valid generated fixtures where practical for:
  - PNG 8/16-bit with alpha;
  - JPEG 8-bit;
  - TIFF strips/tiles, integer/float;
  - PPM/PGM/PFM;
  - RGBE;
  - WebP where a deterministic pure-Rust encoder is available;
  - ICC matrix and LUT profiles;
  - SQLite library/data databases with actual schemas;
  - XMP sidecars with valid RDF/XML and darktable history fields.
- Use required external fixtures for formats/cameras that cannot be represented credibly by tiny generated containers, including representative CR2/CR3, NEF, ARW, ORF/RW2, RAF, compressed DNG, AVIF/HEIF/JXL/OpenEXR/JPEG 2000 as needed by the accepted decoder stack.
- Add a fixture qualification registry that invokes the intended parser/probe for every `valid-binary` and validates:
  - container signature;
  - parser success;
  - dimensions and sample/channel layout;
  - bit depth/sample type;
  - alpha/orientation/CFA/compression as applicable;
  - expected metadata keys;
  - ICC/profile class and tag structure;
  - database/XMP schema/history contents.
- Add deterministic mutation generators for truncation, invalid offsets, dimensions overflow, duplicate/conflicting tags, decompression limits, invalid ICC tags, invalid XMP values, unsupported history versions, and database corruption. Record seed and mutation identity.
- Make merge corpus validation download/provision no network data implicitly. The job must receive the controlled external cache and fail with exact missing IDs when required assets are absent.
- Keep privacy scanning, checksums, exact sizes, decompression bounds, and duplicate-content detection.

## Failure and edge behavior

- A positive fixture with parser failure, mismatched magic, wrong semantic values, descriptor media type, or missing required external bytes fails validation.
- An unsupported matrix cell may have a malformed/signature fixture but cannot have a positive support fixture.
- External hashes and sizes are checked before parser invocation.
- Generators are deterministic and bounded; checked-in generated artifacts include generator identity and expected hash.
- Fixture diagnostics use IDs and relative aliases, never private absolute paths or embedded personal metadata.

## Test matrix

- Schema tests for every artifact class and invalid class/support combinations.
- Magic/extension mismatch, descriptor renamed to production extension, valid file renamed incorrectly, missing parser, wrong dimensions, wrong bit depth, wrong alpha, wrong CFA, wrong ICC class, invalid SQLite schema, and invalid XMP history.
- Valid minimal generated fixture for each supported small-container class.
- Required external asset present, missing, wrong size, wrong hash, duplicate bytes, and optional-local behavior.
- Mutation seed linkage and deterministic regeneration for every malformed class.
- Merge mode proves no supported cell is satisfied by `descriptor` or an absent external asset.

## Acceptance evidence

- Every supported matrix cell is parser-qualified and has actual semantic assertions.
- No production-extension fixture contains the `RUSTTABLE-SYNTHETIC` descriptor header.
- The pinned darktable CLI successfully consumes the selected real source and XMP fixtures used by the reference integration issue.
- The corpus report distinguishes committed valid binaries, malformed binaries, descriptors, and external assets.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446. Blocks #449, codec support claims, compatibility migrations, and operation parity.

## One-PR boundary

Only corpus/fixture schemas, renames, valid fixture generation/registration, controlled external identities, parser qualification, mutations, and tests. Do not implement production decoders beyond the existing parser calls needed to qualify fixtures.
issues.title: Replace descriptor placeholders with valid binary compatibility fixtures

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Correct the darktable capability inventory so every discovered capability maps to the exact GitHub issue or explicit unsupported decision that owns its implementation and acceptance evidence. Remove stale sequence strings, catch-all mappings, and category shortcuts that currently misassign work.

## Fixed decisions

- Capability ownership is stored as immutable GitHub issue numbers, not title sequence tokens.
- One canonical machine-readable issue index is derived from GitHub child issues under #158. The Rust scanner does not contain a second large hard-coded issue map.
- Every discovered capability has an explicit mapping. Catch-all assignment to a generic closure issue is prohibited for known registrations.
- A capability may map to multiple issues only when responsibilities are genuinely split; each mapping includes a role such as `domain`, `implementation`, `UI`, `compatibility`, `export`, or `acceptance`.
- Build options are classified by resulting product capability or intentional exclusion. They do not all map to repository-workflow policy.
- `required`, `redesigned`, and `unsupported` remain the only final states. `unsupported` requires a fixed product decision and a release-gate check that the capability is not advertised.
- The inventory scanner proves registration/discovery. Behavioral evidence is a separate explicit acceptance reference; `reference-scan:<kind>` alone is not behavioral proof.

## Implementation

- Replace the hard-coded `mapping.rs` issue-number/sequence tables with a generated issue index containing issue number, normalized title, milestone, priority, capability IDs, operation compatibility names, and evidence role.
- Add a structured `Capabilities` section to relevant issue bodies or a small GitHub-derived metadata convention that identifies owned capability IDs without relying on title parsing.
- Validate that every mapped issue:
  - exists in `cgasgarth/RustTable`;
  - is a child of #158;
  - is not an obsolete duplicate unless it points to an active replacement;
  - has a milestone and one priority label when open;
  - declares the referenced capability ID and role.
- Audit and correct all current mappings, including at minimum:
  - Lua APIs to the Lua compatibility host issues;
  - print, tethering, slideshow, map, and darkroom views to their actual service/UI issues;
  - build options such as AI, AVIF, HEIF, JXL, camera support, printing, scripting, OpenCL, colord, profiling, G’MIC, ImageMagick/GraphicsMagick, OpenEXR, and platform integrations;
  - storage modules, with Piwigo separate from static gallery;
  - `rasterfile` to the external raster-mask issue rather than overlay compositing;
  - `overlay`, `clipping`, `invert`, `clahe`, `defringe`, `censorize`, and every other registered IOP currently falling through a generic mapping.
- Create missing implementation issues in the same migration if a required discovered capability has no existing one-PR owner. Do not hide missing scope under a parity-closure issue.
- Record category, state, redesign decision, implementation issue role(s), exact reference path/symbol, structural evidence, required behavioral evidence, and final acceptance test ID.
- Add validation that prevents two unrelated capabilities from sharing an issue unless the issue body explicitly lists both and one PR can actually implement them coherently.
- Add a deterministic report of unmapped, multiply mapped, stale, unsupported, and evidence-incomplete capabilities.
- Increment the inventory schema and reject the prior sequence-based schema in release accounting.

## Failure and edge behavior

- A discovered capability without an explicit current issue mapping fails generation.
- A mapping to a missing, closed-not-planned without replacement, superseded, wrong-parent, or wrong-category issue fails.
- Unknown capability IDs in issue metadata fail to prevent stale typos.
- An override may add cross-cutting metadata but cannot mask a discovered registration or bypass issue validation.
- GitHub API unavailability is an infrastructure failure for live index generation; committed generated output remains deterministic and reviewable offline.

## Test matrix

- Valid one-to-one, one-to-many with roles, and explicitly unsupported mappings.
- Missing issue, wrong repository, wrong parent, missing milestone, missing priority, superseded issue, duplicate issue metadata, unknown capability ID, and generic catch-all.
- Fixtures for IOP, library, view, format, storage, Lua, build option, and OpenCL registration.
- Mutation tests adding/removing/renaming a reference registration and adding/removing/renaming issue capability ownership.
- Explicit regressions for every known bad mapping listed above.
- Report stability under issue-title priority changes, proving issue numbers and labels—not title prefixes—own identity.

## Acceptance evidence

- Every discovered capability has explicit issue ownership or an explicit unsupported decision.
- No known registration maps through a default/catch-all branch.
- Every issue reference resolves and declares the matching capability role.
- The inventory contains no four-digit sequence ownership strings.
- Missing required capabilities generate dedicated issues rather than being concealed.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446. Coordinate with #448 because operation ownership and operation names must agree.

## One-PR boundary

Only issue-index derivation, capability ownership metadata, mapping corrections, missing capability issue creation, inventory schema/validation, and tests. Do not implement the mapped product capabilities.

## Post-completion audit

PR #483 generated useful issue-index and capability-owner infrastructure, but its mutation step created generic placeholder issues and failed to recognize existing complete owners. It produced duplicate issues #471/#472/#474 for capabilities already owned by #380/#367/#375 and generic three-section bodies for several missing capabilities. Those duplicates are now closed as superseded, detailed replacements/owners were created or rewritten, and #492 is the required generator/reconciliation correction. Future automated capability issue creation must use #492 rather than treating #451 closure as proof that generated issue scope is complete.
issues.title: Make the darktable capability inventory use complete evidence-backed issue ownership

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Make the machine-readable validation contract executable rather than descriptive: platform selection, prerequisites, severity, network policy, artifacts, budgets, cancellation, output diagnostics, and complete process-tree cleanup must all be enforced consistently on local and hosted surfaces.

## Fixed decisions

- `quality/validation-surfaces.toml` remains the single declaration of repository checks.
- Supported platforms are explicit normalized values: `linux`, `macos`, `windows`, and `all`. A check not applicable to the current platform is omitted with reason `platform-not-selected`; its executable is not required to exist.
- Prerequisites form a directed acyclic graph. A check runs only after every prerequisite passes; failed or skipped prerequisites produce a typed dependent skip.
- `severity = error` fails the surface. `severity = warning` records a warning and permits completion unless the contract separately marks a warning budget.
- `network = none` is enforced, not documented. Cargo commands use offline mode and the process environment contains no proxy or credential variables. Other commands run through the approved network-isolation mechanism available on the host; when hard isolation is unavailable, the check cannot claim network enforcement.
- Declared artifacts are outputs, not labels. Required artifacts must exist, be newly produced by that check, stay within repository/temporary roots, satisfy size/type limits, and be recorded by hash.
- Fast-surface budgets include orchestration overhead. The configured worst case retains an explicit margin rather than exactly filling 30/60/150 seconds.
- One shared process runner owns subprocesses for xtask validation, reference execution, and benchmark setup. Unix uses a new process group/session; Windows uses a Job Object with kill-on-close. Polling `pgrep`, external `kill`, or `taskkill` is not the ownership model.
- Receipts use stable logical command IDs and redacted argument classes. Secret-bearing values and absolute private paths are never serialized.

## Implementation

- Extend contract validation with closed enums for surface, platform, network policy, severity, artifact policy, and execution class.
- Detect the current platform once and filter checks before command-availability validation and budget calculation.
- Build and validate a prerequisite DAG; reject missing nodes, self edges, cycles, cross-surface unavailable prerequisites, and dependencies that cannot run on all applicable platforms.
- Schedule ready checks in deterministic waves. Independent checks may run concurrently; checks sharing an exclusive resource such as Cargo target directory, package manager cache, GPU, or repository mutation run under explicit resource locks.
- Propagate sibling cancellation only for error-severity failures and only within the declared cancellation scope. Produce receipts for cancelled, skipped, warning-failed, error-failed, and passed checks.
- Enforce warning semantics separately from errors.
- Implement network policy:
  - strip proxy, token, credential, and package-registry environment variables unless explicitly allowlisted;
  - set Cargo offline/frozen behavior for network-none Cargo commands;
  - prohibit shell commands that can bypass a declared command wrapper;
  - use host sandbox/firewall facilities in merge jobs where available and record enforcement mode.
- Validate artifacts after successful command exit. Record relative alias, size, media/type class, SHA-256, and freshness marker. Missing/stale/out-of-root/oversized artifacts turn success into failure.
- Remove duplicated workflow invocations. Hosted workflows perform setup and call each declared surface exactly once; individual contract checks are not separately repeated in YAML.
- Either enforce the aggregate main budget or remove it and represent only per-check/job budgets. For this project enforce a 45-minute aggregate main contract in xtask and keep hosted job timeouts at or below it.
- Add a fixed safety margin: configured fast-surface critical-path timeouts plus 10%/5 seconds, whichever is larger, must remain below the surface budget.
- Improve failure receipts with bounded stdout/stderr excerpts, output hashes, exit status, timeout/cancellation reason, process-cleanup result, and artifact findings.
- Refactor `ProcessRequest` to start from an empty environment with explicit allowlists. Replace raw `program`/`args` receipt fields with command ID plus redacted argument descriptors.
- Implement Unix process-group ownership and TERM→bounded drain→KILL escalation. Implement Windows Job Object ownership and termination. Ensure descendants created after cancellation cannot escape.
- Add a bounded drain deadline after parent exit so inherited pipe handles cannot hang the runner.
- Use a real HTTP client or protected header mechanism for GitHub API calls; do not place bearer tokens in `curl` argv.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Unknown platform, network policy, severity, artifact policy, or resource class fails contract loading.
- Platform-inapplicable checks do not fail because their executable is absent.
- A warning failure is visible but does not silently become pass or error.
- A prerequisite failure never starts dependents.
- Network-none enforcement failure is itself a failure; the check cannot run with a weaker undocumented mode.
- Child/grandchild leaks, pipe drain timeout, inability to terminate a process group/job, or artifact validation failure are explicit failures.
- Receipts never contain repository absolute paths, HOME paths, tokens, cookies, authorization headers, or full inherited-environment hashes.

## Test matrix

- Platform filtering and missing platform-only executable on Linux, macOS, and Windows fixtures.
- Prerequisite chain, diamond, cycle, unavailable prerequisite, failure skip, and warning prerequisite.
- Warning versus error behavior and cancellation scope.
- Network environment stripping, Cargo offline enforcement, attempted network access, proxy variables, and missing sandbox enforcement.
- Artifact success, missing, stale, wrong hash/type, oversized, path escape, symlink, and multiple outputs.
- Duplicate workflow execution detection.
- Budget margin, orchestrator overhead, timeout, and main aggregate budget.
- Bounded stdout/stderr excerpts and output-limit failure.
- Unix child/grandchild/process-group escape, ignored TERM, pipe held open, and cancellation race.
- Windows Job Object child/grandchild, ignored termination, and handle-leak tests.
- GitHub authorization header absent from argv and receipts.

## Acceptance evidence

- Every field in `validation-surfaces.toml` has at least one mutation test proving it affects execution.
- Local and hosted surfaces invoke the same declared check set exactly once for the same platform.
- Required artifacts are verified and hashed.
- Process-leak tests pass on Linux, macOS, and Windows.
- Fast surfaces retain the required safety margin and complete within 30/60/150 seconds.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446. Blocks adding further validation gates and blocks acceptance of foundation receipts that rely on unenforced contract fields.

## One-PR boundary

Only validation contract/executor, process runner, GitHub request transport, wrappers/workflow deduplication, fixtures, and tests. Do not add new product tests, coverage policy, or performance thresholds.

## Post-completion audit

PR #484 did not satisfy this issue’s complete contract. Current `main` still ignores platform selection and warning severity during execution, does not enforce prerequisite result state across hosted PR groups, does not generically verify declared artifacts or network isolation, inherits the complete process environment, serializes raw argv/paths, uses `pgrep`/external `kill` on Unix, and has no Windows Job Object implementation. #515 is the required corrective implementation and #496 must reject this issue’s receipt as insufficient until #515 completes.
issues.title: Complete validation-surface execution semantics and cross-platform process ownership

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Complete the workspace dependency-DAG gate so it covers every supported platform, every relevant Cargo feature context, and both internal and external dependency ownership. Prevent product crates from bypassing architecture through target-specific, optional, build, dev, or third-party dependencies.

## Fixed decisions

- Supported product targets are sourced from one platform contract and initially include:
  - `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu`;
  - `aarch64-apple-darwin`;
  - `x86_64-pc-windows-msvc`.
- `architecture/workspace-dag.toml` owns internal package roles and local edges.
- A companion section in the same contract owns external dependencies by crate/package role. External dependencies are not invisible to architecture validation.
- Normal, build, dev, target-specific, optional, and activated feature dependencies are represented separately.
- Feature validation covers default, all-features, no-default-features, each declared singleton feature, and each explicitly declared interacting feature set. Merely running no-default-features once per package is insufficient.
- The composition root may depend on product services; product crates may never depend back on the application. Product crates may not depend on repository tooling.
- Runtime-owner restrictions are explicit:
  - Iced only in app/UI and approved presentation adapters;
  - Tokio only in app/orchestration/integration owners, not domain algorithms;
  - WGPU only in GPU/render/UI viewport owners;
  - redb only in catalog-store and approved migration tooling;
  - SQLite only in compatibility adapters and test tooling;
  - Wasmtime/mlua only in scripting/plugin hosts;
  - native adapters only in explicitly named adapter crates.

## Implementation

- Add one machine-readable platform-support contract and make workflows, DAG validation, package checks, and packaging derive their target lists from it.
- Add Windows metadata contexts to DAG validation.
- Extend the DAG contract with external dependency rules: exact package name, allowed source roles/packages, dependency kinds, targets, feature constraints, and whether transitive presence is allowed.
- Parse Cargo metadata package sources and resolved nodes so both workspace and external edges are discoverable.
- Reject undeclared direct external dependencies even when the local workspace edge set is unchanged.
- Add declared feature-set metadata per package. Generate contexts for:
  - default;
  - all features;
  - no default features;
  - each public feature individually;
  - each reviewed interacting combination;
  - each supported target where target filtering changes the graph.
- Detect aliases and renamed dependencies by resolved package identity, while preserving the manifest alias in diagnostics.
- Validate build scripts and proc-macro dependencies according to role; do not permit them to smuggle runtime-owner crates into domain packages.
- Validate dev-dependency ownership and integration-test placement. Test-only external dependencies still require an allowed owner.
- Extend receipts with target, feature set, source role, destination package/source, alias, dependency kind, optional activation, and exact rule matched.
- Add mutation fixtures for adding direct `redb`, `rusqlite`, `tokio`, `wgpu`, `iced`, `wasmtime`, `mlua`, and native `-sys` crates to disallowed packages.
- Replace broad claims such as “complete DAG” unless every supported target and declared feature context is actually represented.

## Failure and edge behavior

- A supported target missing from the DAG/platform check fails.
- New workspace or external dependencies fail until explicitly reviewed in the contract.
- Unknown Cargo features, stale declared feature sets, untested public features, and unsupported feature combinations fail validation.
- Target-specific dependencies are checked only where their target predicate is active but remain declared globally.
- Git/registry/path source changes for an external dependency are reported as provenance changes.
- Validation remains offline and consumes the locked graph.

## Test matrix

- Linux, macOS, and Windows target-filtered graphs.
- Default, no-default, all-feature, singleton-feature, and interacting-feature contexts.
- Renamed/aliased direct and transitive packages.
- Normal, build, dev, proc-macro, optional, target-specific, and feature-gated dependencies.
- Disallowed runtime-owner crates in domain packages.
- Approved dependencies in correct owner crates.
- New package, missing package, reverse edge, composition-root dependency, tooling leak, cycle, stale feature, and unknown target.
- External package source/provenance change and duplicate-major/minor lines where policy applies.

## Acceptance evidence

- Every supported product target appears in the receipt.
- Every declared public feature appears in at least one validation context.
- Direct external dependencies for every workspace package are either matched by an explicit rule or rejected.
- Mutation tests prove domain crates cannot add Iced, Tokio, WGPU, redb, SQLite, scripting runtimes, or native adapters without an approved ownership change.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446. Coordinate with dependency-baseline enforcement but retain the approved dated Rust beta toolchain.

## One-PR boundary

Only platform/DAG contracts, external dependency rules, feature-context generation, receipts, validation wiring, and tests. Do not move crates or add product dependencies except fixture-only mutations.
issues.title: Extend workspace architecture enforcement to Windows, external dependencies, and feature combinations

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Correct repository file normalization so pre-commit validation inspects the Git index rather than unrelated working-tree bytes, applies complete cross-platform path rules, and recognizes the file classes required by RustTable’s planned shaders, scripting, extensions, UI assets, packaging, and reports.

## Fixed decisions

- Pre-commit/index validation reads mode, path, and content from the exact staged Git index entry. Unstaged working-tree changes cannot make staged invalid content pass or staged valid content fail.
- Repository-wide/PR validation reads committed tree objects. A separate explicit working-tree mode may inspect local files for developer diagnostics, but receipts identify the source (`index`, `tree`, or `worktree`).
- Special filename allowlists match exact repository-relative paths or exact basenames in declared roots. `ends_with` matching is prohibited.
- Text policy is UTF-8 without BOM, LF only, no lone CR, and exactly one final LF unless the exact file class permits empty content.
- Windows portability rejects reserved basenames, trailing spaces/dots, control characters, and `< > : " / \\ | ? *`; path-length policy is measured in UTF-16 code units and bytes with explicit limits.
- Unicode path policy uses NFC plus a documented Unicode case-folding implementation appropriate to collision detection. ASCII lowercase is not sufficient for the claimed policy.
- Binary classification is not solely extension-based when magic/signature validation is available. Fixture class from #450 is authoritative for corpus assets.

## Implementation

- Refactor Git index parsing to retain blob object IDs and stages. Reject unmerged/non-stage-zero entries for governed paths.
- Read staged bytes through Git object access (`git cat-file --batch` or an equivalent library) and verify the object ID before inspection.
- Add committed-tree mode for CI and explicit worktree mode for developer diagnostics.
- Include source identity in every violation and receipt.
- Replace special-name suffix matching with exact matching.
- Detect CRLF and lone CR separately.
- Implement complete Windows-invalid-character/control/reserved-name checks per path component, including names before the first dot and alternate-data-stream colon cases.
- Define and test byte length, UTF-16 component length, and total Windows path length limits.
- Use a reviewed Unicode normalization and full/default case-folding library or generated table. Record Unicode version in the policy receipt.
- Add planned text/artifact classes now:
  - WGSL, WIT, WAT, Lua, SVG, HTML, CSS, JavaScript/TypeScript, LaTeX, desktop/app metadata, plist, XML, SQL, CSV, lock/config files, packaging manifests, and generated JSON/TOML receipts;
  - WASM and other binary extension artifacts;
  - shader reflection/binding artifacts;
  - font assets only when explicitly approved and registered, without distributing font files as debugging artifacts.
- Validate `.gitattributes` coverage against `quality/repository-files.toml` so policy classes cannot drift.
- Validate executable paths from actual tracked entries and reject undeclared executable scripts on every platform.
- Add bounded file-size checks by class and avoid loading arbitrarily large binary fixtures into memory when only metadata/magic is required.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Unmerged index entries, missing blobs, object hash mismatch, symlink/submodule entries, path decoding failure, or unsupported mode fail closed.
- Invalid staged bytes fail even if the worktree file is clean; valid staged bytes pass even if the worktree file is dirty.
- Unknown extensions/classes fail with exact path and suggested policy section.
- Generated files are still validated for encoding/path/mode and must carry their generated marker where required.
- The retained external darktable reference tree remains outside governed RustTable roots.

## Test matrix

- Staged/unstaged divergence in both directions.
- Index blob mutation, unmerged stages, symlink, submodule, mode drift, and missing blob.
- CRLF, lone CR, BOM, invalid UTF-8, NUL, zero/multiple final newlines.
- Exact special names versus misleading suffixes.
- Windows reserved names, invalid characters, controls, trailing spaces/dots, UTF-16 length, and case-insensitive collisions.
- NFC/NFD and non-ASCII case-fold collisions.
- Every planned text and binary class, unknown class, binary false-positive, and magic mismatch.
- `.gitattributes` and repository policy drift.
- Large fixture bounded-read behavior.

## Acceptance evidence

- A staged invalid fixture fails regardless of worktree state.
- Linux, macOS, and Windows fixture suites agree on the same logical violations.
- Every currently tracked governed file and every planned artifact class has an explicit policy.
- `.gitattributes` and repository policy are mechanically synchronized.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446 and should follow #452 so it uses the corrected validation/process contract.

## One-PR boundary

Only file-policy schema/checker, Git index/tree object reading, class inventory, attributes synchronization, fixtures, and validation wiring. Do not broadly renormalize unrelated retained reference data or implement product features.
issues.title: Make repository file policy inspect staged bytes and cover all supported artifact classes

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Correct benchmark identity so different RustTable commits can be compared on the same qualified host, and replace inactive 1×1 placeholder scenarios with real representative workloads before performance receipts are used for prioritization or regression gates.

## Fixed decisions

- Host identity and build identity are separate values.
- `HostIdentity` contains only comparison-environment properties: target triple, OS/kernel, CPU model/microcode/features, physical/logical core policy, memory, GPU adapter/driver/backend/features, thread count, power mode, and benchmark-runner version.
- `BuildIdentity` contains source commit, dirty-state prohibition, Rust compiler/LLVM identity, Cargo.lock hash, feature/profile flags, codegen units/LTO/panic mode, shader hash, and relevant dependency identities.
- Baseline eligibility requires matching scenario/workload identity and qualified host identity. Source commits are expected to differ and are recorded on both sides of the comparison.
- Placeholder scenarios cannot produce reviewed baselines or pass performance gates. Each scenario has an explicit state: `inactive-placeholder`, `qualification`, or `active`.
- Active image workloads use valid fixtures from #450 and verify expected output through the corrected image oracle #447 or exact hashes where appropriate.
- Percentiles are computed from measured repetitions after warmup and include p50/p95/p99. Regression policy is not based on one sample.
- The harness records uncertainty/noise and refuses pass/fail comparison when the environment is unstable or samples are insufficient.

## Implementation

- Split the current host fingerprint into versioned `HostIdentity`, `BuildIdentity`, and `RunEnvironment` values.
- Move source SHA and build profile out of host equality.
- Include both current and baseline build identities in comparison receipts.
- Add host qualification checks for CPU governor/power mode where available, thermal state, concurrent load, memory pressure, GPU adapter/driver, and timer resolution. Record unavailable checks explicitly.
- Define canonical scenario identity from fixture content hashes, dimensions, operations/parameters, thread count, memory cap, timeout, expected output identity, warmup/repetition policy, and harness schema.
- Replace or mark inactive every current 1×1/all-zero scenario.
- Activate representative scenarios with concrete sizes and fixtures:
  - catalog open/checkpoint with a real multi-record redb fixture;
  - import registration batch;
  - valid PNG/TIFF raster decode;
  - representative RAW decode after the decoder is available;
  - thumbnail generation at fixed source/output sizes;
  - minimal CPU pipeline on a megapixel-scale image;
  - WGPU pipeline only after GPU qualification;
  - interactive preview update with generation cancellation;
  - full export;
  - 10k-item library projection using an actual generated catalog.
- Make unavailable future scenarios inactive with a blocking issue reference rather than pretending to benchmark 1×1 placeholders.
- Validate expected output before accepting timing samples. Incorrect output invalidates the run.
- Record per-sample wall/CPU time, peak RSS, allocation counters where supported, cache counters, throughput, preview latency, GPU transfer/dispatch, and cancellation/fallback state.
- Use checked/saturating aggregation with explicit overflow errors; do not silently wrap metric sums.
- Add baseline comparison for p50/p95/p99, throughput, peak memory, allocations, and GPU transfer. Report absolute and percentage deltas plus confidence/noise classification.
- Store reviewed baselines keyed by host class and scenario, but never auto-update them.
- Integrate process isolation with #452.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Host mismatch produces `not-comparable`, not pass/fail.
- Build identities may differ; workload identity may not.
- Dirty source, incorrect output, timeout, cancellation, fallback when GPU was required, thermal/load instability, insufficient samples, counter overflow, or missing required metrics invalidates the run.
- Unsupported metrics are typed `unavailable`; zero is not used to mean unavailable.
- Inactive placeholders cannot be promoted to active without fixtures, expected output, and qualification tests.

## Test matrix

- Same host/two source commits compares successfully and retains both SHAs.
- Different CPU/GPU/driver/thread count/toolchain mode produces not-comparable where required.
- Same workload, changed fixture hash/operation parameters/output hash produces workload mismatch.
- Dirty build, incorrect output, timeout, cancellation, unstable host, missing metric, overflow, duplicate sample, and insufficient repetitions.
- Active/inactive/qualification state enforcement.
- Known percentile vectors and deterministic receipt serialization.
- Real scenario smoke runs on qualified merge hosts.

## Acceptance evidence

- Two distinct source commits on one qualified host produce a valid comparison.
- No active scenario uses 1×1 placeholder data or an all-zero expected output hash.
- Every active scenario validates its output before timing is accepted.
- Baseline receipts identify host, both builds, workload, uncertainty, and metric deltas.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446, #447, #450, and #452. Individual active scenarios may remain blocked by their product implementation issues, but the harness must mark them inactive explicitly.

## One-PR boundary

Only benchmark identities, qualification, scenario states/representative foundation scenarios, receipts, comparison, fixtures, and tests. Do not set final release performance budgets or optimize product code.
issues.title: Separate benchmark host and build identity and activate representative workloads

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Turn the approved dated Rust beta and dependency-source policy from checked-in prose/TOML receipts into executable validation. Ensure every compiler, framework, dependency, feature, cache, packaging, and artifact surface uses the same reproducible baseline while allowing deliberate beta-only Rust capabilities.

## Fixed decisions

- The production compiler remains the exact dated beta `beta-2026-07-17` until a dedicated baseline update changes it. Do not revert to stable Rust.
- Moving `beta`, nightly, and unpinned toolchains are prohibited for product builds.
- `rust-toolchain.toml` is the canonical compiler selector. `quality/compiler-baseline.toml` is the verified receipt derived from the installed toolchain, not a manually trusted duplicate.
- `rust-version = "1.98"` is the intended future compatibility floor and must be described as such; documentation must not claim stable Rust 1.98 already exists.
- Beta-only language and standard-library APIs are permitted when intentionally recorded in the ecosystem capability inventory and covered by regression tests.
- Nightly product features remain prohibited. Optional nightly experimentation must live outside the product dependency graph and cannot be required by CI or packages.
- Direct workspace dependency requirements are exact (`=x.y.z`) or exact immutable Git commits with reviewed exceptions. The committed lockfile remains mandatory.
- One workspace dependency table owns shared third-party versions/features. Product manifests inherit it unless target-specific ownership requires an explicit reviewed entry.
- Policy files are not evidence by themselves; each has an executable verifier and mutation tests.

## Implementation

- Implement `cargo xtask ecosystem verify-baseline` and `cargo xtask dependencies verify-policy`.
- Derive and compare:
  - rustc release/channel/commit/date;
  - Cargo release/commit;
  - LLVM version;
  - installed components/targets;
  - edition and intended rust-version;
  - build flags, warnings/unsafe policy, and relevant Cargo config;
  - framework/dependency versions and enabled features.
- Verify every local/hosted build, Clippy, rustfmt, rustdoc, coverage, benchmark, package, cache key, and artifact identity derives from `rust-toolchain.toml`/baseline rather than embedding another compiler string.
- Centralize shared dependencies in `[workspace.dependencies]`, including Iced and its test support. Replace ordinary caret requirements with exact approved requirements.
- Make target-specific feature policy explicit. Iced X11/Wayland features are enabled only on appropriate Linux targets; macOS and Windows do not inherit Linux window-system features.
- Generate a versioned ecosystem capability inventory for at least Rust, Cargo, LLVM, Iced, WGPU, Tokio, Rayon, redb, Wasmtime, mlua, rawler, codecs, shader tooling, serialization, and test tooling.
- For each relevant current capability, record one of:
  - adopted with owning issue/API use;
  - deferred with exact blocking reason/issue;
  - rejected with project-specific rationale.
- Detect dependency-source provenance, duplicate package lines, semver range drift, floating Git refs, local path leakage, unexpected native `-sys` packages, feature expansion, and lockfile changes.
- Verify the existing dependency security/advisory tools use exact versions and produce reviewed machine-readable reports. Advisory exceptions require ID, scope, expiry/review date, and owning issue; no broad empty policy claim.
- Add an upgrade-diff command that reports compiler capabilities, dependency versions/sources/features, lockfile graph changes, MSRV declarations, and affected platform/package surfaces before a baseline PR can merge.
- Add a non-blocking merge-only next-beta compatibility lane if desired, but it must not write caches/baselines used by the pinned production beta and must not fail the required product check unless promoted through a baseline issue.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Any compiler/component/target mismatch, moving channel, hidden version literal, non-exact direct requirement, unreviewed Git/path source, new native dependency, feature drift, or policy file without verifier fails.
- Beta-only API use not listed in the capability inventory fails source/feature policy.
- A newer beta availability does not automatically change the baseline.
- Toolchain updates occur in one PR and update all compiler-dependent surfaces together.
- Diagnostics contain package/tool identities but no credentials, private registry tokens, or absolute developer paths.

## Test matrix

- Exact pinned beta success and moving beta/nightly/stable mismatch.
- rustc/Cargo/LLVM/component/target/edition/rust-version drift.
- Hidden compiler literals in workflows, scripts, cache keys, packaging, and fixture expectations.
- Caret/range dependency, floating Git branch/tag, immutable Git commit, local path, duplicate versions, new native `-sys` package, and feature expansion.
- Workspace dependency inheritance and target-specific Iced features on Linux/macOS/Windows.
- Adopted/deferred/rejected capability schema and stale owner issue.
- Advisory exception missing owner/expiry and expired exception.
- Upgrade diff for compiler-only, dependency-only, feature-only, and source changes.
- Optional next-beta lane isolation.

## Acceptance evidence

- Every compiler-dependent surface reports the exact approved dated beta.
- Every direct third-party dependency is exact and centrally owned or has an explicit target-specific exception.
- The ecosystem/dependency verifier fails mutation fixtures for every policy field.
- Iced platform features do not force X11 on macOS/Windows and the UI/WGPU version relationship is explicit.
- Policy receipts are regenerated from verified reality, not accepted as self-declarations.

## Dependencies

Depends on #446 and should coordinate with #453 for external dependency ownership and target policy.

## One-PR boundary

Only compiler/framework/dependency baseline verification, workspace dependency centralization, platform feature policy, capability inventory, upgrade diff, advisory/source enforcement, and tests. Do not replace the approved dated beta or broadly upgrade unrelated dependencies unless required to make the current baseline internally consistent.

## Post-completion audit

The dated beta pin and exact direct dependency centralization landed, but PR #486 did not complete the full issue contract. The current ecosystem inventory lists only Iced, `upgrade-diff` emits four file hashes rather than a semantic comparison, the dependency verifier does not reconcile the lock graph, duplicates, native `-sys`/`links` packages, transitive sources, advisories, or exception policy, hidden compiler literals are not comprehensively scanned, and beta-only APIs are not tied to capability evidence. #517 is the required completion. #496 must reject the current #456 receipt as insufficient while preserving the deliberate `beta-2026-07-17` baseline.
issues.title: Enforce the dated Rust beta and dependency ecosystem baseline mechanically

## Why

RustTable needs a durable Iced 0.15.0-dev process host before product workflows can safely span multiple windows or continue background work after windows close. This PR establishes the bounded shell contract without moving catalog, processing, export, or GPU-device ownership into widgets.

## Implementation

- Uses the reviewed Iced development-line git revision `56b706fcd35aa881baede77a696bbb842773fdc6` (Iced `0.15.0-dev`) for both `rusttable-app`, `rusttable-ui`, and compatible `iced_test` dependencies; `Cargo.lock` records the exact source revision.
- Uses the Iced `daemon` builder with explicit window open/close routing and current window/event APIs.
- Adds `rusttable-ui` shell state for stable window keys, roles, workspace/monitor identity, bounds, scale, focus, modals, generations, themes, reduced motion, accessibility, and explicit exit policy.
- Uses composable `Task`/`Subscription` bridges, generation-tagged abortable work, `Task::sip` progress support, bounded/coalescing service events, and keyed window/keyboard/pointer/service subscriptions.
- Adds the WGPU `Shader`/`Program`/`Primitive` viewport boundary with resize, scissor, texture-generation, zero-copy-compatible, device-loss, and placeholder state.
- Uses Iced 0.15 `Fill`/`Shrink` sizing in shell-facing UI and retains design tokens plus `cargo xtask ui-shell --presets all --verify-a11y --verify-window-lifecycle`.
- Adds `architecture/ui-boundaries.toml` checks for UI dependency/ownership boundaries and handwritten-file limits.

## Validation

- `cargo check -p rusttable-ui -p rusttable-app --locked`
- `cargo test -p rusttable-ui -p rusttable-app --locked` (30 app, 44 UI, 2 CLI tests; doc tests pass)

- Rebased on `origin/main` at `aaefb173e7`; branch pushed at `5a28e9afc7`

## Out of scope

Lighttable, darkroom controls, masks, export screens, map/tethering/print/slideshow workflows, complete product screens, service scheduler implementation, and GPU device/processing ownership remain separate issue work.

Closes #420
