- Secret memory is zeroized on scope exit, error, cancellation, panic containment, and adapter conversion. Core dumps/process memory cannot be completely prevented, so receipts must not claim stronger guarantees.
- Platform availability drift invalidates active account capability but retains opaque refs for later recovery; no plaintext migration occurs.
- Test namespaces cannot enumerate or delete non-test items.

## Test matrix

- Create/read/replace/delete/list/idempotent duplicate/missing item, account label normalization, reference serialization, ephemeral secret, and zeroization/drop behavior.
- Locked/unavailable/denied/cancelled/timeout/native error/platform drift and concurrent access.
- Secret leak scans across Debug/display/errors/JSON/TOML/catalog/logs/process arguments/environment/support bundles.
- macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, and Linux Secret Service hosted contract tests using isolated RustTable test items.
- Architecture/DAG/source mutations adding secrets to config/catalog, serializing secret types, direct platform vault access, or plaintext fallback.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-credentials` plus all platform adapter contract suites pass.
- `cargo xtask credentials qualify --platform current --round-trip --failures all --verify-redaction --cleanup` emits availability/operation/cleanup receipts and leaves no test item.
- Piwigo and future integrations can persist only `CredentialRef`, lease a secret for one operation, and handle unavailable/locked/denied states without plaintext fallback.
- No product crate outside the approved adapters accesses Keychain, Credential Manager, Secret Service, or KWallet APIs directly.

## Dependencies

Depends on #168, #179, #180, #452, #453, #456, and #496.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements credential domain/service, platform adapters, ephemeral mode, configuration/privacy integration, tests, and receipts. Do not implement Piwigo authentication UI, general account-management UI, or network clients.
issues.title: Implement the cross-platform credential vault service

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the runtime display-profile service required for color-managed library, darkroom, secondary preview, soft proof, and multi-monitor presentation. RustTable must discover or explicitly select one validated ICC profile per active monitor, track hotplug/profile changes, provide immutable transform inputs to the renderer, and never silently assume sRGB when profile evidence is unavailable.

## Capabilities

- `build-option.USE_COLORD` — role `redesigned-platform-service`

## Fixed decisions

- Add safe product crate `rusttable-display-profile` containing monitor/profile identities, capability snapshots, selection policy, events, service port, errors, and privacy-safe receipts.
- Platform acquisition is isolated in approved adapters:
  - macOS: CoreGraphics/ColorSync display identity and current profile plus display-reconfiguration notifications;
  - Windows: DisplayConfig/monitor identity plus WCS/ICM current profile and display-change notifications;
  - Linux: colord D-Bus device/profile resolution keyed by connector/EDID evidence; X11 `_ICC_PROFILE` and supported Wayland color-management/desktop evidence may supplement colord through isolated adapters.
- `USE_COLORD` is not a compile-time product option. Linux colord is a runtime provider. Missing colord produces a typed provider-unavailable state and proceeds to an explicit user override or explicit degraded mode.
- Monitor identity is a stable privacy-safe hash over platform display identity, connector and nonsecret EDID characteristics. Raw EDID serial numbers and user/device labels do not appear in logs/receipts.
- Profile precedence is fixed:
  1. explicit RustTable per-monitor override copied into the managed profile store;
  2. current operating-system/desktop profile for the exact monitor;
  3. explicit user-selected fallback profile for unprofiled monitors;
  4. visible `Unprofiled` degraded state.
  There is no silent compiled sRGB fallback. A user may explicitly select built-in sRGB as the fallback, which is then receipt-visible.
- Profile bytes are copied/read into an immutable hash-addressed store, bounded, parsed/validated through #260, and identified by SHA-256. Product rendering never depends on a mutable external profile path after acquisition.
- Invalid, unsupported, oversized, unreadable, or changed profile bytes are rejected without retaining a partially parsed transform. The last valid profile remains active only with a visible stale/degraded state and exact reason.
- Each monitor state records monitor identity, provider, profile hash/metadata, acquisition generation, SDR/HDR capability evidence, scale factor/geometry, override/fallback status, and availability.
- The service publishes bounded events for monitor add/remove/change, window monitor transition, profile hash change, provider availability change, and override/fallback change.
- Windows/viewport code asks the service for an immutable `DisplayProfileSnapshot` by monitor identity/generation. ICC transforms are built by #261 and keyed by profile hash plus render intent/flags/format; this issue does not implement color math.
- When a window crosses monitors, the viewport keeps the old transform only until a complete new snapshot/transform is ready, then atomically swaps presentation generation. It never renders one frame with mixed transforms.
- Multi-window/secondary-preview monitor states are independent. Moving one window does not change another monitor’s profile selection.
- HDR capability metadata is inventoried for later tone/presentation policy, but this issue does not create HDR transfer/tone mapping or infer an HDR ICC profile from peak luminance alone.
- Profile/monitor prompts occur only in Preferences or a visible degraded banner. Startup/background monitoring does not unexpectedly open dialogs.

## Implementation

- Define `MonitorId`, `MonitorDescriptor`, `DisplayProfileId`, `DisplayProfileSnapshot`, provider/selection/degraded enums, override/fallback records, events, generation, and receipts.
- Implement platform adapter discovery/notifications with safe owned values and minimal isolated unsafe/native code under #168.
- Implement bounded profile byte acquisition, content hashing, managed-store commit, #260 validation, provider/override precedence, stale-last-valid handling, and startup reconciliation.
- Integrate Iced/winit window monitor events through the application service bridge without platform calls in widgets.
- Add service APIs for list monitors, current snapshot, set/remove override, set fallback, refresh/probe, and subscribe to changes.
- Integrate transform-cache invalidation hooks with #261 and viewport presentation generations; no direct renderer dependency on platform handles.
- Add source/DAG policy preventing colord/ColorSync/WCS/X11/Wayland profile access outside approved adapters.
- Emit monitor alias/provider/profile hash/selection/generation/HDR-capability/change receipts without raw EDID, serial, external paths, or profile payloads.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Distinguish provider unavailable, monitor unresolved, profile absent, permission/read failure, invalid/unsupported ICC, oversized profile, provider/profile drift, monitor removed, override missing/corrupt, and transform-build failure.
- A removed monitor invalidates new snapshot requests; windows move to the platform-selected remaining monitor or visible unprofiled state through #420 policy.
- Profile changes during rendering create a new generation; stale transform results cannot replace the current generation.
- Last-valid use is always marked stale and never persisted as though it were the current OS profile.
- Override removal re-evaluates precedence atomically. Failed override validation leaves the previous selection unchanged.
- Linux provider disagreement is resolved by fixed precedence/evidence and recorded; profiles are not blended.

## Test matrix

- Synthetic monitor/provider/profile inventory, per-monitor overrides/fallback, profile hash/change, invalid/oversized/missing bytes, last-valid stale state, and restart.
- Multi-monitor window moves, simultaneous windows, hotplug/remove/readd, scale/geometry changes, rapid profile events, stale transform completion, and device loss.
- macOS ColorSync, Windows WCS/ICM, Linux colord plus X11/Wayland capability contract tests using isolated/fake platform records where hosted hardware is unavailable.
- sRGB/matrix/LUT ICC profiles through #260/#261, unsupported device-link/broken profiles, HDR capability inventory, and no-silent-fallback assertions.
- Privacy leak scans for EDID serials, user display names, profile paths/payloads, and native handles.
- Architecture mutations adding platform profile access outside adapters.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-display-profile` plus platform adapter/color/viewport contract suites pass.
- `cargo xtask display-profile qualify --providers current --multi-monitor --profile-changes --verify-privacy` emits deterministic service/transform invalidation receipts.
- Each active monitor is explicitly profiled, explicitly user-fallbacked, or visibly unprofiled; no silent sRGB assumption exists.
- Moving windows between monitors swaps complete color transforms generation-safely and leaves other windows unchanged.

## Dependencies

Depends on #168, #179, #180, #257, #260, #261, #420, #452, #453, #456, and #496.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements monitor/profile discovery, platform adapters, managed profile storage, precedence/events, transform invalidation integration, tests, and receipts. Do not implement ICC transform math, soft proof, HDR tone mapping, or complete display-profile preferences UI.
issues.title: Implement cross-platform monitor profile discovery and change tracking

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement RustTable’s pure-Rust internationalization foundation: versioned Fluent message catalogs, locale selection/fallback, ICU4X plural/date/number/list formatting and collation, bidirectional/RTL layout signals, deterministic localized sort keys, pseudo-localization, and build-time translation validation without linking system ICU.

## Capabilities

- `build-option.USE_ICU` — role `redesigned-rust-service`

## Fixed decisions

- Add product crate `rusttable-i18n`. Use pinned Fluent crates for message syntax/bundles and pinned ICU4X components/data for locale identifiers, plural rules, number/date/time/list formatting, Unicode properties, segmentation where required, and collation.
- Do not link ICU4C, GLib localization, gettext runtime, platform localization frameworks, or a native `-sys` crate. `USE_ICU` becomes an always-available Rust-owned capability, not a build option.
- Bundle a compiled ICU4X data provider containing only data needed by shipped locales plus `und`/English fallback. No runtime network/data download or host ICU-version dependence.
- Default locale resolution is explicit user preference → operating-system language list → `en-US`. Persist a canonical BCP-47 locale; invalid/unsupported values fall back visibly and retain the rejected value only in diagnostics.
- English source catalogs use stable semantic message IDs. UI code requests message IDs plus typed arguments; raw English strings are not lookup keys.
- Fluent catalogs support plural/select/gender/grammatical variants. Runtime string concatenation of separately translated fragments is prohibited for user-visible sentences.
- Fallback chain is exact requested locale → progressively less specific locale → `en-US`. Missing English source messages are build failures; missing non-English messages fall back and are reported in translation coverage.
- Typed formatting arguments are string, integer, decimal, percent, byte count, duration, date/time, list, enum/select key, and safe rich-text spans. Arbitrary Fluent functions and HTML injection are prohibited.
- User-provided metadata, filenames, tags, camera/lens names, preset names, paths, and external errors are never translated as catalog messages. They are inserted as escaped values and kept distinguishable from UI text.
- Catalog/library locale-aware ordering uses ICU4X collation with a versioned `CollationProfile` containing locale, strength, numeric mode, case/diacritic policy, alternate handling, and ICU4X data/version hash. Persisted search rules store semantic fields, not opaque collation bytes.
- Default user-facing sort is locale-aware, numeric-aware, and case-insensitive at secondary strength while preserving deterministic tie-breaks by normalized Unicode scalar sequence then stable item ID. File/path storage and protocol ordering remain byte/stable-domain order.
- Search normalization is separate from display collation. Define one versioned Unicode normalization/case-fold pipeline; locale-specific Turkish/Lithuanian behavior is applied only where the search field contract permits it.
- Right-to-left locales set application direction and mirrored directional icons/layout where semantically appropriate. Image coordinates, histograms, curves, timelines where chronological direction is intentionally fixed, and photographic left/right controls are not blindly mirrored.
- Translation catalogs live under one governed `locales/<tag>/` tree, are UTF-8/NFC/LF, and are compiled/validated at build time. No separate translation runtime/plugin system.
- Provide pseudo-locales `en-XA` (expanded/accented) and `ar-XB` (RTL mirrored) for UI testing; they are developer/test options, not ordinary user locale choices.
- Locale changes apply live to ordinary UI state through generation-safe Iced subscriptions. Durable jobs/receipts retain locale-independent IDs and format only presentation text at display time.

## Implementation

- Define locale/collation/message IDs, typed argument values, fallback/coverage records, formatter/collator services, direction metadata, runtime snapshot/events, configuration, and receipts.
- Add Fluent parsing/compilation, typed message-schema generation from English catalogs, cross-locale argument/select compatibility validation, duplicate/missing/unused ID checks, and bounded runtime formatting.
- Build the minimal ICU4X data blob deterministically from declared shipped locales/components and record its identity in the ecosystem baseline.
- Implement locale detection/selection, live reload from bundled catalogs only, fallback, formatting, collation key generation, normalization/case-fold search helpers, and cache invalidation.
- Integrate Iced text direction, design tokens, focus/navigation semantics, and localized command/menu labels without introducing UI-specific strings into domain crates.
- Integrate locale-aware sort/collation services with library/search projections and version/hash invalidation. Persist only source values and profile, regenerating derived keys transactionally when profile/data changes.
- Add source policy forbidding direct user-visible literal strings outside allowed diagnostics/tests, unsafe concatenated message fragments, and system ICU/gettext dependencies.
- Emit locale/data/catalog/coverage/collation-profile/cache-generation receipts without user content.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject malformed BCP-47 tags, invalid Fluent syntax, missing English IDs, argument/type/select mismatch, duplicate IDs, unsupported ICU data, unsafe rich-text markup, formatter overflow, and invalid collation profiles.
- Missing translation falls back deterministically and is visible in coverage, not as an empty string or message ID.
- Formatting failure returns a safe English fallback plus a bounded diagnostic; it cannot panic a view or expose raw template internals to users.
- Locale/data upgrade invalidates/rebuilds derived collation caches without changing catalog source values or saved collection semantics.
- RTL direction changes cannot corrupt persisted geometry/window state or image coordinate transforms.
- User text is escaped/isolated and cannot inject Fluent syntax or rich-text actions.

## Test matrix

- Locale parsing/fallback for language/script/region/variant, unsupported/invalid tags, OS language lists, user override, restart, and live switch.
- Fluent plural/select/typed args/missing/duplicate/mismatch/unsafe markup, long expansions, and fallback coverage.
- ICU4X integer/decimal/percent/byte/duration/date/time/list formatting across representative locales and timezone inputs.
- Collation numeric/case/diacritic/locale/tie-break behavior including Turkish, German, Swedish, Japanese, Arabic, combining marks, emoji, invalid input boundaries, and version rebuild.
- Search normalization/case fold versus display collation separation.
- `en-XA` expansion and `ar-XB` RTL over every major Iced screen/preset with accessibility/focus/navigation tests.
- Dependency/source mutations adding system ICU/gettext or raw UI strings/concatenation.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-i18n` plus UI/library/search integration suites pass.
- `cargo xtask i18n verify --locales all --pseudo --coverage --collation --source-policy` emits deterministic catalogs/data/sort/RTL receipts.
- Switching locale updates all ordinary UI text/formatting/direction without restarting, saved data remains locale-independent, and library sort is deterministic under the selected profile.
- Product binaries have no ICU4C/gettext native dependency and require no runtime locale download.

## Dependencies

Depends on #179, #204, #213, #420, #453, #456, and #496.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements localization/catalog schemas, Fluent/ICU4X services/data, collation/search integration, live locale state, pseudo-locales, validation/source policy, tests, and receipts. Do not translate every future screen or add a translation marketplace/editor.
issues.title: Implement localization, ICU4X formatting, and locale-aware collation

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the macOS-specific runtime integration represented by darktable’s `USE_MAC_INTEGRATION`: native application/menu roles, Finder/Open-With document delivery, Dock reopen, application activation/termination, recent documents, and safe event bridging into the Iced daemon without moving product behavior into AppKit callbacks.

## Capabilities

- `build-option.USE_MAC_INTEGRATION` — role `redesigned-platform-service`

## Fixed decisions

- Add a target-specific adapter `rusttable-platform-macos-native` using pinned Rust AppKit/Foundation/CoreServices bindings. Any Objective-C/unsafe boundary is minimal, isolated, documented, and prohibited from other crates by #168/#453.
- Product state remains in `rusttable-app`/application services. The adapter converts native callbacks into owned typed events and invokes typed commands; it never reads the catalog, imports bytes, opens image codecs, or manipulates Iced state directly.
- Integrate with the `iced::daemon` process model from #420. Native activation and Dock reopen can create/focus a main window when the daemon is running without ordinary windows; last-window close remains governed by explicit RustTable lifecycle policy.
- Native application menu roles are About RustTable, Preferences, Services, Hide/Hide Others/Show All, Window, and Quit. Menu labels/actions are localized through #510; AppKit selectors route to typed RustTable commands.
- `Quit` requests the #180 coordinated shutdown, presents the existing durable-job confirmation policy, and terminates only after accepted shutdown/cleanup. AppKit may not force immediate process exit except after unrecoverable startup failure.
- Finder/Open With and application-open events accept bounded lists of file URLs only. URLs are normalized to local file paths, validated as regular files without following unsafe symlinks, and passed to the ordinary import/open dispatcher. Network/custom URLs are rejected unless a future explicit scheme issue is added.
- Supported document declarations are generated from the canonical image/sidecar/catalog capability registry. The adapter does not hard-code an extension list that can drift from codecs.
- Delivery semantics:
  - before service readiness, queue a bounded deduplicated startup list;
  - after readiness, dispatch one versioned open/import request per URL batch;
  - duplicate native events are idempotent by normalized file identity;
  - opening a RustTable catalog/database uses the explicit catalog-open policy, never generic image import.
- Dock reopen with no visible windows opens/focuses the last main-library workspace or deterministic default; it never creates duplicate main windows.
- Recent Documents entries are added only after a successful explicit user open/import and use platform APIs through the adapter. RustTable does not expose private catalog-derived files automatically.
- Drag/drop inside Iced remains the ordinary cross-platform UI path; native file-open events are normalized into the same application command.
- The initial distributed app is not Mac App Store sandboxed. Security-scoped bookmarks, Apple Events automation, Photos library integration, Quick Look extensions, Share extensions, Touch Bar, Handoff, and custom URL schemes are outside this issue.
- The bundle identifier, document types, menu roles, version identity, and executable architecture are derived from packaging/build receipts, not duplicated literals.

## Implementation

- Define safe `MacApplicationEvent`, `MacOpenRequest`, activation/reopen/terminate events, adapter commands, queue limits, state/generation, and privacy-safe receipts.
- Install the native application delegate/event bridge before Iced startup, then connect it to the application service registry once ready.
- Implement menu roles and localization/action routing, activation/Dock reopen, open-files batching/deduplication, recent-document updates, and coordinated termination reply.
- Generate/validate Info.plist document declarations from the capability registry and bundle identity; add deterministic package tests rather than hand-editing another extension list.
- Add application adapters that map native file URLs to typed import/catalog requests and surface bounded errors through ordinary UI notifications.
- Add target/DAG/source policy preventing AppKit/Foundation lifecycle access outside the adapter.
- Emit event type/count, file alias/hash classification, dispatch result, window action, shutdown decision, and duration receipts without full private paths.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject non-file URLs, malformed/oversized event lists, unsafe symlinks, unsupported file types, missing files, catalog/image ambiguity, service-not-ready overflow, and stale duplicate events with typed outcomes.
- An invalid file in a batch does not hide valid files; per-item outcomes are returned in deterministic order.
- Native termination request can be cancelled by the user/job policy. The adapter replies to AppKit only after the Rust lifecycle decision.
- Reopen/activation events during shutdown are ignored with a typed state rather than reviving services.
- Adapter panic/native exception containment cannot unwind across Objective-C callbacks.
- Recent-document failure is nonfatal and cannot convert an unsuccessful import into success.

## Test matrix

- Cold-start open events before/after readiness, one/multiple files, duplicates, unsupported/missing/symlink/non-file URLs, catalog versus image, and queue bounds.
- Activation, Dock reopen with zero/one/multiple windows, hidden/minimized windows, last workspace/default, and shutdown races.
- About/Preferences/Window/Quit menu roles, localization changes, keyboard equivalents, durable-job shutdown accept/cancel, and native termination replies.
- Generated Info.plist document types against codec registry and bundle/version/architecture identity.
- Hosted macOS integration tests using a test bundle plus pure adapter fixtures on other platforms; architecture/source-policy mutations for direct AppKit use.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-platform-macos-native -p rusttable-app macos_integration` passes on Apple Silicon macOS.
- `cargo xtask platform macos --open-events --reopen --menus --termination --verify-bundle` emits native/application/package receipts.
- Opening supported files from Finder, reopening from the Dock, choosing Preferences/About/Quit, and handling durable jobs route through typed RustTable services with no duplicate windows/imports or forced unsafe exit.
- Non-macOS builds contain no AppKit dependency or feature surface.

## Dependencies

Depends on #168, #180, #248, #420, #453, #456, #510, and #496.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the macOS native adapter, lifecycle/menu/open/reopen/recent-document bridge, generated document declarations, tests, and receipts. Do not implement signing/notarization, App Store sandboxing, Quick Look, Photos integration, custom URL schemes, or general cross-platform import UI.
issues.title: Implement native macOS application lifecycle and file-open integration

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the device/action service that converts keyboard, mouse, tablet, MIDI, and gamepad input into versioned RustTable commands with deterministic mapping, focus/context gating, soft takeover, hotplug, bounded event flow, and no PortMidi or SDL2 dependency.

## Capabilities

- `build-option.USE_PORTMIDI` — role `redesigned-rust-service`
- `build-option.USE_SDL2` — role `redesigned-rust-service`
- `lib.midi` — role `implementation`
- `lib.gamepad` — role `implementation`

## Fixed decisions

- Add safe `rusttable-input` domain/service and target-isolated adapters where native access is required.
- Keyboard/mouse/touch/tablet events originate from Iced/winit platform events and are normalized into typed device events. SDL2 is not linked or used as a second event loop/window/input system.
- MIDI uses the pinned `midir` stack and platform backends (CoreMIDI, Windows MIDI, ALSA). RustTable does not link PortMidi. MIDI input only in the initial contract; SysEx, MIDI output/feedback, network MIDI, and device scripting are later work.
- Gamepad uses the pinned `gilrs` stack with supported platform backends. RustTable does not link SDL2 for controllers.
- Device identity is a privacy-safe stable alias over backend/vendor/product/port evidence. Raw serial numbers, USB paths, and user device names are not emitted in logs/receipts.
- The action registry contains stable semantic action IDs, localized labels/categories, applicable contexts, parameter schema, repeat/continuous policy, undo grouping, and availability predicate. Device mappings target action IDs, never widget pointers, key codes embedded in product logic, or translated labels.
- Input contexts are explicit: global, lighttable, darkroom, map, tethering, print, slideshow, modal dialog, text entry, and focused-control override. Higher-priority contexts shadow lower ones deterministically; text entry consumes ordinary typing unless a binding is explicitly marked global-safe.
- Mapping sources are keyboard chords/sequences, pointer buttons/wheel, tablet buttons/pressure/tilt where provided by winit, MIDI note/CC/pitch/14-bit CC/relative encoder, and gamepad button/axis/hat.
- Continuous mappings define source range/deadzone/curve/invert/relative mode, target parameter range/step, and soft-takeover policy. Absolute knobs use pickup/soft takeover by default to prevent parameter jumps.
- Relative MIDI encoders support reviewed two’s-complement, binary-offset, signed-bit, and increment/decrement modes. Unknown mode is never auto-detected during ordinary use; learn mode can present observed candidates for explicit selection.
- Event processing is bounded and coalesces continuous-axis/CC/pressure updates by device/control/action generation while preserving button/note edge events. UI/event threads cannot be flooded by high-rate hardware.
- Repeat/gesture behavior and undo coalescing are fixed per action. A slider/parameter gesture begins on first accepted continuous event and ends after an inactivity deadline or device release; one gesture creates one undo entry.
- Device hotplug, disconnect, sleep/resume, backend unavailable, and permission denial are runtime states. Missing optional MIDI/gamepad support does not affect keyboard/tablet operation.
- Mappings are versioned user configuration with built-in immutable defaults and user overrides. Unknown action/device/control records are preserved but inactive with a finding.
- No input device can execute shell commands, scripts, file paths, network requests, or arbitrary extension calls directly. It invokes registered action commands subject to ordinary permission/context policy.

## Implementation

- Define device/control/event/action/context/mapping/curve/soft-takeover/gesture models, registry, service snapshot/events, configuration schema/migration, errors, and privacy-safe receipts.
- Implement keyboard/chord/sequence normalization and conflict resolution on top of Iced/winit events.
- Implement target adapters for MIDI enumeration/input and gamepad enumeration/events, hotplug, bounded queues, cancellation, and cleanup.
- Implement tablet pressure/tilt/button normalization from supported pointer events while preserving ordinary mouse fallback.
- Implement mapping resolver, context stack, continuous transforms, relative encoder modes, pickup state, coalescing/backpressure, gesture/undo grouping, and action dispatch to the application command bus.
- Add action registration APIs that domain/UI modules use without depending on hardware crates.
- Add source/DAG policy forbidding PortMidi/SDL2 and direct `midir`/`gilrs`/native input access outside adapters.
- Emit device alias/capability, mapping/action/context, coalescing/gesture/result, hotplug, and duration receipts without raw device identity or user data.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject malformed mappings, unknown action/context/control/mode, impossible ranges/curves, duplicate/conflicting bindings without explicit precedence, excessive sequence length, and unsupported device capabilities.
- Disconnect during a gesture closes/cancels the gesture deterministically and cannot leave a parameter locked in pickup state.
- Device/backend failure affects only that adapter and generates a bounded availability event; keyboard/application input continues.
- High-rate input beyond queue limits coalesces continuous values and records drops; edge events use a reserved bounded lane and cannot be silently converted to continuous state.
- Modal/text contexts prevent destructive global actions unless explicitly safe and tested.
- Configuration migration/parse failure leaves last valid mappings active and preserves unknown records for recovery.

## Test matrix

- Keyboard chords/sequences/repeat/context/focus/text/modal conflicts across major workspaces.
- MIDI note/CC/14-bit/relative modes, soft takeover, curves/ranges/invert, hotplug/disconnect, malformed/high-rate events, and backend unavailable.
- Gamepad buttons/axes/deadzone/hat, multiple devices, hotplug, and coalescing.
- Tablet pressure/tilt/buttons, unsupported capability, mouse fallback, and gesture/undo grouping.
- Mapping persistence/migrations/unknown actions/conflicts/defaults/overrides and deterministic resolution.
- Privacy/source/DAG mutations adding PortMidi/SDL2/direct hardware access or shell/script actions.
- Hosted platform adapter tests with deterministic virtual/fake devices where real hardware is unavailable.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-input` plus platform adapter/application command suites pass.
- `cargo xtask input qualify --devices fake-all --contexts all --high-rate --hotplug --verify-undo --verify-privacy` emits deterministic service/action receipts.
- Keyboard remains fully functional without MIDI/gamepad backends; supported devices map to actions with context/soft-takeover/undo semantics and no event-thread flooding.
- Product binaries contain no PortMidi or SDL2 dependency.

## Dependencies

Depends on #179, #180, #420, #453, #456, and #496.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements the action registry, input service/adapters, mapping resolution, contexts, continuous/gesture behavior, persistence, tests, and receipts. Do not implement the complete mapping editor UI, MIDI output/feedback, SysEx, scripting, or app-specific default mapping sets beyond minimal fixtures.
issues.title: Implement unified keyboard, MIDI, gamepad, and tablet action input service

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Add the Iced editor for discovering RustTable actions and configuring keyboard, mouse/tablet, MIDI, and gamepad mappings from #512, including learn mode, context/conflict analysis, continuous curves/soft takeover, profile import/export, reset, accessibility, and live generation-safe application.

## Fixed decisions

- Add `Shortcuts & input` under Preferences with action-centric and device-centric views over the same #512 mapping snapshot.
- The action list is derived from the runtime action registry and shows localized action/category, context, current bindings, parameter/continuous capability, availability, and conflict state. UI does not maintain a separate hard-coded action list.
- Search operates on localized labels plus stable action IDs/categories; saved mappings store action IDs only.
- Binding editors are source-specific:
  - keyboard chord/sequence recorder with explicit modifier/key display and maximum sequence length from #512;
  - pointer/tablet button, wheel, pressure, and tilt controls exposed only when the action/source contract supports them;
  - MIDI learn for note/CC/pitch/14-bit/relative encoders plus explicit relative-mode choice and source channel/device filter;
  - gamepad learn for button/axis/hat plus deadzone/invert/curve.
- Learn mode is explicit, modal within the editor, has a 15-second inactivity timeout, shows raw normalized observations without private device serial/path data, and never dispatches the captured action while learning.
- Continuous binding editor exposes input range, deadzone, curve (`Linear`, `Fine center`, `Exponential` with checked exponent), invert, relative mode, target range/step, and soft-takeover `Pickup` default/`Jump` explicit. Invalid combinations cannot be saved.
- Conflict analysis uses #512 context precedence. Exact same-context collisions block save unless one binding is removed; shadowing across contexts is allowed only with a visible explanation and deterministic preview.
- Built-in default mappings are immutable. User mappings can override/disable defaults and reset by action/device/context/all.
- Mapping profiles contain schema/version, user-visible name, optional device match criteria, and mapping records. Export/import uses canonical JSON with no raw device serials, executable/script fields, or extension code. Imported unknown action/device records are shown inactive and can be retained or removed.
- Applying changes validates and commits one complete mapping generation through #512. Failed validation/commit leaves the previous live generation active.
- Device availability/hotplug updates the editor live. Offline-device mappings remain editable by stored capability descriptors and become active only when a compatible device returns.
- A `Test binding` mode previews normalized value/action/context and soft-takeover behavior without executing destructive actions.
- Keyboard-only operation, screen-reader labels/relationships/live capture status, focus order, high-DPI, localization, RTL, reduced motion, and large virtualized action lists are complete.

## Implementation

- Add typed action/device/profile/filter/editor/learn/conflict/test/apply/reset/import/export state, messages, and view models.
- Subscribe to #512 registry/device/mapping generation events and coalesce high-rate learn/test observations without rendering every hardware event.
- Implement source-specific recorders/editors, conflict/context preview, continuous-curve visualization, soft-takeover simulator, and deterministic canonical mapping summaries.
- Implement profile JSON parse/validation/preview/commit/export through service ports, with exact changed/unknown/conflict counts before confirmation.
- Implement generation-safe apply/revert/reset and preserve unsaved editor changes across nonconflicting device hotplug events.
- Add architecture tests forbidding UI direct access to `midir`, `gilrs`, platform hardware APIs, configuration files, or action execution during learn/test.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Unknown/unavailable action, device disconnect during capture, learn timeout/cancel, malformed input, exact conflict, invalid curve/range/mode, profile parse/schema/limit error, stale mapping generation, and service commit failure have explicit recoverable states.
- Device events observed during ordinary text editing do not enter learn mode or change bindings.
- A stale apply result cannot overwrite newer mappings; the editor offers reload/merge-discard with exact changed records.
- Imported profiles cannot create script/shell/file/network actions or bypass context safety.
- Test mode cannot execute actions or create undo/history changes.
- Removing the last binding from a critical action is allowed only where the action registry does not mark a nonremovable accessibility/safety fallback.

## Test matrix

- Action/device searches, contexts, built-in/user overrides, disable/reset, exact/shadow conflicts, stale generations, and live apply.
- Keyboard chords/sequences/layout-independent keys/modifiers, reserved OS combinations, text/modal contexts, and recorder timeout.
- MIDI/gamepad/tablet learn, multiple devices/channels/controls, relative modes, ranges/curves/deadzone/invert/pickup, hotplug/disconnect, and high-rate coalescing.
- Profile import/export round trip, unknown records, malformed/oversized JSON, device-match privacy, conflict preview, and canonical bytes.
- Keyboard-only/a11y/RTL/localization/high-DPI/virtualization and no-action-dispatch learn/test assertions.
- Architecture tests proving all device/action logic remains in #512.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-ui -p rusttable-app input_mapping_editor` passes.
- `cargo xtask ui input-mappings --devices fake-all --conflicts all --profiles all --verify-learn --verify-a11y` emits deterministic UI/service receipts.
- Users can discover actions, learn/configure each supported input type, resolve conflicts, test without execution, apply/restart, and import/export profiles without raw device identifiers or unsafe action types.

## Dependencies

Depends on #420, #510, #512, and #496.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements shortcut/device mapping editor UI, learn/test/conflict/profile workflows, accessibility, tests, and receipts. Do not implement device backends, action execution, MIDI output/SysEx, scripting, or new product actions.
issues.title: Implement the shortcut and device-mapping editor UI

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Implement the package-metadata validation layer for Linux, macOS, and Windows distributions. It must generate or validate AppStream, desktop-entry, MIME/document-type, Info.plist, MSIX/AppxManifest, icons, and artifact identity from canonical RustTable capability/build data without requiring `xmllint` or trusting hand-maintained duplicate metadata.

## Capabilities

- `build-option.USE_XMLLINT` — role `redesigned-build-validation`
- `build-option.VALIDATE_APPDATA_FILE` — role `redesigned-build-validation`
- `build-option.BINARY_PACKAGE_BUILD` — role `build-profile`
- `build-option.BUILD_MSYS2_INSTALL` — role `unsupported-packaging-path`

## Fixed decisions

- Add repository tooling under `xtask package metadata`; no product runtime crate parses or validates package manifests.
- Use pinned pure-Rust XML/desktop/plist/JSON manifest parsers and RustTable-owned semantic validators. `xmllint` is not a required local/CI/product dependency.
- `BINARY_PACKAGE_BUILD` becomes an explicit package/release build profile derived from the compiler/dependency/build identity. It is not a preprocessor flag and cannot change product semantics silently.
- `BUILD_MSYS2_INSTALL` is unsupported. Windows packages use the native MSVC target and reviewed Windows packaging issue; MSYS2 installer scripts/import libraries/runtime layout are not produced.
- One canonical `packaging/product.toml` owns product ID, display name, executable IDs, version source, vendor/publisher placeholders, URLs, categories, supported architectures, document/MIME capability mapping, icon/source identities, and package-specific declarations. Generated platform manifests derive from it and the image/capability registry.
- Linux validation covers AppStream metainfo, `.desktop`, MIME associations, icon sizes/formats, executable command, categories, startup WM class/application ID, release/version linkage, and installed-tree paths.
- macOS validation covers Info.plist bundle ID/name/version/build, executable, supported architectures/minimum OS, document/UTType declarations, icons, high-resolution capability, and consistency with #511 and package receipts.
- Windows validation covers Appx/MSIX identity/publisher placeholder, version conversion, executable/application ID, display names, architecture/minimum OS, capabilities, file associations, visual assets, and installed-tree paths. Signing certificate identity is a later release input but must match publisher when supplied.
- Document/file associations are generated from the canonical codec/catalog/sidecar capability registry and explicit product policy. An unsupported codec or migration artifact cannot enter package metadata because an extension happens to exist in a fixture.
- Metadata is deterministic for exact product/capability/build inputs. Generated files carry schema/generator/input hashes and are checked in only when the packaging workflow requires them; generated drift fails.
- PR checks run pure parser/semantic fixtures and generated-diff checks within budget. Merge/package jobs validate actual staged package roots with platform-native inspection tools as supplementary evidence, not as the sole source of truth.
- AppStream descriptive/release text is concise product metadata, not a separate documentation project. No licensing-planning issue or license gate is introduced.

## Implementation

- Define canonical product/package metadata schema, capability/document mapping, platform output models, generator version, limits, errors, and receipts.
- Implement deterministic Linux AppStream/desktop/MIME/icon generation and structural/semantic validation.
- Implement deterministic macOS plist/document/icon generation/validation consistent with bundle and #511 runtime identities.
- Implement deterministic Windows Appx/MSIX manifest/visual/file-association generation/validation for native MSVC package roots.
- Add staged-root validation: exactly declared executables/assets/manifests, contained relative paths, regular files, modes, architecture, version, and metadata cross-references.
- Integrate compiler/build/artifact identity from #456, document capabilities, and packaging smoke/release commands without duplicating version strings.
- Add source/workflow policy rejecting hand-authored drifting copies, `xmllint` dependency, MSYS2 packaging commands, unsupported file associations, and unbounded XML entity/external-resource behavior.
- Emit input/generator/output hashes, platform semantics, document associations, asset inventory, build identity, and staged-root result receipts.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Reject malformed/oversized XML/plist/manifest, DTD/external entity, duplicate IDs, unknown fields/capabilities, invalid platform IDs/versions, missing/wrong icons/assets, architecture/executable mismatch, unsupported association, path escape/symlink, stale generated file, and staged-root drift.
- Platform-native validator disagreement blocks the package job and records both parser/semantic and native findings; it is not ignored.
- Missing signing identity is allowed for explicitly unsigned smoke artifacts but cannot be labeled signed/release-ready.
- An unsupported MSYS2 request returns a stable policy failure and cannot generate a partial installer.
- Version conversion failures (for example, nonrepresentable MSIX numeric version) fail before staging.

## Test matrix

- Valid/minimal/full Linux, macOS, and Windows metadata plus malformed XML/plist/JSON, entities, duplicate/missing IDs, invalid versions, icons/assets, paths, associations, and architectures.
- Capability registry changes adding/removing codecs/document types, generated drift, stale hashes, unsupported fixture extensions, and catalog/sidecar associations.
- Unsigned versus signed metadata fields, native-validator disagreement, staged-root missing/extra/symlink/wrong executable/architecture.
- Exact `xmllint` and MSYS2 dependency/command mutations plus duplicate version literal detection.
- Deterministic generation from two checkout paths/platforms where platform-independent outputs should match.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p xtask package_metadata` passes.
- `cargo xtask package metadata verify --all-platforms --fixtures all` emits deterministic generation/semantic receipts.
- Each real staged package job validates its platform metadata and installed tree, and no package association advertises unsupported behavior.
- Product/package validation requires neither `xmllint` nor MSYS2.

## Dependencies

Depends on #137, #456, #470, #492, #496, #510, and #511. Platform signing/publication issues may depend on this contract.

## One-PR boundary

One PR implements canonical package metadata, platform generators/validators, staged-root checks, policy/tests, and receipts. Do not build/sign/publish final Flatpak/DMG/MSIX packages or implement runtime platform behavior.
issues.title: Implement deterministic desktop-package metadata validation

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Correct the incomplete implementation accepted under #452/PR #484. Make every field in `quality/validation-surfaces.toml` control real execution; replace inherited-environment/raw-receipt subprocesses and process discovery with owned Unix process groups and Windows Job Objects; and prove local/hosted validation, artifacts, networking, prerequisites, severity, cleanup, and budgets through mutation and platform tests.

## Observed blocking defects on current `main`

- `platforms` is parsed but checks are selected only by surface/group. A platform-specific check is not filtered before executable lookup or execution.
- `severity = "warning"` is parsed, but any failed receipt still fails the validation group and cancels siblings.
- Prerequisites are validated lexically by group name; `execute_surface_for_group` does not evaluate prerequisite result state. PR workflow matrix jobs execute groups independently, so a later group can run even when its declared prerequisite group failed or never ran.
- Declared artifacts are copied into receipts but generic artifact existence, containment, freshness, type, size, and hash are not validated. Only one workspace-DAG command receives special stdout/stderr files.
- `network = "none"` is validated as a string but does not build a minimal environment, add Cargo offline/frozen policy, or isolate the process from network access.
- `ProcessRequest::new` inherits the complete parent environment. Receipts retain raw program, argv, current directory, and an environment hash over secret-bearing inherited values.
- Unix termination still recursively discovers children with external `pgrep` and sends external `kill` commands. No owned process group/session is created.
- There is no Windows Job Object implementation in the current process service.
- Pipe read errors are treated as EOF; post-exit reader joins have no independent bounded drain deadline and can hang when descendants retain handles.
- The aggregate main budget remains nonenforced in the execution loop.
- PR #484 changed only workflow grouping/timeouts plus tests; it did not land the claimed process/validation implementation.

## Fixed decisions

- Replace the current subprocess layer with one versioned `ProcessService` used by validation, reference, benchmark, extension/tooling, and future managed external-editor jobs. Callers cannot bypass it with direct `std::process::Command` except inside reviewed platform adapters/tests.
- `ProcessRequest` begins with an empty environment. Callers select a named allowlist profile (`RustTool`, `GitTool`, `GitHubApi`, `ReferenceTool`, `PlatformTool`, `TestFixture`) plus explicit values. Full environment inheritance is prohibited.
- Secrets use non-serializable redacted values. GitHub HTTP uses the approved Rust client/connector path with secret headers; no token may appear in argv, receipt, debug output, environment hash input, or persisted artifact.
- Receipts record logical command ID, classified/redacted argv, stable path aliases, selected environment variable names plus nonsecret policy hash, process-tree identity, output hashes/excerpts, artifacts, cleanup, and timing. Raw absolute checkout/temp paths are prohibited.
- On Unix, spawn the child as leader of an owned process group/session before user code executes. Termination is `SIGTERM` to the process group, bounded grace, then `SIGKILL`, followed by complete reap/handle/drain verification. Do not use `pgrep`, `pkill`, or shell `kill`.
- On Windows, assign the child to an owned Job Object before execution can escape, enable kill-on-job-close, and use Job Object process/accounting state for cancellation/cleanup. `taskkill` is not ownership.
- Output readers preserve read errors as typed failures, stream bounded artifacts, retain bounded excerpts/hashes, and have an explicit post-exit drain deadline. Descendants retaining stdout/stderr cannot hang the caller indefinitely.
- Validation platform filtering occurs before command availability. Supported selectors are `all`, `linux`, `macos`, and `windows`; unknown selectors fail the contract. Omitted receipts include exact platform reason.
- Prerequisites form a real DAG of check IDs. One logical surface scheduler executes ready waves only after every prerequisite has `Passed` or accepted `Warning`; failed/omitted prerequisites yield deterministic `SkippedPrerequisite` results.
- Hosted PR execution does not split dependency-related groups into independent matrix jobs without a receipt handoff. Use one scheduler job or explicit GitHub job `needs` plus signed/content-addressed prerequisite receipts validated by the downstream group. The simpler initial implementation is one bounded PR scheduler job with internal waves.
- Severity semantics are exact: `error` fails/cancels according to policy; `warning` records failure and continues, cannot satisfy a prerequisite requiring strict pass unless the prerequisite edge declares `accept_warning = true`; unknown values fail contract load.
- `network = none` uses minimal environment plus Cargo `--offline`/`CARGO_NET_OFFLINE=true` where applicable and is qualified on Linux in a network namespace/firewall fixture. `network = read` is main-only, receives no write credentials, and follows bounded destination policy.
- Artifact descriptors are structured records with path, required type, root, max bytes, hash policy, and freshness policy. Successful commands fail if required artifacts are missing, stale, outside the declared root, symlinks, wrong type, oversized, or unreadable. Artifact SHA-256/size enter the receipt.
- Resource groups are explicit. Cargo commands sharing one target directory run sequentially; parallel Cargo work requires isolated `CARGO_TARGET_DIR` values. The contract validator proves no resource conflict.
- Budgets include command execution, cancellation, process cleanup, output drain, artifact verification, and scheduler overhead. All four aggregate budgets, including main, are enforced.
- Wrapper/workflow commands appear exactly once per logical surface. Hosted setup such as toolchain/cache provisioning is modeled separately from repository checks and cannot duplicate a contract command.

## Implementation

- Define typed process command/environment/secret/path/limits/termination/output/artifact/cleanup receipts and platform adapter interfaces.
- Implement Unix process-group/session ownership and Windows Job Object ownership with minimal isolated unsafe/native code and safety invariants.
- Migrate every current `xtask`/testkit direct process caller to the shared service or add an explicit tracked follow-up when migration cannot fit this one PR; validation/reference/benchmark/GitHub API paths are mandatory in this PR.
- Redesign validation contract artifact, prerequisite, platform, resource, environment, and severity schemas with backward migration or a single reviewed schema bump.
- Implement topological scheduling, ready waves, strict/warning prerequisite rules, platform omission, resource exclusion, cancellation, aggregate budgets, and deterministic receipts.
- Replace the PR matrix group design with one dependency-correct scheduler or verified receipt handoff; remove obsolete timeout/group workarounds.
- Implement artifact verification after process success and include stable identities.
- Implement minimal environment profiles and network-none enforcement/qualification.
- Add policy scanning that rejects `pgrep`, `pkill`, `taskkill`, shell `kill`, inherited environment defaults, raw secret argv, direct process spawning outside adapters, and absolute paths in durable receipts.
- Update #496 foundation contract to consume the new receipt schema and platform qualification evidence.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Spawn-before-ownership failure, Job Object/session setup failure, environment construction, pipe read, wait, timeout, cancellation, TERM/KILL, reap, drain timeout, descendant leak, artifact, network-policy, budget, and receipt-redaction failures are distinct typed statuses.
- Cleanup failure overrides apparent child success.
- A warning cannot cancel unrelated error checks, and it cannot satisfy a strict prerequisite.
- A platform-omitted prerequisite omits its dependent with explicit causal evidence; it does not execute it.
- Process output beyond in-memory cap does not change command success when a bounded artifact sink is configured; artifact failure does.
- Secret values are zeroized/released after request construction and never formatted on any failure path.
- A child cannot escape ownership by double-forking, spawning grandchildren, inheriting handles, or exiting before descendants.
- Main-budget expiration cancels all active work and completes cleanup before returning a failed surface receipt.

## Test matrix

- Platform selection and command availability for Linux/macOS/Windows/all/unknown.
- DAG pass, strict warning dependency, accepted warning dependency, failure skip, omitted-platform skip, unknown/self/cyclic dependency, deterministic waves, resource conflicts, and cancellation.
- Error versus warning behavior, sibling cancellation, multiple simultaneous failures, stable receipt ordering, and all aggregate budgets including cleanup overhead.
- Network-none process attempting localhost/external connection, Cargo offline cache hit/miss, read-only main check, and credential absence.
- Artifact missing/stale/symlink/outside-root/wrong-type/oversized/hash drift/fresh success and command-success/artifact-failure precedence.
- Unix process group and Windows Job Object: direct child, grandchildren, double-fork/detach attempt, inherited output handle, graceful TERM, forced KILL, timeout, cancellation, parent success with live descendant, and complete reap.
- Read errors, invalid UTF-8, stdout/stderr flood, artifact streaming, drain deadline, reader panic, and cleanup failure.
- Secret/path redaction over argv, environment, errors, receipts, artifacts, debug output, and GitHub HTTP calls.
- Hosted PR/main workflow inventory proving each logical command executes once and prerequisites cannot be bypassed by parallel jobs.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p xtask process validation` passes on Linux, macOS, and Windows hosted runners.
- `cargo xtask ci qualify --all-surfaces --all-mutations --verify-network --verify-artifacts --verify-process-trees --verify-redaction` emits complete receipts.
- No current source path uses `pgrep`, `pkill`, `taskkill`, external `kill`, inherited environment defaults, or raw secret-bearing command receipts.
- A failing prerequisite prevents every dependent hosted/local check, warning semantics match the contract, required artifacts are verified, and all four budgets include cleanup.
- #496 accepts the new platform/process/validation receipts and rejects the previous #452 receipt schema as insufficient.

## Dependencies

Depends on #452 and #453. Blocks #494, #496, #506, and any job/service requiring owned subprocesses.

## One-PR boundary

One PR replaces the shared subprocess service, validation scheduler/contract semantics, mandatory caller migrations, workflow orchestration, policy checks, tests, and receipts. Do not implement product task scheduling, external-editor business logic, or unrelated validation checks.
issues.title: Finish validation execution and cross-platform process isolation after #452

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Correct the remaining defects in the schema-2 differential image oracle delivered by #447/PR #464 so alpha weighting has one consistent mathematical meaning, normalization cannot manufacture invalid canonical pixels from an invalid transfer declaration, and durable JSON receipts remain bounded while heatmap/blink evidence is stored as separate content-addressed artifacts.

## Observed defects on current `main`

- `observe_pair` multiplies the alpha error by `alpha_weight`, then `MetricsAccumulator::observe` squares that already weighted error and multiplies it by `alpha_weight²` again. Alpha therefore contributes `raw_error² * alpha_weight⁴` to RMSE while absolute/relative errors use only one weight.
- `normalize` accepts `TransferFunction::Gamma(gamma)` without validating `gamma`; an invalid/nonpositive/nonfinite gamma returns `NaN` from `decode_transfer` and produces a nominal canonical buffer rather than a typed normalization error.
- `DiffReceipt` embeds `DiffArtifact { bytes: Vec<u8> }`, and `stable_json` serializes full heatmap and both full float blink planes into JSON. The receipt documentation claims no unbounded image dump, but receipt size/memory grows with every requested image plane.
- The current tests cover only `alpha_weight = 1.0`, one invalid/nonfinite path, and artifact envelope parsing; they do not detect the weighting inconsistency or enforce bounded durable receipt size.

## Fixed decisions

- Define `alpha_weight` as a multiplier on the raw alpha scalar error. The weighted alpha error is `abs(a-b) * alpha_weight` for maximum absolute, relative, severity, and RMSE. RMSE squares this weighted error exactly once. No second accumulator weight is applied.
- RMSE denominator is the sum of squared channel weights: `3 + alpha_weight²`. With stored weighted errors, the accumulator sums RGB error² plus weighted-alpha-error² and divides once by `pixel_count * (3 + alpha_weight²)`.
- `alpha_weight` remains finite and nonnegative. Zero excludes alpha from aggregate RMSE/severity while the receipt still records raw alpha mismatch count/maximum in a dedicated alpha metric so callers can distinguish intentional exclusion from equality.
- Add explicit receipt metrics:
  - `maximum_rgb_absolute_error`;
  - `maximum_alpha_absolute_error` (raw linear coverage error);
  - `weighted_maximum_absolute_error` used by policy pass/fail;
  - `rgb_rmse`, `alpha_rmse`, and combined weighted `rmse`.
  Migrate to diff schema 3; schema 2 cannot satisfy new parity/foundation gates.
- Normalization validates transfer declarations before traversal:
  - `Linear` and `Srgb` are valid;
  - `Gamma(g)` requires finite `g > 0` and a reviewed maximum bound;
  - an invalid declaration returns `InvalidTransfer`, never a NaN pixel.
- Normalization validates every generated RGB/alpha sample according to an explicit `NonFiniteInputPolicy`. Default parity normalization rejects nonfinite encoded inputs and nonfinite results from unpremultiplication/transfer/profile conversion. Tests that intentionally compare infinities construct canonical buffers explicitly under the comparison policy rather than relying on normalization to manufacture them.
- Premultiplied normalization defines subnormal/near-zero alpha behavior with a checked normalization parameter `unpremultiply_epsilon`, default `1e-8`: finite alpha `<= epsilon` normalizes hidden RGB to zero while retaining alpha; larger alpha is unpremultiplied. The epsilon and policy enter normalization/receipt identity.
- Durable `DiffReceipt` contains artifact descriptors only: kind, schema, dimensions, media type, byte size, SHA-256, and stable artifact ID/path alias. Heatmap/blink bytes are returned separately as `DiffArtifactPayload` values and written through the bounded artifact service.
- Heatmap remains exact full-dimension RGBA8. Blink remains one versioned manifest plus two separately framed canonical float planes, but the payload is never JSON/base64/number-array embedded in the receipt.
- Artifact production is optional evidence. When requested, failure returns metrics plus a typed artifact-production failure object and `passed = false`; it cannot discard metrics or convert a failed comparison to success.
- Receipt/payload size limits are explicit and checked before allocation. A requested full-image artifact exceeding the evidence budget fails before allocation with the required byte estimate.
- Preserve the existing closed canonical profiles, CIEDE2000, symmetric neighborhood matching, matching-infinity policy, and fixed top-32 retention unless a new regression test proves a separate defect.

## Implementation

- Introduce schema-3 policy/metrics/receipt/artifact descriptor/payload types and fail-closed schema-2 decoding.
- Refactor pair observation to retain raw RGB/alpha errors and derive weighted policy errors once.
- Refactor metric accumulation into independently testable RGB, alpha, and combined accumulators using deterministic `f64` sums; remove the second alpha multiplier.
- Add transfer/nonfinite/unpremultiply normalization validation and typed errors before output publication.
- Split `make_artifacts` into descriptor planning, bounded payload generation, hash/size verification, and receipt projection. Integrate with the shared artifact writer rather than `serde_json` bytes.
- Update every parity caller and #496 contract to require schema 3 and to persist referenced payloads alongside the receipt.
- Add migration diagnostics identifying old schema-2 evidence as superseded rather than silently accepting/recomputing it.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Invalid gamma, nonfinite encoded input under default policy, nonfinite unpremultiplication/profile result, negative/nonfinite alpha where prohibited, invalid epsilon, dimension/artifact overflow, evidence budget excess, artifact write/hash mismatch, and schema-2 receipt use are typed failures.
- `alpha_weight = 0` cannot produce division by zero; combined denominator remains three RGB channels. Raw alpha metrics still report differences, and policy explicitly controls whether raw alpha mismatch is independently required to pass.
- Artifact write failure leaves no descriptor claiming a missing payload and removes partial files atomically.
- Normalization failure publishes no partially canonical buffer.
- Different output roots produce identical receipt identities and payload hashes using stable aliases.

## Test matrix

- Hand-computed 1×1 RGB/alpha RMSE for alpha weights `0`, `0.25`, `0.5`, `1`, `2`, and `10`, proving exactly quadratic weighted contribution rather than fourth-power contribution.
- Alpha-only pass/fail and raw/weighted metric behavior for every weight, straight and premultiplied inputs, zero/subnormal/epsilon-boundary/one alpha, hidden RGB, negative/nonfinite alpha policy.
- Linear/sRGB/valid gamma and zero/negative/NaN/infinite/overlarge gamma declarations; nonfinite input and conversion results.
- Schema-3 stable receipt bounded independently of image dimensions/artifact payload; schema-2 rejection.
- Heatmap/blink descriptor/payload size/hash/parse, over-budget planning, write failure, partial cleanup, path aliases, and two-root determinism.
- Existing neighborhood, CIEDE2000, top-32, HDR/PSNR, profile conversion, NaN/infinity, and symmetry suites remain green.
- Mutation tests reintroducing the accumulator alpha multiplier or embedding payload bytes in JSON fail.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo test -p rusttable-testkit image_diff` passes all hand-known weighting and normalization cases.
- `cargo xtask parity qualify-diff --schema 3 --weights all --artifacts external --verify-bounds` emits metrics, descriptor, payload, hash, and cleanup receipts.
- A million-pixel comparison with heatmap/blink requested has a bounded JSON receipt whose size is independent of the image planes; payload sizes are explicit and separately bounded.
- #496 rejects every schema-2 receipt and accepts schema-3 receipts only when all referenced payload hashes are present.

## Dependencies

Depends on #447 and #515. Blocks #494, #495, #496, operation/GPU parity acceptance, and any new persisted diff evidence.

## One-PR boundary

One PR changes differential normalization/weighting metrics, schema-3 receipts, external artifact payloads, callers, tests, and foundation integration. Do not change CIEDE2000, operation implementations, GPU kernels, general color management, or image codecs.
issues.title: Correct image-diff alpha weighting, normalization validation, and artifact receipt bounds

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Correct the incomplete implementation accepted under #456. Preserve the deliberate dated Rust beta baseline, but make the entire compiler/framework/dependency/advisory/source/capability contract executable from verified reality rather than a small set of self-declared TOML fields and file hashes.

## Observed blocking defects on current `main`

- `quality/ecosystem-baseline.toml` inventories only the compiler and Iced. It does not contain the required WGPU, Tokio, Rayon, redb, Wasmtime, mlua, RAW/codec, shader, serialization, or testing framework capabilities and ownership decisions.
- `ecosystem upgrade-diff` emits hashes of four current files and a generic surface list. It does not compare a proposed baseline, compiler capabilities, dependency versions/sources/features, resolved graph, platform surfaces, MSRV, advisories, or affected packages.
- `dependencies verify-policy` validates direct manifest requirements and one Iced X11 rule, but it does not inspect the lock graph for duplicate versions, package/source/checksum drift, new native `-sys`/`links` packages, transitive Git/path sources, feature expansion, advisory exceptions, or policy/lock reconciliation.
- `quality/dependency-sources.toml` declares duplicate/advisory/Git policies, but the verifier does not read or enforce that file.
- Compiler verification checks `rust-toolchain.toml`, one baseline file, and workspace `rust-version`; it does not mechanically scan workflows, scripts, Cargo config, cache keys, packaging, benchmark/coverage/doc commands, artifact identity, or generated fixtures for hidden competing toolchain literals.
- The baseline verifier trusts manually entered release/commit/LLVM fields after comparing them to the installed toolchain, but does not regenerate and compare the complete receipt or enforce target/component inventory beyond installed component names.
- Beta-only API use is not tied to a capability owner/evidence record, and there is no source-level check for unrecorded beta API adoption.
- Iced target policy checks X11 presence/absence only; it does not derive complete Linux X11/Wayland versus macOS/Windows feature ownership from the platform/DAG contract.

## Fixed decisions

- Keep production compiler exactly `beta-2026-07-17` / Rust `1.98.0-beta.4` until a dedicated baseline issue changes it. This issue must not move to stable or another beta.
- `rust-toolchain.toml` remains the selector. `quality/compiler-baseline.toml`, `quality/ecosystem-baseline.toml`, and `quality/dependency-sources.toml` become generated/reconciled policy receipts with explicit reviewed sections; they are never accepted merely because their schema parses.
- Add one typed `EcosystemBaseline` containing:
  - compiler/Cargo/LLVM/channel/commit/date/components/targets/profile/config identities;
  - every direct framework/tooling dependency version/source/features;
  - selected transitive/runtime-critical packages;
  - platform feature policy;
  - capability records with `Adopted`, `Deferred`, or `Rejected`, owning issue number, API/feature evidence, minimum/exact version, target scope, and rationale;
  - resolved lock-graph identity and advisory/source policy identity.
- Initial required ecosystem entries include Rust/Cargo/LLVM, Iced/Iced test/WGPU/Naga/winit, Tokio/tokio-util, Rayon, redb, Wasmtime/WASI/Cranelift, mlua/Lua, RAW decoder stack, image/codec crates, ICC/color crates, ONNX/ort when added, archive/HTTP/credential/input/i18n crates when added, serde/postcard/TOML, shader tooling, property/fuzz/Miri/sanitizer/coverage/benchmark tooling, and every native `-sys`/`links` package.
- A package absent from the current graph is represented `Deferred` with its owner issue rather than invented version data. Once added, exact graph facts replace the deferred record in the same dependency PR.
- Direct registry versions remain exact and centrally owned. Immutable Git `rev` sources are allowed only with explicit baseline entry and source hash; moving branch/tag references and external local paths are prohibited.
- Inspect the complete `Cargo.lock` graph. Duplicate versions are classified by package plus semver line and allowed only through an exception containing exact versions, reason, owner issue, review/expiry condition, and affected packages. Empty exception arrays do not prove compliance.
- Every package with `links`, `-sys`, build script, bundled native source, system probe, or downloaded binary is classified and explicitly owned by #168/#453/#480 or a later adapter issue. New native surface fails closed.
- Advisory policy is generated from exact pinned `cargo-audit`/`cargo-deny` reports. Exceptions require advisory ID, exact affected package/version/source, risk disposition, owner issue, review date/expiry, and removal condition. Expired or broad exceptions fail.
- Hidden compiler/toolchain/version literals are prohibited outside generated baseline data and exact reviewed fixture locations. Workflows/scripts/caches/package/artifact identities derive through one generated command/output interface.
- Platform dependency/features are derived from #453’s platform contract. Iced/winit/WGPU/X11/Wayland/macOS/Windows features are explicit per target and verified through target-filtered Cargo metadata, not a string search for X11 alone.
- Beta-only Rust standard-library/language usage is allowed, but every intentional use is marked by a stable capability ID/attribute or source registry entry linked to an `Adopted` baseline capability and regression test. Nightly feature gates/`-Z`/moving channels remain prohibited.
- Upgrade diff compares two complete baseline candidates: current accepted baseline and proposed working-tree/receipt. It produces semantic additions/removals/changes, not just hashes, and blocks unreviewed capability/source/advisory/platform changes.
- All subprocesses/receipts in the verifier use #515’s empty-environment, redacted, owned process service.

## Implementation

- Define versioned compiler/ecosystem/dependency/lock-package/feature/native/advisory/capability/exception models with canonical serialization and content hashes.
- Add `cargo xtask ecosystem refresh-baseline --candidate <path>` that derives verified toolchain, Cargo metadata, lock graph, source/checksum/features, platform contexts, and tool reports into a candidate receipt without overwriting the accepted baseline.
- Make `verify-baseline` derive current reality and compare every accepted field, including workflows/scripts/cache/package/artifact references through a generated toolchain identity scanner.
- Make `dependencies verify-policy` parse `quality/dependency-sources.toml`, full Cargo metadata/lockfile, package checksums/sources/features/build scripts/links, duplicates, native surface, and exact exception records.
- Implement advisory report ingestion/normalization and exact tool version verification. Network refresh belongs to an explicit main-only read-only command; PR verification consumes the committed normalized database/report identity and fails stale policy according to a reviewed age rule.
- Replace `upgrade-diff` with `upgrade-diff --candidate <receipt>` comparing compiler, components/targets, capabilities, direct/transitive packages, sources/checksums, features, native surface, duplicates, advisories/exceptions, MSRV/edition, platform contexts, and affected workspace packages.
- Populate the required ecosystem entries from the actual current graph and explicit deferred owner issues. Do not leave generic arrays such as `beta = []` as the capability model.
- Add source scanning/attribute validation for intentional beta-only APIs and prohibited nightly/moving-channel use.
- Replace the special Iced X11 function with target metadata/feature rules reconciled with #453.
- Integrate receipts/artifacts into #515 validation and #496; the previous #456 verification schema cannot satisfy the foundation gate.

## Failure and edge behavior

- Missing/stale/incomplete ecosystem entry, graph package, source/checksum, feature, target, native surface, advisory, exception field, owner issue, or capability evidence fails with exact identity and affected packages.
- A new transitive dependency, duplicate version, build script, `links` value, `-sys` package, Git/path source, or feature activation cannot hide behind an unchanged direct manifest.
- A newer beta’s existence is informational only. It does not fail or change the accepted baseline.
- Tool/advisory network failure cannot mutate the accepted baseline. Candidate refresh emits an incomplete failure receipt and leaves files unchanged.
- Candidate comparison is deterministic and path/credential safe. Private registry credentials/URLs are redacted while source identity remains sufficient to detect drift.
- A beta-only API removed or changed by a future beta is reported by its capability ID and owner tests; the verifier never weakens warnings/lints automatically.

## Test matrix

- Exact dated beta plus moving beta/stable/nightly/commit/component/target/Cargo/LLVM/profile/config drift.
- Hidden compiler literals in every workflow/script/cache/package/artifact/fixture class and legitimate generated exceptions.
- Direct/transitive registry/Git/path sources, checksums, renamed packages, optional/target features, build/dev/proc-macro dependencies, build scripts, `links`, bundled/downloaded native code, and `-sys` packages.
- Duplicate exact/patch/minor/major lines with valid/missing/expired/broad exceptions.
- Advisory clean, known allowed exact exception, wrong version/source, expired review, new advisory, tool/database/report drift, and offline PR verification.
- Required adopted/deferred/rejected ecosystem entries, missing/stale owner issue, version/feature/source changes, new/removed capability, and intentional/unrecorded beta API use.
- Linux X11/Wayland, macOS, and Windows target feature graphs derived from #453.
- Semantic upgrade diff for compiler-only, dependency-only, source/checksum, feature, native, advisory, MSRV, platform, and no-op candidates.

## Acceptance evidence

- `cargo xtask ecosystem verify-baseline`, `cargo xtask ecosystem dependencies verify-policy`, and semantic `upgrade-diff` pass against current reality and fail every mutation class.
- The ecosystem baseline contains the complete required current/deferred package and capability inventory with valid owners.
- Every lockfile package/source/checksum/feature/native/advisory/duplicate is reconciled to executable policy.
- No hidden toolchain literal, moving source, unowned native dependency, expired advisory exception, or unrecorded beta API can merge.
- #496 rejects the previous #456 receipt schema and accepts only the complete new receipts produced through #515.

## Dependencies

Depends on #453, #456, #492, and #515. Blocks #496 and dependency additions in #478, #508–#514, and later framework work until their candidate diffs are reviewed.

## One-PR boundary

One PR completes compiler/ecosystem/dependency/lock/native/advisory verification, semantic upgrade diff, baseline population, beta-API/source scanning, platform feature integration, tests, and foundation receipts. Do not change the approved beta or broadly upgrade dependencies except changes strictly required to reconcile the current graph.
issues.title: Complete beta ecosystem inventory, lock-graph policy, and upgrade-diff enforcement

issues.body:
Parent: #158

## Outcome

Prevent agents and maintainers from selecting an issue whose priority/dependencies are ready but whose body is ambiguous, generic, internally contradictory, or too weak to drive one complete PR. Deliver the executable semantic issue-specification contract, reviewed snapshot, deterministic remediation evidence, and ready-queue integration that the separate source-map successor #555 extends with exact darktable source ownership.

## Fixed decisions

- Readiness is composed from independent gates:
  1. priority/milestone/dependency/claimed-PR readiness from #446;
  2. semantic specification readiness delivered by this issue;
  3. canonical darktable source-anchor readiness delivered by successor #555.
  An issue failing any applicable gate is not ready. #518 does not depend on #555; #555 consumes and extends the completed #518 contract.
- The completed semantic specification schema is `rusttable.issue-spec.v2`. It requires these semantic roles:
  - outcome/user-visible or architectural result;
  - fixed decisions, including selected algorithms/frameworks/formats/defaults where the issue owns them;
  - implementation scope with concrete repository/service/crate boundaries;
  - failure and edge behavior;
  - test matrix covering positive, negative, boundary, cancellation/restart/platform/parity cases as applicable;
  - acceptance evidence with executable commands/receipts/artifacts;
  - explicit dependencies by GitHub issue number or `None`;
  - one-PR boundary and exclusions;
  - `Capabilities` metadata when the issue owns a darktable capability.
- Exact Markdown heading names are preferred but not required for existing legacy issues. A deterministic semantic parser may map older `Goal`, `Required implementation`, and `Acceptance criteria` sections to v2 roles only when all required content is present.
- A v2 issue is not sufficient merely because every heading exists. Validate content quality:
  - no empty or boilerplate-only section;
  - no unresolved `decide`, `TBD`, `choose later`, `as appropriate`, `if practical`, or comparable deferral for a decision the issue can make now;
  - algorithms/protocols/formats/frameworks/defaults are named where behavior depends on them;
  - dependencies and owner issue references exist and are not superseded without replacement;
  - acceptance evidence is executable/measurable rather than “tests pass” alone;
  - one-PR scope is coherent and does not combine unrelated capability implementations;
  - failure/cancellation/restart/platform behavior is present when the implementation has those concerns.
